```java
10  |  ID: 3147  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given:
    
    `class M { 
       static Object obj = null;
       public M(String val){ obj = val; }
    }
    
    class N{
    
       private M m = new M("hello");
    
       public static void main(String[] args){
          N n = new N();
          n = null;
          System.gc();
          System.out.println(M.obj);
       }
    }`
    
    Assuming that `System.gc()` does its job, what will be printed?

  1. `null`
  2. It will throw a `NullPointerException` at run time.

_Explanation:_ It is important to understand that garbage collection doesn't set the references that are "in scope" i.e. the references that you can access, to `null`. In fact, if you are able to access a reference, then, by definition, the object pointed to by that reference is NOT garbage! So, you will almost NEVER get a NullPointerException because of garbage collection. (There is a category of references called "soft references", which do not prevent an object from being collected and such references may become null after garbage collection. This is a very advanced topic and is not required for the OCP exam.)

  3. `hello`
  4. It will thrown an `IllegalAccessException` at run time.
  5. It will not compile.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Note that objects pointed to by static variables of a class are not collected even when all objects of that class are collected because those variables belong to the class (and not to the instance of the class). They are GCed only when the class itself is unloaded by the class loader.
    
    In the given code, `m` is an instance variable of class `N`. So, when an object of class `N` is garbage collected, the object pointed to by its instance variable is also liable to be garbage collected if there are no other references to that object. Therefore, here, the object refered to by the variable `n` and the object referred to by the variable `n.m`, will be garbage collected.
    
    However, `obj` is a static variable of class `M`. The object referred to by `obj` will not be garbage collected because the static variable remains in scope as long as the class `M` remains loaded by the classloader.
    
    

11  |  ID: 4012  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    Given:
    `class TestClass{
        static class Node{
            Node next;
            public Node(){ 
                next = null; 
            }
            public Node(Node next){
                this.next = next;
            }
            public String dump(){
                return "from dump";
            }
        }
        public static void main(String[] args) {
            Node n1 = new Node();
            Node n2 = new Node(n1);
            Node n3 = new Node();
            n1 = null;
            System.out.println(n2.dump());
        }
    }
```
    How many instances of the `Node` class are eligible for garbage collection while the program prints `from dump`?

  1. 0
  2. 1
  3. 2
  4. 3



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The program creates three `Node` instances pointed to by three local variables `n1`, `n2`, and `n3`. However, the `Node` instance pointed to by `n2` to contains a reference to the `Node` instance pointed to by `n1`. Therefore, the `Node` instance pointed to by `n1` actually has two references that are pointing to it - `n1` and `n2.next`.
    So, even if `n1` is being set to `null`, the Node instance pointed to by `n1` is still accessible through `n2.next`. Therefore, until `n2` is accessble, the `Node` instance pointed to by `n2.next` cannot be garbage collected.
    This implies that all three Node instances are reachable while the program is printing the value of `n2.dump()` and so, none of them can be GCed at this point.

## 📖 02 - Java OOA - Interfaces

47 questions

1  |  ID: 1770  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    What will the following code print when compiled and run?
```java
    interface Eatable{
        int types = 10;
    }
    class Food implements Eatable {
        public static int types = 20;
    }
    public class Fruit extends Food implements Eatable{  //LINE1
        
        public static void main(String[] args) { 
            types = 30; //LINE 2
            System.out.println(types); //LINE 3
        }
    }`

  1. Compilation failure at //LINE 1.
  2. Compilation failure at //LINE 2.
  3. Compilation failure at //LINE 3.
  4. 10
  5. 20
  6. 30
  7. Compilation failure at //LINE 2 as well as at //LINE 3

_Explanation:_ Class `Fruit` implements `Eatable` as well as extends `Food`. Since `Eatable` and `Food` both have a static int field named `types`, class `Fruit` has access to two fields by the same name. Now, if you try to access the `types` field from `Fruit` without specifying which field to you really mean to access, the compiler will complain that types is ambiguous because both are equally applicable.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 7

2  |  ID: 836  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following statements are correct?

  1. An abstract class can be extended by an abstract or a concrete class.
  2. A concrete class can be extended by an abstract or a concrete class.
  3. An interface can be extended by another interface.
  4. An interface can be extended by an abstract class.

_Explanation:_ A class "implements" an interface. It does not "extend" an interface.

  5. An interface can be extended by a concrete class.
  6. An abstract class cannot implement an interface.

_Explanation:_ Any class, whether abstract or concrete, can implement any interface.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 3

3  |  ID: 3039  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Given:
    `//Insert code here  
    
       public abstract void draw();
    }
    
    
    //Insert code here
       public void draw(){  System.out.println("in draw..."); }
    }`
    
    Which of the following lines of code can be used to complete the above code?

  1. `sealed class Shape { `and` class Circle extends Shape {`

_Explanation:_ 1\. Since there is an abstract method in the first class, the shape class must be declared abstract. 2\. A non-nested sealed class must have a permits clauses.

  2. `public class Shape { `and` class Circle extends Shape {`
  3. `abstract Shape { `and` public class Circle extends Shape {`

_Explanation:_ `class keyword is missing from the first declaration.`

  4. `public abstract class Shape { `and` class Circle extends Shape { @Override`
  5. `public abstract class Shape { `and` class Circle implements Shape {`

_Explanation:_ You can only implement an interface not a class. So Circle implements shape is wrong.

  6. `public interface Shape { `and` class Circle implements Shape { @Override`

_Explanation:_ By default all the methods of an interface are public and abstract so there is no need to explicitly specify the "abstract" keyword for the draw() method if you make Shape an interface. But it is not wrong to do so.

  7. `sealed abstract class Shape permits Circle{ `and` class Circle extends Shape {`

_Explanation:_ 1\. Since there is an abstract method in the first class, the Shape class must be declared abstract. 2\. A direct subclass of a sealed class must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The @Override annotation is an optional annotation that can be applied to an overriding method. This annotation ensures that the method really does override a super class's (or interface's) method. 
    
    If there is a mistake in the name of the subclass's method or if the parameters don't match the overridden method, then it would become an entirely new method instead of an override. In absence of this annotation, the compiler has no way of knowing whether your intention was to actually create a new method or to override the super type's method and this may cause unexpected behavior at run time, which is difficult to trace. 
    When the compiler sees this annotation, it cross checks with the super type and verifies that your method actually does override a super type's method. If the method does not correctly override any method, then the compiler raises an error. Thus, it is a good practice to apply this annotation to make your intention clear.

4  |  ID: 3038  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    Which of the following are valid declarations independent of each other inside the following interface?
    
    `public interface FooI{
    
    }`

  1. `public int FOO;`

_Explanation:_ All fields of an interface are always final and static, even if you don't specify the static or final keywords. However, they cannot be left uninitialized. They must be given a value explicitly.

  2. `public static void main(String[] args){ };`

_Explanation:_ Interfaces are allowed to have static methods with any access modifier.

  3. `private static void mbaz(){ }`

_Explanation:_ Java 9 onwards, an interface is allowed to have private (but not protected) as well as static methods. Fields are always static and public.

  4. `private int BOO = 10;`

_Explanation:_ Only methods of an interface can be private. private/protected fields are not allowed.

  5. `public default void base(){ };`
  6. ` @Override public void bar();`

_Explanation:_ The `@Override` annotation is used to convey the information that this method overrides an inherited method with the same signature. Applying this annotation prevents hard to trace logical errors caused due to spelling mistakes in the method name. It also makes the code easier to understand. However, here, the `bar()` method does not override any method and therefore, compiler will generate an error. 

  7. `sealed class X { } non-sealed class Y extends X { }`

_Explanation:_ It is ok for a nested sealed class to omit the permits clause.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3, 5, 7

5  |  ID: 3037  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following are valid declarations inside the `FooI` interface?
    
```
    interface AI{
       void moo();
    }
    interface FooI extends AI{
       //INSERT CODE HERE
    }`

  1. `private void foo(){ };`

_Explanation:_ Prior to Java 9, all interface methods had to be public. (No access control keyword in the method declaration also means public in an interface. Note that the absence of access control keyword in the method declaration in a class means package protected.) However, Java 9 onwards, an interface is allowed to have private methods. It still cannot have protected methods though.

  2. `private abstract void foo();`

_Explanation:_ A private and abstract cannot go together because a private method is never inherited and so, there is no way for any subclass/subinterface to implement it. Thus, abstract doesn't make sense for a private method.

  3. `static void foo();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body in that case.

  4. `protected void foo();`

_Explanation:_ All interface methods have to be either public or private.

  5. `public final void compute();`

_Explanation:_ final is not allowed.

  6. `final void foo(){ }`

_Explanation:_ Only default, static, and private methods of an interface can have a method body. All other methods are abstract and must not have a body. Further, if a method is abstract, it cannot be also be final at the same time.

  7. `@Override void moo();`

_Explanation:_ The `@Override` annotation is used to convey the information that this method overrides an inherited method with the same signature. Applying this annotation prevents hard to trace logical errors caused due to spelling mistakes in the method name. It also makes the code easier to understand. Here, the `moo()` method does override the `moo()` method from `AI` and therefore, is valid. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 7

6  |  ID: 3047  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Assuming that the following code compiles without any error, identify correct statements.
    
```java
    interface Processor {
       A process(String str);
    }
    
    class ItemProcessor implements Processor{
       @Override
       public B process(String str){
           return new B(str);
       }
    }
```

  1. B must be a sub type of A.

_Explanation:_ According to the rule of covariant returns, an overriding method must return either the same type or a sub type of the type returned by the overridden method. Here, return type of ItemProcessor's process method of returns B, which is not same as the type (A) returned by the overridden method. Therefore, B must be a sub type of A.

  2. A must be a sub type of B.
  3. There need not be any type - sub type relation between A and B or B and A.
  4. B must be final.

_Explanation:_ There is no restriction on B. It may or may not be final.

  5. B must not be final.

_Explanation:_ Since B must be a sub type of A, A cannot be final.

  6. A cannot be abstract.

_Explanation:_ It could be.

  7. B cannot be abstract.

_Explanation:_ Since the code contains the statement `new B(str);`, it means that B is not abstract.

7  |  ID: 3042  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    What will the following code print when compiled and run?
    
```java
    interface Pow{
        static void wow(){ 
            System.out.println("In Pow.wow");    
        }
    }
    
    abstract class Wow{
       
       static void wow(){  // LINE 9
           System.out.println("In Wow.wow");    
      }    
    }
    
    public class Powwow extends Wow implements Pow {
        public static void main(String[] args) {
            Powwow f = new Powwow();
            f.wow();
        }
    }
```

  1. `In Wow.wow`

_Explanation:_ Observe that `f` is reference variable of type `Powwow`. Since `Powwow` extends `Wow`, `Powwow` inherits the static method `wow() `from `Wow`. Java allows a static method of a class to be invoked using a reference variable and so `f.wow()` invoke's `Wow`'s `wow()`. Static methods of a interface are not inherited in the same way by an implementing class. Therefore, the static method `wow()` defined in the interface `Pow`, cannot be accessed through a reference variable. It can only be accessed using the name of the interface i.e. using `Pow.wow()`.

  2. `In Pow.wow`
  3. It will print `In Pow.wow` if `f.wow()` is replaced with` ((Pow)f).wow();`

_Explanation:_ It will not compile if you make this change because it is illegal to invoke a static method of an interface using a reference variable. If you want to invoke a static method of an interface, you need to use the name of the interface. For example, `Pow.wow();` You can use a reference variable to invoke a static method of a class or super class though. So this is valid: `((Wow)f).wow();` even though it is not a good practice to use a reference to invoke a static method.

  4. It will not compile as is.
  5. It will not compile if the line marked //LINE 9 is replaced with: `@Override static void wow(){`

_Explanation:_ Remember that static methods can never be overridden. Therefore, @Override annotation doesn't apply to static methods.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 5

8  |  ID: 3041  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which line(s) in the following code will fail compilation?
    
```java
    interface Tool{    //1
        void operate(); //2
    }
    
    abstract class PowerTool implements Tool{ } //3
    
    class PowerSaw implements PowerTool{  //4
        @Override //5
        public void operate(){ }
    }
    
    class SteamPowerSaw extends PowerSaw{ //6
        @Override //7
        public void operate(int pressure){ }
    }`

  1. //1
  2. //2
  3. //3
  4. //4

_Explanation:_ A class cannot "implement" another class. It can only "extend" another class. Thus, `PowerSaw` cannot "implement" `PowerTool` because `PowerTool` is a class. `PowerSaw` can "extend" `PowerTool`. On the other hand, an interface cannot "implement" another interface. It can "extend" another interface.

  5. //5
  6. //6
  7. //7

_Explanation:_ The `operate(int pressure)` method does not override any method from the super class or interface. Therefore, `@Override` is invalid here.

  2. `interface I2 extends I1{ void analyse(); }`

  4. `interface I3 implements I1{ void perform_work(); }`

    The `getValue(int i)` method of class B in option c, is different than the method defined in the interface because their parameters are different. Therefore, this class does not actually implement the method of the interface and that is why it needs to be declared abstract. Further, these method have "default" access whereas the interface methods are always public by default.

10  |  ID: 3040  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Given the following code:
```
    interface Device {
       public abstract void switchOn();
    }
    
    abstract class Router /* LOCATION 1 */ {
       /* LOCATION 2 */ 
       public void switchOn(){    }
    
       public abstract void reset();
    }
    
    class MimoRouter extends Router implements Device{
       /* LOCATION 3 */ 
    }
```java
    Which code, when inserted at one or more marked locations, would allow classes `Router` and `MimoRouter` to compile?
    (Consider each option as independent of other options.)

  1. `implements Device //at location 1 @Override //at location 2`

_Explanation:_ This will leave `MimoRouter` uncompilable because it will not implement the abstract method `reset()` that it inherits from Router.

  2. `public void reset(){ } //at location 3`
  3. `@Override //at location 2 public void reset() { } //at location 3`

_Explanation:_ This is valid but for the `@Override` annotation. Without the `implements Device` clause, `Router` will not inherit the abstract method `switchOn`, and in that case, the `switchOn` method provided by `Router` will not be overriding any method at all. It will be considered a new method in `Router` and the `@Override` annotation would be invalid at location 2.

  4. `@Override //at location 3 void switchOn(){ } //at location 3 @Override //at location 3 public void reset(){ }//at location 3`

_Explanation:_ This will not work because of the accessibility of the `switchOn` method. An overriding method can be made accessible but not less. The `switchOn` method is public in `Router` and. By making it default, `MiMoRouter` is trying to reduce its accessibility, which is not allowed. 

  5. `@Override //at location 2 and 3 public void switchOn(){ } //at location 2 and 3 public void reset(){ }//at location 3`
  6. `implements Device //at location 1 @Override //at location 2 public void reset(){ }//at location 3`

    An abstract method cannot be overridden.

  1. True
  2. False



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Abstract methods are meant to be overridden in the subclass. Abstract methods describe a behavior but do not implement it. 12  |  ID: 1072  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    What, if anything, is wrong with the following code?
    
```
    //Filename: TestClass.java
    class TestClass implements T1, T2{
       public void m1(){}
    }
    interface T1{
       int VALUE = 1;
       void m1();
    }
    interface T2{
       int VALUE = 2;
       void m1();
    }`
    

  1. `TestClass` cannot implement them both because it leads to ambiguity.
  2. There is nothing wrong with the code.
  3. The code will work fine only if `VALUE` is removed from one of the interfaces.
  4. The code will work fine only if `m1()` is removed from one of the interfaces.
  5. None of the above.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Inheriting ambiguous fields or methods due to implementing multipe interface does not cause any problem by itself (except in the case of default methods) but referring to such fields/methods in an ambiguous way will cause a compile time error. So you cannot call : `System.out.println(VALUE);` because it will be ambiguous (there are two `VALUE` definitions). But the following lines are valid :
    
    `TestClass tc = new TestClass();
    System.out.println(( ( T1) tc).VALUE);`
    
    However, explicit cast is not required for calling the method m1() :` ( ( T2) tc).m1();`
    `tc.m1()` is also fine because even though `m1()` is declared in both the interfaces, the definition to both resolves unambiguously to only one `m1()`, which is defined in TestClass.

```java
14  |  ID: 3242  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of these statements concerning interfaces are true?
    

  1. An interface may extend an interface.

_Explanation:_ Unlike a class, an interface can extend from multiple interfaces.

  2. An interface may extend a class and may implement an interface.

_Explanation:_ An interface cannot implement another interface. It can extend another interface but not a class.

  3. A class can implement an interface and extend a class.
  4. A class can extend an interface and can implement a class.
  5. An interface can only be implemented and cannot be extended.

_Explanation:_ It can be extended by another interface.

  6. All methods of an interface are public and abstract.

_Explanation:_ Java 8 onwards, an interface is allowed to have default and static methods, which are non-abstract methods. Java 9 onwards, an interface is also allowed to have private methods.

  7. An interface may extend a sealed interface.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 3, 7

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The keyword implements is used when a class inherits method prototypes from an interface. The keyword extends is used when an interface inherits from another interface, or a class inherits from another class.
```

16  |  ID: 1481  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Given:
```java
    interface Account{
      public default String getId(){
         return "0000";
      }
    }
    
    interface PremiumAccount extends Account{
       //INSERT CODE HERE
    }
```
    Which of the following options can be inserted in PremiumAccount independent of each other?

  1. `static String getId(){ return "1111"; }`

_Explanation:_ Trying to override a static method with a non-static method (and vice-versa) in a class will result in a compilation error. Even in case of interfaces, a subinterface cannot override a default method with a static method. You can, however, have a default method in a subinterface with the same signature as a static method of its super interface because a static method of an interface can only be called using that interface's name. Example: `interface I{ public default void invalid(){ } public static void valid(){ } //Can be called only using I.valid(); } interface I2 extends I{ public static void invalid(){ } //WILL NOT COMPILE public default void valid(){ } //this is ok. }`

  2. `String getId();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can redeclare a default method and also make it abstract. Also note that methods of an interface are public by default (i.e. they are public when no access modifier is explicitly specified). This is unlike a class, where absence of an access modifier means "package private". So, in this option, since the method has not specified any access modifier, it will be deemed to be public and therefore it satisfies the rule that an overriding method cannot be made more restrictive than the overridden method.

  3. `default String getId(){ return "1111"; }`

_Explanation:_ An interface can redeclare a default method and provide a different implementation.

  4. `abstract static String getName();`

_Explanation:_ 1\. static methods can never be abstract (neither in an interface nor in a class). 2\. An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body.

  5. `static String getName();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body.

  6. `default String getName();`

_Explanation:_ A default method must have a body.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3

17  |  ID: 3627  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following are valid declarations inside an interface independent of each other?

  1. `void compute();`

_Explanation:_ All interface methods have to be either public or private. No access control keyword in the method declaration also means public in an interface. (Note that the absence of access control keyword in the method declaration in a class means package protected.)

  2. `public void compute();`
  3. `public final void compute();`

_Explanation:_ final is not allowed.

  4. `static void compute();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body in that case.

  5. `protected void compute();`

_Explanation:_ All interface methods have to be either public or private.

  6. sealed class MyClass { } (Assume that there is no other member in the interface.)

_Explanation:_ 1\. An inteface may have a nested sealed class, 2\. The permits clause is optional for nested sealed classes/interface. 3\. Even though the permits clause is optional for a nested sealed classes/interface, existence of a direct subclass of that sealed class/interface is required. Here, no such subclass of MyClass exists and so this definition will not compile. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2

18  |  ID: 1478  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which statements about the following code contained in BankAccount.java are correct?
```java
    interface Account{
      public default String getId(){
         return "0000";
      }
    }
    
    interface PremiumAccount extends Account{
      public String getId();
    }
    
    public class BankAccount implements PremiumAccount{
      public static void main(String[] args) {
          Account acct = new BankAccount();
          System.out.println(acct.getId());
      }
    
    }
```

  1. It will print `0000` when run.
  2. It will compile if class `BankAccount` provides an implementation for `getId` method.

_Explanation:_ Since interface `PremiumAccount` redeclares `getId` method as abstract, the `BankAccount` class must either provide an implementation for this method or be marked as abstract. In this case, making the class abstract will not help because of the statement - `Account acct = new BankAccount();`

  3. It will not compile unless interface `PremiumAccount` is marked abstract.

_Explanation:_ Interfaces are always abstract. You can but you don't have to mark them abstract. Methods of an interface that are not marked default, static, or private, are also always abstract. You don't have to mark them as abstract.

  4. It will compile if `getId` method in `PremiumAccount` is replaced with: `public String getId(){ super.getId(); }`

_Explanation:_ 1\. You cannot provide a method body in an interface method unless you mark it as default (or static). 2\. You cannot use super keyword in an interface's method to invoke a method defined in its super interface.

  5. It will compile if `getId` method in `PremiumAccount` is replaced with: `public default String getId(){ super.getId(); }`

_Explanation:_ `super.methodName` is a valid way to invoke a super class's method from anywhere within a subclass's method. But it works only for classes. To invoke an interface's default method, you need to use the name of that interface as well. Like this: `Account.super.getId();` A class (or an interface) can invoke a default method of an interface that is explicitly mentioned in the class's implements clause (or the interface's extends clause) by using the same syntax i.e. <InterfaceName>.super.<methodName>. However, this technique cannot be used to invoke a default method provided by an interface that is not directly implemented (or extended) by the caller. Here is an example: ` interface A { default void hello() { } } interface B extends A { default void hello() { super.hello(); //This is NOT valid. A.super.hello(); //This is valid. } } public class TestClass implements B { public void hello() { super.hello();//This is NOT valid. A.super.hello(); //This is NOT valid because TestClass does not implement A directly. B.super.hello(); //This is valid. } } `

  6. It will compile if `getId` method in `PremiumAccount` is replaced with: `public default String getId(){ super(); }`

_Explanation:_ `super();` is used to invoke the super class's constructor. So, if present, it can only be the first statement of a constructor. Calling `super();` does not invoke the super class's method.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

19  |  ID: 1479  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of these statements about interfaces are true?

  1. Interfaces are always abstract.
  2. An interface can have static methods.

_Explanation:_ Java 8 allows interfaces to have static methods as well as default methods. Java 9 allows interfaces to have private methods also.

  3. All methods in an interface are abstract although you need not declare them to be so.

_Explanation:_ An interface may also have default methods, static methods, and private methods. None of these methods are abstract. 

  4. Fields of an interface may be declared as transient or volatile but not synchronized.

_Explanation:_ All fields of an interface are public, static, and final. Therefore, volatile, transient, and synchronized do not make sense for such fields.

  5. Interfaces cannot be final.
  6. Interfaces allow multiple implementation inheritance through default methods.

_Explanation:_ Multiple inheritance of implementation is the ability to inherit method definitions from multiple classes. Java does not allow a class to extend from multiple classes but does allow a class to implement multiple interfaces. Prior to Java 8, interfaces were not allowed to contain any method implementation but with Java 8, interfaces can have method implementations in the form of "default" methods. This effectively allows a class inherit implementations from multiple sources. In other words, default methods introduce one form of multiple inheritance of implementation. However, you still cannot have a class that implements two unrelated interfaces where both the interfaces contain a default method with the same signature unless the class provides an implementation for that method itself. For example, in the following code, class C will not compile: `interface I1{ public default void m1(){ System.out.println("in I1.m1"); } } interface I2{ public default void m1(){ System.out.println("in I2.m1"); } } class CI implements I1, I2{ //This class will not compile. } //This class will compile because it provides its own implementation of m1. class C2 implements I1, I2{ public void m1(){ System.out.println("in C2.m1"); } } ` You can have a class inherit a method with the same signature from an interface and a superclass though. This is allowed because the superclass's version always overrides the interface's version. The class doesn't get two implementations. It gets only the version from super class.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 5, 6

20  |  ID: 3651  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which statement(s) about the following code are correct?
```java
    interface House{
      public default void lockTheGates(){
         System.out.println("Locking House");
      }
    }
    
    interface Office {
      public void lockTheGates();
    }
    
    class HomeOffice implements House, Office{ //1
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
    
      public static void main(String[] args) {
        Office off = new HomeOffice();  //2
        off.lockTheGates(); //3
        House home = (House) off; //4
        home.lockTheGates(); //5
      }
    }
```

  1. Code for class `HomeOffice` will cause compilation to fail at //1.

_Explanation:_ If this code is permitted, the `HomeOffice` class would inherit two versions of `lockTheGates` methods - one from the Office interface (which is default), and one from the `House` interface (which is abstract). But Java does not permit a class to inherit multiple versions of the same method from multiple unrelated interfaces (even if the method is abstract in one of the interfaces). Java forces the class to provide its own implementation for that method so that there is no ambiguity about which implementation of the method should be invoked at run time.

  2. Lines at //3 and //5 will cause compilation to fail.
  3. Line at //4 will cause compilation to fail.
  4. There will no compilation error. The code will print `Locking House` twice.

_Explanation:_ The only way class `HomeOffice` can implement both the interfaces is when provides its own implementation of the `lockTheDoors` method.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

21  |  ID: 3650  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which statements about the following code are correct?
```java
    interface House{
      public default void lockTheGates(){
         System.out.println("Locking House");
      }
    }
    
    interface Office {
      public void lockTheGates();
    }
    
    class HomeOffice implements House, Office{ //1
      public void lockTheGates(){
         System.out.println("Locking HomeOffice");
      }
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
    
      public static void main(String[] args) {
         Office off = new HomeOffice();  //2
         off.lockTheGates(); 
      }
    }
```

  1. Code for class `HomeOffice` will cause compilation to fail.
  2. Line at //1 will cause compilation to fail.
  3. Line at //2 will cause compilation to fail.
  4. The code will compile successfully if the `lockTheGates` method is removed from class HomeOffice.

_Explanation:_ If you remove the `lockTheGates` method from `HomeOffice`, the class will fail to compile because the class will inherit an abstract `lockTheGates` method from the Office interface but the `HomeOffice` class is not abstract. Even if you make `HomeOffice` abstract, it will still fail to compile because it will inherit two incompatible methods with the same name - one from `Home`, which is default and one from `Office`, which is abstract. To prevent this situation, Java prohibits a class from inheriting multiple implementations of the same method from different unrelated interfaces. However, if a class provides its own implementation of the same method, the ambiguity is removed because the compiler can unambiguously invoke the implementation provided by the class itself. Thus, the only way to resolve this incompatibility is to provide an implementation of `lockTheGates` in `HomeOffice`. 

  5. It will compile fine and print `Locking HomeOffice` when run.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

```java
Consider the following code: 
    
    
    
    class Super{

    }
    class Test{
      public static void main(String[] args){
         One o = null;
         Two t = new Two();
      }
    }
    

What will be the output when class Test is run ?

  1. It will print `one super two`

_Explanation:_ "`one`" will not be printed as class One is not actively used.

  2. It will print `one two super`
  3. It will print `super two`

_Explanation:_ `Super` will be instantiated before `Two`.

  4. It will print `two super`
  5. None of the above



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    As per JLS 12.4.1 - A class or interface type T will be initialized immediately before the first occurrence of any one of the following: 
    
    T is a class and an instance of T is created. 
    T is a class and a static method declared by T is invoked. 
    A static field declared by T is assigned. 
    A static field declared by T is used and the field is not a constant variable. 
    T is a top-level class, and an assert statement lexically nested within T is executed. 
    
    The statement `One o = null;` does not fall in either of the cases mentioned above. So, class One is not initialized and its static block is not executed. Class Two is initialized only after its superclass Super has been initialized.

25  |  ID: 903  |  Difficulty: Tough

A java source file contains the following code:  

    
    
    interface I { 
      int getI(int a, int b); 
    }
    
    interface J{
        int getJ(int a, int b, int c);
    }
    
    abstract class MyIJ implements J , I { }
    
    class MyI{ 
        int getI(int x, int y){ return x+y; }
    }
    
    interface K extends J{
        int getJ(int a, int b, int c, int d);
    }
    

Identify the correct statements: 

  1. It will fail to compile because of `MyIJ`

_Explanation:_ `MyIJ` declares that it implements interfaces I and J, but does not implement the methods declared in these interfaces. However, since `MyIJ` has been declared as `abstract`, it is valid.

  2. It will fail to compile because of `MyIJ` and `K`
  3. It will fail to compile because of `K`

_Explanation:_ K is a valid interface because an interface is permitted to extend another interface.

  4. It will fail to compile because of `MyI` and `K`

_Explanation:_ Both are valid.

  5. It will fail to compile because of `MyIJ, K, `and `MyI`
  6. It will compile without any error.

26  |  ID: 1821  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which statements about the following code are correct?
```
    interface House{
      public default String getAddress(){
         return "101 Main Str";
      }
    }
    
    interface Office {
      public default String getAddress(){
         return "101 Smart Str";
      }
    }
    
    class HomeOffice implements House, Office{
      public String getAddress(){
         return "R No 1, Home";
      }
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
    
      public static void main(String[] args) {
        House h = new HomeOffice();  //1
        System.out.println(h.getAddress()); //2
      }
    }
```java

  1. Code for class `HomeOffice` will cause compilation to fail.
  2. Line at //1 will cause compilation to fail.
  3. Line at //2 will cause compilation to fail.
  4. The code will compile successfully if the `getAddress` method is removed from class `HomeOffice`.

_Explanation:_ If you remove the `getAddress` method from `HomeOffice`, `HomeOffice` will fail to compile because it will now have two different implementations of the same method and the compiler will not be able to figure out which method to invoke. To prevent this situation, Java prohibits a class from inheriting multiple implementations of the same method from different unrelated interfaces. However, if a class provides its own implementation of the same method, the ambiguity is removed because the compiler can unambiguously invoke the implementation provided by the class itself.

  5. It will compile fine and print `R No 1, Home` when run.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

27  |  ID: 3145  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Given the following code:
```
    abstract class Device{
        String name;
        String number;
        String address;
        int frequency;
        
        abstract void setName(String name);
        abstract void connect();
        abstract void disconnect();
        abstract void call(String number);
        abstract void listenOn(int frequency);
        
    }
    
    interface Caller{     }
    
    interface Receiver{    }
    
    class Phone extends Device implements Caller{ }
    class TV extends Device implements Receiver{ }
    class WalkieTalkie extends Device implements Caller, Receiver{ }
```java
    and the following requirements specification regarding the `call` and `listenOn` functionality:
    
    1. `Phone` supports only the `call` operation. Users should have the ability to call a number, but not set the frequency. Assume frequency is fixed at 0. 
    
    2. `TV` supports only the `listenOn` operation. Users should have the ability to set the frequency using the `listenOn` method, but not change the number. Assume number is fixed at "0". 
    
    3. `WalkieTalkie` contains both `call` and `listenOn` features. Users should have the ability to call a number using `call` as well as set frequency using `listenOn`. 
    
    The methods setName, connect, and disconnect are applicable to Phone, TV, and WalkieTalkie.
    
    Which two changes together would allow you to achieve these specifications? 
    

  1. make `Device` an interface rather than an abstract class 
  2. move `String number` from `Device` to `Caller`
  3. move `int frequency` from `Device` to `Receiver` 
  4. make `Device`, rather than `Phone, TV, `and` WalkieTalkie` implement `Caller` and `Receiver`.
  5. move `call` from `Device` to `Caller`
  6. move `listenOn` from `Device` to `Receiver`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The operations that are specific to a particular behavior should be defined in the interface that is meant to describe that behavior. Therefore, in this case, the `call` method should be in the `Caller` interface and the `listenOn` method should be in the `Receiver` interface.
    
    After this change, the `Phone, TV,` and `WalkieTalki` classes must provide an implementation only for the methods that they support. i.e. `Phone` will be required to implement only the `call` method and `TV` will be required to implement only the `listenOn` method, besides the other abstract methods of the `Device` class.
    
    Ideally, `int frequency` and `String number` should not be there in `Device` but since that is not in the options, you may ignore this part.

28  |  ID: 4014  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code:
    `interface Bar{
        void bar();
        default void printBar(){ 
            print("printing"); 
        }
        private void showBar(){ 
            print("showing"); 
        }
        public static void print(String x){
            System.out.println(x+" bar");
        };
    }
    
    class FooBar implements Bar{
      public void bar(){
         System.out.println("In foobar");  
      }    
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
       public static void main(String[] args){
          //INSERT CODE HERE
       }
    }
```
    What can be inserted in the above code?

  1. `FooBar.printBar();` 

_Explanation:_ Since `printBar// is an instance method, it cannot be invoked without a reference to a `Bar` object. 

  2. `new FooBar().showBar();` 

_Explanation:_ Since `showBar` is a private method of the `Bar` interface, it is not visible outside `Bar`. It can be invoked only from within `Bar`.

  3. `FooBar.print("test");`

_Explanation:_ A static method of an interface can only be invoked using the name of the interface in which it is defined. So, the `print` method of `Bar` can only be invoked using `Bar.print`.

  4. `FooBar.bar();` 

_Explanation:_ Since `bar` is an instance method of the `FooBar` class and is declared in the `Bar` interface, a reference to an instance of `FooBar` or any class that implements `Bar` is necessary to invoke it.

  5. `new FooBar().printBar();` 
  6. `Bar.print("test");`
  7. `new Bar().print("test");`

_Explanation:_ Since `Bar` is an interface, it cannot be instantiated. So, `new Bar()` is invalid.

  8. `new Bar(){}.printBar();` 

_Explanation:_ `new Bar(){ }` could have been a valid instantation of an anonymous class that implements `Bar` and it is valid to invoke `printBar()` on this instance. However, this anonymous class is also required to implement the abstract method `bar()` declared in the `Bar` interface. So, `new Bar(){ }` is invalid. You could do: `new Bar(){ public void bar(){ } //provide an implementation for the abstract method }.printBar();`




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5, 6

29  |  ID: 4013  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Given:
```java
    interface Account{
        int LIMIT = 100;
        public default boolean canWithdraw(int amount){
            return false;
        }
        public static int getBalanceAmount(int amount){
            return LIMIT - amount;
        }
        int makeBid();
        String toString();
    }
    
    public class VirtualAccount implements Account{
    
        //INSERT CODE HERE 
    
        int amount;
        public VirtualAccount(int amount){ 
            this.amount = amount; 
        }
    
        public static void main(String[] args) {
            try{
                VirtualAccount va = new VirtualAccount(10);
                int balance = va.makeBid();
                System.out.println("Account Balance : "+va.amount+" Balance: "+balance);
            }catch(Exception e){
                System.out.println(e);
            }
        }
    }
```
    
    Which of the following options when inserted in the above code will make the `VirtualAccount` class correctly implement the `Account` interface?
    
    Note: You may find a few very long questions in the real exam. Eliminating obviously wrong options quickly will help you save time while attempting such questions.

  1. `@Override public int makeBid() throws Exception{ if(canWithdraw(amount)){ return Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); }else{ throw new RuntimeException("Not enough balance"); } } @Override public String toString(){ return "Acct balance "+this.amount+" margin: "+Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); } `

_Explanation:_ An implementing method is allowed to throw only those checked exceptions (or their subclasses) that are listed in the `throws` clause of the overridden method. Since the `makeBid()` method declared in the `Account` interface does not have any `throws` clause, the implementing class cannot declare any checked exception in its `throws` clause. That makes this option obviously wrong. There is no need to waste time in reading the whole code. 

  2. `@Override public int makeBid() throws RuntimeException{ if(canWithdraw(amount)){ return Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); }else{ throw new RuntimeException("Not enough balance"); } }`

_Explanation:_ 1\. An overriding method is allowed to declare unchecked exceptions in its throws clause without worrying about the throws clause of the overridden method. 2\. Since the `Object` class implements the `toString` method, all classes automatically inherit this method. Therefore, it is ok even if the `VirtualAccount` class does not override the `toString` method. In fact, declaring methods that are defined in the `Object` class (such as `equals`, `hashCode`, and `toString`) in an interface is redundant.

  3. `@Override public int makeBid() throws RuntimeException{ if(canWithdraw(amount)){ return Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); }else{ throw new Exception("Not enough balance"); } } @Override public String toString(){ return "Acct balance "+this.amount+" margin: "+Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); } `

_Explanation:_ If a method throws a checked exception (such as `Exception`) from its body, that exception (or its superclass) must be declared in its `throws` clause. Thus, this option is obviously wrong.

  4. `@Override public int makeBid() { if(canWithdraw(amount)){ return Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); }else{ throw new RuntimeException("Not enough balance"); } } @Override public String toString(){ return "Acct balance "+this.amount+" margin: "+Account.getBalanceAmount(amount); } `

_Explanation:_ `RuntimeException` is an unchecked exception. It is not necessary for it to be declared in the throws clause. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4

31  |  ID: 1168  |  Difficulty: Level 5
    
    
    Consider these two interfaces:
```java
    interface I1
    {
       void m1() throws java.io.IOException;
    }
    interface I2
    {
       void m1() throws java.sql.SQLException;
    }`
    
    What methods have to be implemented by a class that says it implements `I1` and `I2` ?
    (Assume that `IOException `and` SQLException` do not have a parent-child relationship.)

  1. Both, `public void m1() throws SQLException` and `public void m1() throws IOException`
  2. `public void m1() throws Exception`
  3. The class cannot implement both the interfaces simultaneously because they have conflicting methods.
  4. `public void m1() throws SQLException, IOException`

_Explanation:_ A class that implements a method of an interface (or that overrides a method of a super class), cannot include any new exceptions in its throws class. It is only allowed throw either the same exceptions or any subclass of those exceptions as declared by the interface method. It is also allowed to not throw any exception at all. Therefore, if you declare `SQLException` as well as `IOException` in your throws clause, neither of the interface methods will be satisfied.

  5. None of the above.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  
When interfaces are involved, more than one method declaration may be implemented by a single method declaration. In this case, the overriding declaration must have a throws clause that is compatible with ALL the overridden declarations. The declaration public void m1(){} satisfies both the declarations. Example: 
    
    
    import java.io.*;
    import java.sql.*;
    interface I1{   void m1() throws IOException;}
    interface I2{   void m1() throws SQLException;}
    public class TestClass implements I1, I2
    {
       public static void main(String args[])  throws Exception
       {
         TestClass tc = new TestClass();
         I1 i1 = (I1) tc; //This is valid.
         i1.m1();
    
         I2 i2 = (I2) tc; //This is valid too.
         i2.m1();
    
       }
       public void m1()
       {
         System.out.println("Hi there");
       }
    }

    Consider the following class and interface definitions (in separate files):
    
    `public class Sample implements IInt{
       public static void main(String[] args){
          Sample s = new Sample();  //1
          int j = s.thevalue;       //2
          int k = IInt.thevalue;    //3
          int l = thevalue;         //4
       }
    }
    public interface IInt{
          int thevalue = 0;
    }
```
    
    What will happen when the above code is compiled and run?

  1. It will give an error at compile time at line //1.
  2. It will give an error at compile time at line //2.
  3. It will give an error at compile time at line //3
  4. It will give an error at compile time at line //4.
  5. It will compile and run without any problem.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    As a rule, fields defined in an interface are public, static, and final. The methods are public. 
    Here, the interface `IInt` defines `thevalue` and thus any class that implements this interface gets this field. Therefore, it can be accessed using `s.thevalue` or just `thevalue` inside the class. Also, since it is static, it can also be accessed using `IInt.thevalue` or `Sample.thevalue`.

33  |  ID: 2013  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Given the following code:
    `interface Movable{ 
       int offset = 100; 
       public void move(int dx);
    }
    interface Growable{
      public void grow(int dy);
    }
    
    class Animal implements Movable, Growable{
       public void move(int dx){ }
       public void grow(int dy){ }
    }`
    

  1. `Animal` class illustrates Java's support for multiple inheritance of type.
  2. `Animal` class illustrates Java's support for multiple inheritance of state.
  3. `Animal` class illustrates Java's support for multiple inheritance of type as well as state.
  4. `Animal` class illustrates Java's support for multiple implementation inheritance.

_Explanation:_ Java 8 allows an interface to contain implementations (in the form of default methods and as static methods). However, Java 8 still does not allow a class to inherit multiple implementations of the same method from two different unrelated interfaces. For example, the following will not compile: ` interface Movable{ public default void move(int dx) { System.out.println("in m");} } interface Growable{ public default void move(int dx) { System.out.println("in g");} } class Animal implements Movable, Growable{ } ` Compiler will complain that `Animal` inherits unrelated defaults for move(int) from types `Movable` and `Growable`. To make it compile, you must provide an implementation of the `move` method explicitly in `Animal` class.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Interfaces, classes, and enums are all "types". Java allows a class to implement multiple interfaces. In this way, Java supports multiple inheritance of types. 
    "State", on the other hand, is represented by instance fields. Only a class can have instance fields and therefore, only a class can have a state. (Fields defined in an interface are always implicitly static, even if you don't specify the keyword static explicitly. Therefore, an interface does not have any state.) Since a class is allowed to extend only from one class at the most, it can inherit only one state. Thus, Java does not support multiple inheritance of state.
    
    This is an important concept and is explained in more detail here: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/IandI/multipleinheritance.html

34  |  ID: 2014  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Identify correct option(s):
    Note: The options are not about Java but about OOP in general.

  1. Multiple inheritance of state includes ability to inherit instance methods from multiple classes.

_Explanation:_ Methods do not have state. Ability to inherit instance methods from multiple classes is called multiple inheritance of implementation. Default methods introduce one form of multiple inheritance of implementation. A class can implement more than one interface, which can contain default methods that have the same name. However, such a class cannot be compiled. In this case, the implementing class is required to provide its own implementation of the common method to avoid ambiguity.

  2. Multiple inheritance of state includes ability to inherit instance fields from multiple classes.
  3. Multiple inheritance of type includes ability to inherit instance fields as well as instance methods from multiple classes.
  4. Multiple inheritance of type includes ability to implement multiple interfaces and ability to inherit static or instance fields from interfaces and/or classes.
  5. Multiple inheritance of type includes ability to implement multiple interfaces and/or ability to extend from multiple classes.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Interfaces, classes, and enums are all "types". Java allows a class to implement multiple interfaces. In this way, Java supports multiple inheritance of types. 
    "State", on the other hand, is represented by instance fields. Only a class can have instance fields and therefore, only a class can have a state. (Fields defined in an interface are always implicitly static, even if you don't specify the keyword static explicitly. Therefore, an interface does not have any state.) Since a class is allowed to extend only from one class at the most, it can inherit only one state. Thus, Java does not support multiple inheritance of state.
    
    This is an important concept and is explained in more detail here: https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/java/IandI/multipleinheritance.html

36  |  ID: 3361  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following statements are correct regarding abstract classes and interfaces?

  1. An abstract class can have private as well as static methods while an interface can not have static methods.

_Explanation:_ Interfaces can have static methods (public as well as private). Interfaces cannot have protected methods. It cannot have non-public fields and instance fields.

  2. An abstract class cannot implement multiple interfaces while an interface can extend multiple interfaces.

_Explanation:_ An abstract class (or any class for that matter) can implement any number of interfaces.

  3. Abstract classes can have abstract methods but interface cannot.

_Explanation:_ Both abstract classes and interfaces can have abstract as well as non-abstract methods. The difference is that by default (i.e. when no modifier is specified), the methods of an abstract class have "default" access and are non-abstract (i.e. they must have a body), while the methods of an interface are public and abstract. 

  4. Abstract classes can have instance fields but interfaces can't.

_Explanation:_ Fields of an interface are always public, static, and final. Fields of an abstract class can be static or instance and may have any accessibility (i.e. public, private, protected, or default).

  5. An abstract class can have final methods but an interface cannot.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 4, 5

```java
            System.out.println("getting length");
            return 10;
        };

            return 20;
```

    Several similar default methods need to be added to this interface and each method is supposed to log a message at the start of the method. As of now, the logging is done using just a println statement but it may change later.
    
    What changes can be done in the above interface to make it more maintainable without exposing any non-business functionality to the users of this interface?

  1. Add a `public void log(String msg)` method to write the log message and invoke it from other methods instead of using println statement.

_Explanation:_ Although it would work but it would expose the log method also. We do not want that as it has nothing to do with the business functionality of the interface.

  2. Add a `protected void log(String msg)` method to write the log message and invoke it from other methods instead of using println statement.

_Explanation:_ Interfaces still do not allow protected methods.

  3. Add a `private void log(String msg)` method to write the log message and invoke it from other methods instead of using println statement.
  4. Add a `static void log(String msg)` method to write the log message and invoke it from other methods instead of using println statement.

    Consider the following interface definition:
    `interface Bozo{
             int type = 0;
             public void jump();
    }
    
```java
    Now consider the following class:
```
    public class Type1Bozo implements Bozo{
             public Type1Bozo(){
                type = 1;
             }
    
             public void jump(){
                System.out.println("jumping..."+type);
             }
    
             public static void main(String[] args){
                Bozo b = new Type1Bozo();

             }

```java
  2. jumping...1
  3. This program will not compile.
  4. It will throw an exception at runtime.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Fields defined in an interface are ALWAYS considered as public, static, and final, even if you don't explicitly define them as such. In fact, you cannot even declare a field to be private or protected in an interface. Therefore, you cannot assign any value to 'type' outside the interface definition.

39  |  ID: 3566  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    Which of the following are valid declarations inside an interface independent of each other?

  1. `default void compute();`

_Explanation:_ When you mark a method in an interface as default, you are basically providing a default implementation of that method so that any class that implements this interface doesn't necessarily have to provide its own implementation. Thus, a default method without a method body doesn't make sense. `default void compute(){ }` would be valid. Remember that default keyword can occur for a method only in an interface and NOT in an abstract class.

  2. `public void compute();`
  3. `static void compute(){ System.out.println("computing..."); }`

_Explanation:_ This is a valid static method in an interface. Note that all members of an interface (i.e. fields as well as methods) are public by default. Java 9 allows interface methods to be marked private.

  4. `static void compute();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body in that case because a static method cannot be abstract.

  5. `default static void compute(){ System.out.println("computing..."); };`

_Explanation:_ An interface method cannot be default and static at the same time because a default method is always an instance method.

  6. `interface I { sealed class Inner { } final class IC extends Inner{ } }`

_Explanation:_ Normally, a sealed class/interface needs to have a permits clause. However, if the sealed class/interface is a nested class/interface, the permits clause is optional as a matter of convenience. Therefore, there is no issue with the declaration of classes `Inner` and `IC` in the above code. Note that only the permits clause is optional. The presence of a direct subclass of the seal class is still necessary. So, if you remove the definition of class `IC` from the above code, class `Inner` will cause a compilation error because there will be no direct subclasses of `Inner`. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3, 6

40  |  ID: 3565  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which statements about the following code are correct?
```
    interface House{
      public default String getAddress(){
         return "101 Main Str";
      }
    }
    
    interface Bungalow extends House{
      public default String getAddress(){
         return "101 Smart Str";
      }
    }
    
    class MyHouse implements Bungalow, House{
    
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
    
      public static void main(String[] args) {
        House ci = new MyHouse();  //1
        System.out.println(ci.getAddress()); //2
      }
    }
```java

  1. Code for interface `House` will cause compilation to fail.
  2. Code for interface `Bungalow` will cause compilation to fail.
  3. Code for class `MyHouse` will cause compilation to fail.
  4. Line at //1 will cause compilation to fail.
  5. Line at //2 will cause compilation to fail.
  6. The code will compile successfully.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    There is no problem with the code. It is perfectly valid for a subinterface to override a default method of the base interface. A class that implements an interface can also override a default method.
    It is valid for `MyHouse` to say that it implements `Bungalow` as well as `House` even though `House` is redundant because `Bungalow` is a `House` anyway. `MyHouse` will get only one version of the `getAddress` method and that will be from `Bungalow`.
    
    It will actually print `101 Smart str`.
    
    Note that if you remove `extends House` from `Bungalow`, `MyHouse` will fail to compile because in that case, `MyHouse` will be inheriting a version of the `getAddress` method from each of the two interfaces and that would be unacceptable.

    What will the following code print when compiled and run?
```
    interface Boiler{
        public void boil();
        private static void log(String msg){ //1
           System.out.println(msg);
        }
        public static void shutdown(){
            log("shutting down");
        }
    }
    interface Vaporizer extends Boiler{  
        public default void vaporize(){
            boil();
            System.out.println("Vaporized!");
        }
    }
        
    public class Reactor implements Vaporizer{
        public void boil() {
            System.out.println("Boiling...");
        }
    
        public static void main(String[] args) {
            Vaporizer v =  new Reactor(); //2
            v.vaporize(); //3
            v.shutdown(); //4
        }
    }
```java

  1. `Boiling... Vaporized! shutting down`
  2. Compilation failure at //1.

_Explanation:_ Since Java 9, an interface is allowed to have private (but not protected) static as well as instance methods. Fields of an interface are still always implicitly public, static, and final.

  3. Compilation failure at //2.
  4. Compilation failure at //4.
  5. If code at //4 is changed to `Vaporizer.shutdown();`, it will print `Boiling... Vaporized! shutting down`
  6. Definition of interface Vaporizer will cause compilation to fail.

_Explanation:_ Definition of interface Vaporizer is fine.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Remember that static method of an interface can only be accessed by using the name of that interface. i.e. `Boiler.shutdown()` in this case. This is unlike a static method of a class, which can be accessed using a subclass name or a variable name as well.

42  |  ID: 1328  |  Difficulty: Medium

Consider the following classes : 
    
    
    interface I{
    }
    
    class A implements I{
    }
    
    class B extends A {
    }
    
    class C extends B{
    }

And the following declarations: 
    
    
    A a = new A();
    B b = new B(); 

Identify options that will compile and run without error.

  1. `a = (B)(I)b;`

_Explanation:_ class B does implement I because it extends A, which implements I. A reference of type I can be cast to any class at compile time (except if the class is final and does not implement I). Since B is-a A, it can be assigned to a.

  2. `b = (B)(I) a;`

_Explanation:_ This will fail at run time because a does not point to an object of class B.

  3. `a = (I) b;`

_Explanation:_ An I is not an A. Therefore, it will not compile.

  4. `I i = (C) a;`

_Explanation:_ It will compile because a C is-a A, which is-a I, and a reference of class A can point to an object of class C. But it will fail at runtime because a does not point to an object of class C.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

43  |  ID: 3512  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which statements about the following code are correct?
```
    interface House{
      public default String getAddress(){
         return "101 Main Str";
      }
    }
    
    interface Office {
      public static String getAddress(){
         return "101 Smart Str";
      }
    }
    
    interface WFH extends House, Office{
       private boolean isOffice(){ return true; }
    }
    
    class HomeOffice implements House, Office{ 
      public String getAddress(){
         return "R No 1, Home";
      }
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
    
      public static void main(String[] args) {
        Office off = new HomeOffice();  //1
        System.out.println(off.getAddress()); //2
      }
    }
```java

  1. Code for class `HomeOffice` will cause compilation to fail.
  2. Code for interface `WFH` will cause compilation to fail.

_Explanation:_ Since Java 9, an interface is allowed to have private (but not protected) static as well as instance methods.

  3. It will compile fine and print `R No 1, Home` when run.
  4. Line at //1 will cause compilation to fail.
  5. Line at //2 will cause compilation to fail.

_Explanation:_ Since the declared type of variable `off` is `Office`, the compiler will check the call to `getAddress` against the `Office` interface. However, `getAddress` in `Office` is static and Java 8 requires a static interface method to be invoked using the interface name instead of a reference variable. That is why, the compiler will raise the following error message: ` TestClass.java:26: error: illegal static interface method call System.out.println(h.getAddress()); //2 ^ the receiver expression should be replaced with the type qualifier 'Office' 1 error `




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

44  |  ID: 3511  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which of the following sets of interface definitions are valid?

  1. `interface Measurement{ public int getLength(){ return 0; } } interface Size extends Measurement{ public int getLength(); }`

_Explanation:_ If a method in an interface has a body, the method must be marked as default. Here, getLength in Measurement is missing the default keyword. It is ok for a subinterface to redeclare an inherited default method as abstract. Therefore, Size is fine.

  2. `interface Measurement{ public default int getLength(){ return 0; } public static int getBreadth(){ return 0; } } interface Size extends Measurement{ public static final int UNIT = 100; public static int getLength(){ return 10;} } `

_Explanation:_ getLength method in Size is invalid because a default method cannot be overridden by a static method. You can, however, redeclare a static method of a super interface as a default method in the sub interface.

  3. `interface Measurement{ public int getLength(); public static int getBreadth(){ return 0; } private void helper(){ } } interface Size extends Measurement{ private void helper(){ } }`

_Explanation:_ Since Java 9, an interface is allowed to have private (but not protected) static as well as instance methods. Remember that private methods are not inherited. Private fields are still not allowed in an interface. Fields of an interface are still always implicitly public, static, and final.

  4. `interface Measurement{ public default final int getUnit(){ return 100; } private void helper(){ } } interface Size extends Measurement{ public int getLength(); }`

_Explanation:_ interface methods can never be declared final.

  5. `interface Measurement{ public int getLength(); public static int getBreadth(){ return 0; } private void helper(){ } } interface Size extends Measurement{ private final int STEP = 10; }`

_Explanation:_ Private or protected fields are still not allowed in an interface. Fields of an interface are still always implicitly public, static, and final.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

45  |  ID: 3510  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Given:
```
    interface Book{
      public default String getId(){
         return "ISBN123456";
      }
    }
    
    interface Encyclopedia extends Book{
       //INSERT CODE HERE
    }
```java
    Which of the following options can be inserted in Encyclopedia independent of each other?

  1. `static String getId(){ return "AIN8888"; }`

_Explanation:_ You cannot override a non-static method with a static method and vice versa. (A default method is a non-static method.) You can, however, redeclare a static method of a super interface as a default method in the sub interface. For example:` class Base{ static void m(){ } void n(){ } static void x(){ } void y(){ } } class Sub extends Base{ void m(){ } //WILL NOT COMPILE static void n(){ } //WILL NOT COMPILE static void x(){ } //VALID, x() of base is hidden void y(){ } //VALID, y() of base is overridden } `

  2. `String getId();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can redeclare a default method and also make it abstract. Note that if a class then implements the subinterface, it will not inherit the default method from the super interface (because it has been redeclared as abstract by the subinterface).

  3. `default String getId(){ return "AIN8888"; };`

_Explanation:_ An interface can redeclare a default method and provide a different implementation.

  4. `abstract static String getName();`

_Explanation:_ 1\. static methods can never be abstract (neither in an interface nor in a class). 2\. An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body.

  5. `static String getAuthor();`

_Explanation:_ An interface can have a static method but the method must have a body.

  6. `default String getAuthor();`

_Explanation:_ A default method must have a body.

  7. `private String getId(){ return "ISBN123456"; }`

_Explanation:_ Although an interface can have private methods, you still cannot override a public method with a private method. Here, `getId` is public in `Book` and so, it cannot be overridden by a private method in `Encyclopedia`.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3

46  |  ID: 3526  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Given:
```
    interface IHello
    {
       public int hello(int x, int y);
       public long hello(long x, long y);
    }`
    
    Which of the following options are valid definitions in a different file assuming that the above definition is already available?

  1. `interface IHello2 extends IHello{ private static void print(){ }; }`

_Explanation:_ Since Java 9, an interface is allowed to have private (but not protected) static as well as instance methods. Fields of an interface are still always implicitly public, static, and final.

  2. `interface iHello2 implements IHello{ }`

_Explanation:_ An interface cannot "implement" anything.

  3. `abstract class Hello implements IHello{}`

_Explanation:_ Since the class is defined abstract, it is ok if it does not implement any method.

  4. `abstract class Hello implements IHello{ public short hello(short a, short b){ return 0; } }`

_Explanation:_ This is valid because the method `hello()` defined in Hello class is totally different from the `hello(`) method in the interface. Since the class is abstract, there is no problem.

  5. `abstract class Hello implements IHello{ public short hello(int a, int b){ return 0; } }`

_Explanation:_ This is invalid because the method name hello and its arguments match the method name and arguments in the interface but the return type does not match. This causes the compiler to throw an error message: `Hello.java:1: hello(int,int) in Hello cannot implement hello(int,int) in IHello; attempting to use incompatible return type found : short required: int`




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 3, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The concept here is that the presence of ambiguous methods does not create a problem. A problem occurs when method is calling in an ambiguous way such that the compiler is not able to determine which method to call.

47  |  ID: 1346  |  Difficulty: Level 5
    
    
    Consider these two interfaces:
```java
    interface I1
    {
       void m1() throws java.io.IOException;
    }
    interface I2
    {
       void m1() throws java.io.FileNotFoundException;
    }`
    
    Which of the following are valid method declarations for a class that says it implements I1 and I2 ?

  1. Both, `public void m1() throws FileNotFoundException;` and `public void m1() throws IOException;`
  2. `public void m1() throws FileNotFoundException`

_Explanation:_ `FileNotFoundException` is a subclass of `IOException`, so this option satisfies both the interfaces.

  3. The class cannot implement both the interfaces as they have conflicting methods.
  4. `public void m1() throws Exception;`
  5. None of the above.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    When interfaces are involved, more than one method declaration may be overridden by a single overriding declaration. In this case, the overriding declaration must have a throws clause that is compatible with ALL the overridden declarations. This could be achieved by either declaring an exception that satisfies the is-a test for all of the overridden methods in the throws clause (which means, it should be a subclass of all of them) or avoiding a throws clause altogether.

## 📖 02 - Java OOA - LVTI

19 questions

1  |  ID: 3705  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following code fragments will NOT cause a compilation error when put inside the following code?
    
    `public TestClass{
       public static void main(String[] args) {
    
        //INSERT CODE HERE
    
       }
    }`

  1. `var m = 20; m = "20";`

_Explanation:_ Since `20` is an int, the type of `m` will be `int`. You cannot assign a String to an int variable.

  2. `var list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add(20); list.add("20");`

_Explanation:_ `list` will be deduced to be of type `ArrayList<Object>`. Thus, you may add any object to this list including Integer and String.

  3. `var ex = new Exception(); ex = new java.io.IOException(); `

_Explanation:_ Type of `ex` will be `Exception`. Since `IOException` is a subclass of `Exception`, it is ok to assign an object of type `IOException` to a variable of type `Exception`.

  4. `var str = null;`

_Explanation:_ The compiler cannot definitively determine the type of `null`. So, it cannot decide the type of the variable `str` and refused to compile this statement.

  5. ` Runnable r = new Runnable(){ var str = "hello"; public void run(){ System.out.println(str); } };`

_Explanation:_ var declarations are not allowed for class/instance fields as well as method parameters. Other than that, this code is fine.

  6. `var list = new ArrayList<>(); list.add("20"); String str = list.get(0); `

_Explanation:_ `list` will be deduced to be of type `ArrayList<Object>`. Thus, you may add any type of object to this list but you can't assign a value retrieved from it to a variable of any type other than Object without a cast.

  7. `class Rule{ var id = 10; }`

_Explanation:_ var declarations are allowed only within local scope (that is why it is called "Local" variable type inference). Local scope implies methods, constructors, and static and instance initializers. var declarations are not allowed even if a static or instance field is a member of a nested class.

  8. `var _ = """ hello hi""";`

_Explanation:_ An underscore ( _ ) by itself is a reserved word and therefore it cannot be a valid identifier name in Java 21. Java 22 has introduced "unnamed variables and patterns" feature that allows usage of _ as an unnamed variable is certain situations (not important for the OCP 21 exam). The text block is valid though and contains two lines. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3
```
