3  |  ID: 3068  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given the following code:
```java
    public void someMethod(){
       var values = new ArrayList<String>();
       //INSERT CODE HERE
    }
```
    What can be inserted at the given location without causing any compilation error?

  1. `values.sort( var a, var b -> a.compareTo(b) );`

_Explanation:_ The lambda expresion is syntactically incorrect. If you want to specify the type of the lambda variables or if there are more than one arguments, then you have to put the argument list within parentheses, like this: `values.sort((var a, var b) -> a.compareTo(b));` 

  2. `values.sort( (a, b) -> a.compareTo(b) ); values.forEach( System.out::println );`

_Explanation:_ Note: List extends Iterable interface, which defines a default forEach method: `default void forEach(Consumer<? super T> action)` Performs the given action for each element of the Iterable until all elements have been processed or the action throws an exception. Actions are performed in the order of iteration, if that order is specified. Exceptions thrown by the action are relayed to the caller. The behavior of this method is unspecified if the action performs side-effects that modify the underlying source of elements, unless an overriding class has specified a concurrent modification policy. Implementation Requirements: The default implementation behaves as if: ` for (T t : this) action.accept(t);`

  3. `values.sort(); values.forEach( System.out::println);` 

_Explanation:_ The `sort` method requires a Comparator as an argument. Note: `java.util.List` interface defines a `default void sort(Comparator<? super E> c)` method, which sorts the elements of the list as per the ordering enforced by the passed `Comparator`.

  4. `values.removeIf((var k)->k.equals("a") );`
  5. `String[] sa = values.toArray();`

_Explanation:_ `values.toArray();` will return an `Object[]`. You cannot assign it to `sa`, which is of type `String[]`, without a cast. List has two `toArray` methods. 1\. `Object[] toArray() `: Returns an array containing all of the elements in this list in proper sequence (from first to last element). 2\. `<T> T[] toArray(T[] a) `: Returns an array containing all of the elements in this list in proper sequence (from first to last element); the runtime type of the returned array is that of the specified array. If the list fits in the specified array, it is returned therein. Otherwise, a new array is allocated with the runtime type of the specified array and the size of this list Thus, the argument to this method can be an array with length 0 (but not null) i.e. `String[] sa = values.toArray(new String[0]); //valid, //sa will be assigned a new array of Strings containing //all the elements of the array pointed to by values. String[] temp = null; String[] sa = values.toArray(temp); //will compile //but will throw NullPointerException`

  6. `class Local{ var value = 10; }`

_Explanation:_ `Local` is a valid local class, but `value` would be a instance field of that class and var declarations are not allowed for instance/static initializers. Therefore, this will not compile.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Although not relevent for this question, the exam expects you to know the natural order of strings that start with numbers (`0` to `9`), the minus sign, upper case and lower case English alphabets. 
    The order is:
    `-` < digits (`0` to `9`) < upper case letters (`A` to `Z`) < lower case letters (`a` to `z`).
    
    So, for example, `"-21"` would be considered "smaller" than `"1"` and `"Aaaa"` would be considered smaller than `"a"`.
    Similarly, `"-100"` will be put before `"-200"` even though, numerically, `-200` is smaller than `-100`.

4  |  ID: 3064  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following code snippets appearing inside a method are valid?
    

  1. `for(var x : System.getProperties().entrySet()){ var m = x.getKey(); }`

_Explanation:_ `System.getProperties()` returns a `Properties` object. `Properties` class extends `Hashtable<Object, Object>`, which means the types for the key and the value are Object and Object respectively. (FYI, `HashTable` class implements the `Map` interface.) `Map` has a method named `entrySet()`, which returns `Set<Map.Entry<K,V>>`. Since, in the case of Properties, K and V are typed to Object and Object respectively, `x` is typed to `Map.Entry<Object, Object>` and, therefore, `m` is typed to `Object`. 

  2. `for(var x : System.getProperties().keySet()){ System.out.println(x.length()); }`

_Explanation:_ `Map` has a method named `keySet()`, which returns `Set<K>`. As explained above, K is typed Object in this case. Therefore, `x.length()` will not compile. It will work if this is changed to : `System.out.println(x);` 

  3. `var obj = null;`

_Explanation:_ A var declaration must have an initializer that the compiler can use to infer the type without any ambiguity.

  4. `var k = System.out::println`;

_Explanation:_ `System.out::println` is a valid a method reference, however, the compiler cannot determine the type of the object that will be pointed to by `k` using this method reference. That is why, `var k` will not compile. The error message will say, "method reference needs an explicit target-type".

  5. `var _ = new ArrayList<>();`

_Explanation:_ Since Java 9, _ has been made a keyword. Therefore, it cannot be used as a variable name anymore. If you change the variable name to something like `_al`, this statement will be valid.

  6. `var _ = 10;`

_Explanation:_ Since Java 9, _ has been made a keyword. Therefore, it cannot be used as a variable name anymore. Otherwise, this statement is valid.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    NOTE 1: Some candidates have reported getting a question that uses `System.getProperties()` method. Information presented in this question is sufficient and you do not need to dig any deeper into this method.
    NOTE 2:  Java 22 has introduced "unnamed variables and patterns" feature that allows usage of _ as an unnamed variable is certain situations but this is not important for the OCP 21 exam.
    
    
      

5  |  ID: 3063  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given the following code that appears inside a method:
```java
       var values = new ArrayList<String>();
       //INSERT CODE HERE
```
    What can be inserted at the given location without causing any compilation error?

  1. `values.forEach(var k->System.out.print(k.length()));`

_Explanation:_ The lambda expresion is syntactically incorrect. If you want to specify the type of the lambda variables, then you have to put the variable list within parenthesis, like this: `values.forEach((var k)->System.out.print(k.length()));`

  2. `values.forEach( (var k)->System.out.print(k.length()));`
  3. `values.forEach( k -> System.out.print(k.length()));`
  4. `var k = values.get(0); values.add(k);`

_Explanation:_ `values.get(0)` will cause an `java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException` to be thrown at run time because `values` points to an empty ArrayList. It won't cause a compilation error though.

  5. `for(var value : values){ }`
  6. `values.add(1);`

_Explanation:_ The type of values is `ArrayList<String>`. You cannot add an `Integer` to a list of `String`s.

7  |  ID: 3062  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the following code:
    `import java.util.*;
    
    class Student{
       int marks;
    }  
    
    class TestClass {
        var k = new Student(); //1
    
        public static void main(String[] args) {
    
           var s = new Student(){    //2
                    @Override 
                    public String toString(){ return "student obj"; };
                   };
    
           var slist = Set.of(new Student()); //3
    
           for(var i : slist){  //4
                System.out.println(i);
           }
    	
           slist.removeIf((var s1) -> s1.marks<0); //5
        }
    }`

  1. It will compile without any error.
  2. Compilation error at //1

_Explanation:_ Var declarations are allowed only within local scope (within a method and static and instance initializers), which is why it is called "local" type inferencing. It is not allowed for declaring class or instance fields, method return type, and method parameters.

  3. Compilation error at //2

_Explanation:_ This code creates an anonymous subclass of Student and overrides the toString method. As another example, the following is also valid: `var o = new Object(){ @Override public String toString(){ return "student obj"; }; };`

  4. Compilation error at //3
  5. Compilation error at //4
  6. Compilation error at //5

_Explanation:_ This is a valid lambda expression because the type of the lambda argument can be inferred by the compiler from the context (It is `Student` because `slist` is a `Set` of Students). However, the following will not compile: `var m1 = (str)-> str.length()>10;` because the compiler cannot determine the type of the object that will be created using the lambda expression from the context. The following will compile fine: `Predicate<String> m2 = (str)-> str.length()>10;`




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Java 9 has added List.of/Set.of methods that return an unmodifiable list/set containing an arbitrary number of elements.
    Note that `Set.of` throws an `IllegalArgumentException` if the input contains duplicate values and both `List.of` and `Set.of` throw a `NullPointerException` if the input contains a `null` value.
    

8  |  ID: 3061  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which of these array declarations and initializations are legal?
    

  1. `var i[][] = { { 1, 2 }, { 1 }, { }, { 1, 2, 3 } } ; `

_Explanation:_ There are two problems here: 1\. `var` is not allowed as an element type of an array. `var i[]` implies that the type of the elements of the array is `var`, which is not allowed. Therefore, it should just be `var i` instead of `var i[][]` or `var i[]`. 2\. An array initializer needs an explicit target-type if the variable is declared using var declaration. Without it, the compiler is not able to deduce the type of the array unambiguously. Therefore, `{ { 1, 2 }, { 1 }, { }, { 1, 2, 3 } }` must written as `new int[][]{ { 1, 2 }, { 1 }, { }, { 1, 2, 3 } }`

  2. `var i[ ] = new int[2] {1, 2} ;`

_Explanation:_ 1\. If you give the elements explicitly you can't give the size. So it should be just `int[] { 1, 2 }` (or just `{ 1, 2 }` if you are not using `var` declaration). 2\. `var i[]` is incorrect as well. It should be `var i`.

  3. `var i = new int[ ][ ] { {1, 2, 3}, {4, 5, 6} } ;`
  4. `var i = { { 1, 2 }, new int[ 2 ] } ;`

_Explanation:_ As explained in option 1, `new int[][]` is required on the right side.

  5. `var i[4] = new int[]{ 1, 2, 3, 4 } ;`

_Explanation:_ 1\. You cannot specify the size of the array on left hand side. 2\. As explained in option 1, it should be `var i = ...;` instead of `var i[] = ...;`.

  6. `var fa = new Float{ 1.1F, 2.2F, 3.3F };`

_Explanation:_ Missing [ ] on Float. This would have been valid: `var fa = new Float[]{ 1.1F, 2.2F, 3.3F };`




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    If you explicitly specify the members then you can't give the size. So option 2 is wrong.
    The size of the array is never given during the declaration of an array reference. So option 5 is wrong.
    The size of an array is always associated with the array instance, not the array reference.
    
      

9  |  ID: 3059  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Given:
```java
    import java.util.*;
    
    class Student{
       int marks;
     }  
    
    public class TestClass {
       static var getHighest(var students){
           int highest = 0;
           for(var student : students){
              if(highest <student.marks) highest  = student.marks;
           }
           return highest;
       }
    
       public static void main(String[] args)  { 
    
           var student  = new Student(); 
    
           var allStudents = new ArrayList<Student>();  
    
           allStudents.add(student); 
    
           var h = getHighest(allStudents);
           
           System.out.println(h);
       }
    
    }
```
    Identify correct statements about the above code.

  1. It will compile if the `getHighest` method declaration is changed to: `static var getHighest(ArrayList<Student> students)`
  2. It will compile if the `getHighest` method declaration is changed to: `static int getHighest(var students)`
  3. It will compile if `allStudents` type is changed from var to `ArrayList<Student>`.
  4. It will compile if the type of `h` is changed from `var` to `int`.
  5. None of the above.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Remember that var declarations are not allowed for class/instance fields, method return type, and method parameters. Therefore,  `static var getHighest(var students)` must be changed to `static int getHighest(ArrayList<Student> students)`.

10  |  ID: 3231  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
     Given:
```java
    public class Switcher{
     
       public static void main(String[] args){
           switch(Integer.parseInt(args[1]))  //1
           {
              case 0 :
                 var b = false; //2
                 break;
         
              case 1 :
                 b = true; // 3
                 break;
           }
           
           if(b) System.out.println(args[2]); //4
       }
    }
```
    What will the above program print if compiled and run using the following command line: 
    `java Switcher 1 2 3
```java
    

  1. It will print 1
  2. It will print 2
  3. It will print 3
  4. It will not print anything.
  5. It will not compile because of //1.

_Explanation:_ There is no problem here because Integer.parseInt() returns an int.

  6. It will not compile because of //2.

_Explanation:_ var declarations can be used inside a method.

  7. It will not compile because of //3.

_Explanation:_ There is no problem here. b is in scope for the rest of the switch block.

  8. It will not compile because of //4.

_Explanation:_ It will not compile because of `if(b)` because `b` is declared in the switch block and it is out of scope after the switch block ends. Pay close attention to question text. It may seem to test you on one concept but actually it could be testing something entirely different.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 8

11  |  ID: 3220  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
     Which of the following are valid at line 1?
    
    `public class X{
        //line 1: insert code here.
    }`
    

  1. `var s;`

_Explanation:_ Cannot use 'var' on variable without initializer. In other words, a var declaration must assign a value to the variable. Further, var declaration can only be used for variables with a local scope (i.e. variables defined within a method or within static or instance code blocks.) It cannot be used for instance or static fields or method parameters.

  2. `String s = 'asdf';`

_Explanation:_ Only a char value can be specified within single quotes. A string must be enclosed within double quotes ".

  3. `String s = 'a';`

_Explanation:_ 'a' is a char. "a" is a String.

  4. `String s = this.toString();`

_Explanation:_ Since every class directly or indirectly extends the `Object` class and since the `Object` class has a `toString()` method, that `toString()` method will be invoked and the `String` that it returns will be assigned to `s`.

  5. `String s = asdf;`

_Explanation:_ there is no variable asdf defined in the given class.

  6. `String s;`

_Explanation:_ It is ok to have an uninitialized local variable. It is not to to access it without initializing it.

  7. `var al; al = new ArrayList<>(); `

_Explanation:_ The statement `var al;` will not compile because a var declaration must initialize the variable in the same statement.

  8. `var al = new ArrayList<>(); `

_Explanation:_ This is invalid because var declaration are only allowed for "local" variables i.e. variables defined in methods. Had this statement been in a method, the type of `al` will be inferred here as `ArrayList<Object>`. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4, 6

12  |  ID: 3203  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following are valid code snippets appearing in a method:

  1. `var a = b = c = 100;`

_Explanation:_ There are two issues here - 1\. You cannot declare multiple variables in the same statement using `var`. 2\. Chaining to use a value of a variable at the time of declaration is not allowed. Had `b` and `c` been already declared, it would have been valid. For example, the following is valid: ` var b = 0; var c = 0; var a = b = c = 100;` 

  2. `var a = 100, b = 10; var a = b;`

_Explanation:_ 'var' is not allowed in a compound declaration. Therefore, var a = 100, b = 10; will not compile.

  3. `int a, b, c=100;`
  4. `int a=100, b, c;`
  5. `int a = 100 = b = c;`
  6. `int a = b = c = 100;`

_Explanation:_ Chaining to use a value of a variable at the time of declaration is not allowed. Had b and c been already declared, it would have been valid. For example, the following is valid: ` int b = 0, c = 0; int a = b = c = 100;` Even the following is valid: `int b , c; //Not initializing b and c here.` `int a = b = c = 100; //declaring a and initializing c, b, and a at the same time.` Notice the order of initialization of the variables - c is initialized first, b is initialized next by assigning to it the value of c. Finally, a is initialized. 

  7. `int a, b, c; a = b = c = 100;`
  8. `Integer x = 10d;`

_Explanation:_ Suffixing a d to a non-decimal number makes it a double literal. Thus, 10d is a double value. It cannot be assigned to an Integer variable. The following is valid though: `Integer x = (int) 10d;` Here, `10d` is cast to an `int`, which is then boxed into an `Integer`.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 4, 7

     Consider the following code snippet:
```

    //INSERT LINE OF CODE HERE

      }`
    
    What type can be inserted in the code above so that the above code compiles and runs as expected ?
    

  1. `int condition;`

_Explanation:_ It will not compile because condition is not initialized before it is used in the switch.

  2. `long condition = 2;`

_Explanation:_ long, float, double, and boolean can never be used as a switch variable.

  3. `var condition = Integer.valueOf("1");`
  4. `String condition = "1";`

_Explanation:_ Although a String can be used in a switch statement, it will not work here because the case statements in the given code do not use Strings.

  5. `var condition = Short.valueOf(1);`

_Explanation:_ This is almost a valid option but for the fact that 1 is an int and you can't pass an int to Short.valueOf because it expects a short (or a String). That is why it will not compile. `short condition = Short.valueOf((short)1);` would have been valid.

  6. `Byte condition = 1;`

14  |  ID: 3201  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
     Consider the following code appearing in a file named TestClass.java:
```java
    class Test{ }  // 1
    
    public class TestClass {
    
       var v1; // 2
    
       public int main(String[] args)  { // 3
    
           var v2; //4
    
           double x=10, double y;  // 5
    
           var v3 = null; //6
           
           for(var k=0; k<x; k++){ } //7
    
           Float params[][] = {1.1f, 1.2f, 3.2f};  //8
            
           return 0;
       }
    }
```
    Which of the lines are valid?

  1. // 1 

_Explanation:_ It is a valid piece of code that declares a class.

  2. // 2

_Explanation:_ var declarations are allowed only inside a method and in for loop declarations.

  3. // 3

_Explanation:_ This is a valid declaration of a method named main. Although, it is not a correct declaration for the standard main method that can be used to execute the class, but it is a valid method nevertheless.

  4. // 4

_Explanation:_ A var declaration must have an initializer. Without the initializer, the compiler will not be able to infer the type of the variable.

  5. // 5

_Explanation:_ This statement syntactically invalid. It can be written either as `double x=10; double y;` or as `double x=10, y;` Note that even though `x` is a `double` and `10` is an `int`, it is valid because `10` will automatically be converted to a `double`. The reverse would not be valid i.e. `int x = 10.0;` will be invalid. You need a cast to assign a `double` value to an `int` variable: `int x = (int) 10.0;`

  6. // 6

_Explanation:_ This is invalid because the compiler cannot infer any type information from `null` and so it cannot determine the type of the variable `v3`.

  7. // 7

_Explanation:_ This is a valid usage of var declaration in a for loop.

  8. //8

_Explanation:_ `params` is declared to be a two dimensional array while the initializer contains a single dimensional array. The following would have been valid though: `Float params[] = {1.1f, 1.2f, 3.2f}; ` and `float params[] = {1.1f, 1.2f, 3.2f}; `




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 3, 7

15  |  ID: 3604  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following declarations are valid?
    

  1. `float f1 = 1.0;`

_Explanation:_ 1.0 is a double. 

  2. `float f = 43e1;`

_Explanation:_ 43e1 is a double.

  3. `float f = -1;`
  4. `float f = 0x0123;`
  5. `float f = 4;`
  6. `var f1 = 1.0; float f2 = f1;`

_Explanation:_ `1.0` is a `double` value. Therefore, compiler makes `f1` a `double` variable. However, a `double` value cannot be assigned to a `float` variable and so, `float f2 = f1;` fails compilation.

  7. `var f = 4f;`

_Explanation:_ Type of `f` will be `float` because of the suffix `f` in `4`. (Thus, `float f2 = f;` would also be valid if put next to this statement).

  8. `void m(float x){ var x = 20; }`

_Explanation:_ The variable x is already declared as a method parameter and is therefore "in scope" for the entire method. You cannot declare another variable by the same name in the same scope.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 4, 5, 7

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The values in option 1 and 2 (`1.0 and 43e1`) are double values. Although these values are compile time constants and are small enough to fit into a `float` variable, the assignment is still invalid because Java does not permit implicit narrowing from `double` to `float`. Implicit narrowing is permitted only among `byte, char, short, `and` int`.

16  |  ID: 3600  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following statements are valid when defined inside a method?
    

  1. `String[ ] sa = new String[3]{ "a", "b", "c"};`

  2. `String sa[ ] = { "a ", " b", "c"};`
  3. `String sa = new String[ ]{"a", "b", "c"};`

_Explanation:_ here `sa` is not declared as array of strings but just as a String.

  4. `String sa[ ] = new String[ ]{"a", "b", "c"};`
  5. `String sa[ ] = new String[ ] {"a" "b" "c"};`

_Explanation:_ There are no commas separating the strings.

  6. `var sa = {""" a b""", "c"};`

_Explanation:_ You can't apply `[]` to `var` and if you want to assign an array to a `var` variable, the array's type must be specified in the array initializer. You can write this code as follows: `var sa = new String[]{""" a b""", "c"};` 

  7. `var[] sa = new String[ ]{"a", "b", "c"};`

_Explanation:_ You can't apply `[]` to `var`.

17  |  ID: 3601  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following declarations is equivalent to `var  b = 10>11;`.
    

  1. `bool b = false;`

_Explanation:_ bool is not a valid type in Java.

  2. `var b = false;`
  3. `var b = false|true;`

_Explanation:_ This will assign `true` to `b`.

  4. `bool b = (10<11);`
  5. `boolean b = false;`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    `bool` is an invalid keyword. Therefore, 1 and 4 can't be right. (Although 1 could be right if `bool` were a user defined class but as per Java coding conventions, a class name should start with a capital letter.)
    
    `boolean b = 1;` is wrong because you can only assign `true` or `false` to a `boolean` variable. 1 is an integral value it cannot be converted to `boolean`. Also note that `boolean b = null;` would be invalid as well because `null` is not a true or false value. A primitive (whether it is a boolean or an int or a double), can never be assigned `null`.
    
    `boolean b = true|false; `and `boolean b = true||false;` are both valid and the difference between true|false and true||false is not material in this case. However, there is a lot of difference between | (and &) and || (and &&) as explained below:
    
    
    || and && perform short circuit evaluation, while & and | do not. Which means, if you use the || and && forms, Java will not bother to evaluate the right-hand operand if the result of the expression can be known by just evaluating the left hand operand.
    
    Consider the following example.
    
    `Boolean b = true; 
    if(b || foo.timeConsumingCall()) {    
      //entered here without calling timeConsumingCall() 
    } 
```java
    Another example:
    
    `String s = null;
    if(s != null && s.isEmpty())  //No NullPointerException because string.isEmpty() is not called.
    //If you use & instead of && , s.isEmpty will be called 
    //and a NullPointerException will be thrown.
    {     
       ...
    } `

    Given:
```
    import java.util.*;
    class Student{ }  
    
    public class TestClass {
    
       var students = new ArrayList<Student>(); //1
    
       public static void main(String[] args)  { 
    
           var student  = new Student(); //2
    
           var allStudents = new ArrayList<>();  //3
    
           allStudents.add(student); //4
           
           for(var s : allStudents){ //5
              System.out.println(s);
           } 
           Student s2 = allStudents.get(0); //6
    
           var var = "what?"; //7
    
           var ch = '\\t'; //8
       }
    
    }`
    Which lines will cause compilation error?

  1. //1

_Explanation:_ var declaration is allowed only inside method body and for loop declarations. It is not allowed to declareclass/instance fields, method parameters, or method return types.

  2. //2
  3. //3

_Explanation:_ This is valid. Here, the type of `allStudents` will be ArrayList of Objects. You can take out an Object from the list and add any Object (including instances of subclasses of Object because all objects are Objects) to the ArrayList using `allStudents`.

  4. //4
  5. //5

_Explanation:_ This works because the type of `s` will be inferred as `Object`.

  6. //6

_Explanation:_ Observe that `new ArrayList<>()` means the type of the objects in the list is `java.lang.Object`. So, you cannot assign an object retrieved from this list to a `Student` variable. Had it been `new ArrayList<Student>()`, the assignment would have been valid.

  7. //7

_Explanation:_ `var` is not a keyword and so, it is ok to name a variable as `var`.

  8. //8

_Explanation:_ `'\\\t'` is not a valid character. `'\\\'` is a valid way to write a single backslash character and `'\t'` is a valid way to write a single tab character. `"\\\t"` would be a valid String containing two characters - a backslash and a t.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 6, 8

19  |  ID: 3516  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Given:
    `Map<String , List<? extends CharSequence>> stateCitiesMap =
                new HashMap<String, List<? extends CharSequence>>();`
    
    Which of the following options correctly achieves the same or compatible declaration using type inference?

  1. `var stateCitiesMap = new HashMap<String, List<>>();`

_Explanation:_ This will not compile because you can't use a diamond operator within a generic type specification i.e. `<String, List<>>` is wrong because of `List<>` being inside of another `<` and `>`.

  2. `Map<String , List<? extends CharSequence>> stateCitiesMap = new HashMap();`

_Explanation:_ While this is valid code but this does not take advantage of type inferencing and will generate a warning at compile time. To use type inferencing, the diamond operator must be used.

  3. `Map<String , List<? extends CharSequence>> stateCitiesMap = new HashMap<>();`

_Explanation:_ This option use the diamond operators correctly to indicate the type of objects stored in the HashMap to the compiler. The compiler inferences the type of the objects stored in the map using this information and uses it to prevent the code from adding objects of another type.

  4. `Map<String , List<? extends CharSequence>> stateCitiesMap = new HashMap<<>>();`

_Explanation:_ This will not compile because `new HashMap<<>>(`) is uses invalid syntax. You cannot use a blank <> inside another <>.

  5. `var stateCitiesMap = new HashMap<String, List<? extends CharSequence>>();`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 5

↑ Back to Table of Contents

## 📖 02 - Java OOA - Nested classes 

30 questions

1  |  ID: 1521  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of these statements are true?
    

  1. Non-static inner class cannot have static members.

_Explanation:_ This restriction has been removed in Java 16. Since Java 16, non-static nested class i.e. inner class is allowed to have static members. Before Java 16, they were allowed to have a static field only if that field is final.

  2. Objects of static nested classes can be created without creating instances of their Outer classes.
  3. Member variables in any nested class cannot be declared final.

_Explanation:_ Nested classes can have final members (i.e. methods and fields)

  4. Anonymous classes cannot define constructors explicitly in Java code.
  5. Anonymous classes cannot be static.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Since Java 16, non-static nested classes (aka inner classes) are allowed to contain static members (fields as well as methods).
    Anonymous classes cannot have explicitly defined constructors, since they have no explicitly defined names.
    
    Remember: A nested class is any class whose declaration occurs within the body of another class or interface. A top level class is a class that is not a nested class. An inner class is a nested class that is not explicitly or implicitly declared static. A class defined inside an interface is implicitly static.
    
     Consider the following nested class B which is static:
```java
    public class A  //This is a standard Top Level class.
    {
      class X
      {
        static int j = 10;  //compiles fine
      }
      public static class B //This is a static nested class
      {
      }
    }`
    
    You can create objects of B without having objects of A. For example: `A.B b = new A.B();`
    Members in outer instances are directly accessible using simple names. There is no restriction that member variables in inner classes must be final.
    Nested classes define distinct types from the enclosing class, and the instanceof operator does not take of the outer instance into consideration.

2  |  ID: 1311  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following statements are true?
    

  1. Non-static nested classes must have either default or public accessibility.

_Explanation:_ Non-static nested classes (except method local classes), can have any type of accessibility. 

  2. Non-static nested classes cannot contain static members.

_Explanation:_ Prior to Java 16, non-static inner classes were not allowed to have static methods and non-final static fields. This restriction has been removed in Java 16.

  3. Methods in all nested classes can be declared static.
  4. All nested classes can be declared static.

_Explanation:_ Local (i.e. inside a method) nested classes cannot be declared static.

  5. A static nested class can contain a non - static inner class.
  6. A nested class may be sealed.

_Explanation:_ Yes, it is allowed as long as the class fulfills other requirements of a sealed class.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 5, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Here is the official terminology used the the Java Language Specification:
    1. A top level class is a class that is not a nested class.
    2. A nested class is any class whose declaration occurs within the body of another class or interface.
    3. An inner class is a nested class that is not explicitly or implicitly declared static.
    
    
    Nested classes are divided into two categories: static and non-static. Nested classes that are declared static are simply called static nested classes. Non-static nested classes are called inner classes.
    
    Further, a static nested class is behaviorally a top-level class that has been nested in another top-level class for packaging convenience.
    
    Since Java 16, Inner classes can declare non-final static members as well.  For example, the following is valid:
    `class Outer {
      class Inner {
        static int i;
      }
    }`

3  |  ID: 1550  |  Difficulty: Easy

Consider: 
    
    
    
    public class Outer
    {
      int i = 10;
      class Inner
      {
        public void methodA()
        {
           //line 1.
        }
      }
    }
    
    

Which of the following statements are valid at line 1?

  1. `System.out.println(this.i);`

_Explanation:_ This will not compile because there is no '`i`' in '`this`'. There is an '`i`' in `Outer.this`.

  2. `System.out.println(i);`

_Explanation:_ As there is no conflict, the compiler figures out that '`i'` is actually `Outer.this.i`.

  3. `System.out.println(Outer.this.i);`
  4. '`i`' cannot be accessed inside the inner class method.
  5. The code cannot be compiled.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3

4  |  ID: 1726  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    What will the following code print when compiled and run?
    `public class Onion {
        private String data = "skin";
        
        private class Layer extends Onion {
            String data = "thegoodpart";
            public String getData() {
                return data;
            }
        }
    
        public String getData() {
            return new Layer().getData();
        }
    
        public static void main(String[] args) {
            var o = new Onion();
            System.out.println(o.getData());
        }
    }
```

  1. It will print an empty String.
  2. skin
  3. thegoodpart
  4. It will not compile.
  5. It will throw an exception at run time.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    This is quite straightforward. You are calling `Onion`'s `getData()`, which in turn creates a `Layer` object and calls its `getData()`. `Layer`'s `getData()` returns `data` belonging to `Layer` (and not to `Onion`) because the reference use to access `data` is "this" i.e. `return this.data;` and inside Layer, "this" is of type Layer. To access Onion's data inside Layer, you can do `Onion.this.data`.
    
    As an interesting exercise, you should try running the given code after removing `getData() `method from `Layer` class and see what happens.

5  |  ID: 1492  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following statements are true?
    

  1. Top level (aka package level) classes can be declared static.
  2. Classes declared as members of top-level classes can be declared static.
  3. Local classes can be declared static.
  4. Anonymous classes cannot be declared static.
  5. No type of class can be declared static.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The modifier static pertains only to nested classes, not to top level or local or anonymous classes. That is, only classes declared as members of top-level classes can be declared static. Top leve/Package level classes, local classes (i.e. classes declared in methods) and anonymous classes cannot be declared static.

6  |  ID: 1484  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following statements are true?
    

  1. A nested class may be declared static.

_Explanation:_ FYI, JLS defines the following terminology in chapter 8: A top level class is a class that is not a nested class. A nested class is any class whose declaration occurs within the body of another class or interface. An inner class is a nested class that is not explicitly or implicitly declared static. 

  2. Anonymous inner class may be declared public.
  3. Anonymous inner class may be declared private.
  4. Anonymous inner class may be declared protected.
  5. Anonymous inner class may extend an abstract class.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
```java
    abstract class SomeClass {   public abstract void m1(); }
    public class TestClass
    {
      public static class StaticInnerClass { }  //option 1
    
      public SomeClass getSomeClass()
      {
          return new SomeClass()
             {
                public void m1() { }
             };
      }
    }
```
    Note that the anonymous class created inside the `getSomeClass()` method above extends the abstract class `SomeClass`.
    

7  |  ID: 1243  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following statements regarding inner classes are true ?
    

  1. A non static inner class may have static members.

_Explanation:_ Since Java 16, non-static nested class i.e. inner class is allowed to have static members. Before Java 16, they were allowed to have a static field only if that field is final. ` class OuterClass { public class InnerClass { static int VAL = 10; //COMPILES FINE static String STR = "1234"; //COMPILES FINE static Object obj = new Object(); / static int val2 = 10; //COMPILES FINE static final void method() {} //COMPILES FINE } }`

  2. Anonymous inner classes cannot have any 'extends' or 'implements' clause.
  3. Anonymous inner classes can only be created for interfaces.

_Explanation:_ It can also be created for classes.

  4. Anonymous inner classes can never have initialization parameters.

_Explanation:_ They can if they are for classes.

  5. Anonymous inner classes cannot be static.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 5

**Explanation:**  
If anonymous class is created for interface, it extends Object class and implement that interface, if it is created for a class then it extends that class. Since Java 16, inner classes are allowed to has static fields as well as static methods. Example:
    
    
    
    public class Outer
    {
       class Inner
       {
         static int k = 10; //valid since Java 16
         static void m(){}; //valid since Java 16
       }
    }
    
    

Consider the anonymous inner class for catching action events: 
    
    
    ActionListener al = new ActionListener()
                                 {
                                        public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)
                                        {
                                        }
                                 };
    

Here, the anonymous class implicitly extends Object and implements the interface ActionListener. Explicit extends or implements clauses are not allowed for such classes. Other inner class (i.e. non anonymous) can have them. Consider the following (although of no use) class: 
    
    
    public class TestClass
    {
        public TestClass(int i)  {    }
        public void m1()
        {
            TestClass al = new TestClass(10)
                           {
                                public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)
                                {
                                }
                          };
        }
    }
    

This illustrates : 

  1. Inner class can extend the outer class. 
  2. Anonymous inner class can be created for classes. (Not just for interfaces). They implicitly extend the class.(Here, TestClass) 
  3. Anonymous inner class can have initialization parameter. (If the class they extend has a corresponding constructor). 


8  |  ID: 1444  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Consider the following program...
    `public class Outer
    {
        private double d = 10.0;
        //put inner class here.
    }
```java
    Which of the following options can be inserted in Outer class?
    
    

  1. `class Inner { public void m1() { this.d = 20.0; } }`

_Explanation:_ It will not compile because of 'this.d'. To access d, you should either use d or Outer.this.d 

  2. `abstract class Inner { static int i1; public void m1() { d = 20.0; } } `

_Explanation:_ Since Java 16, non-static nested class i.e. inner class is allowed to have static members. Before Java 16, they were allowed to have a static field only if that field is final.

  3. `final class Inner { public void m1() { d = 20.0; } }`
  4. `private class Inner { public void m1() { d = 20.0; } } `



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    d is an instance member of Outer and is therefore accessible to all the other instance members of the class including a non-static inner class. You can access d from a static inner class by using a reference to an instance of Outer.

    Given:
```
    public class TestClass {
        static int val = 10;
        public static int reduce(int val){
            class Inner{
                public int reduce(int mval){
                    return mval-val--;
                }
            }
            val--;
            return new Inner().reduce(val);
        }
                
         public static void main(String[] args) {
            reduce(5);
            System.out.println(val);
        }
    }
```java
    

  1. 5
  2. 10
  3. 0
  4. 9
  5. Compilation failure.

_Explanation:_ Observe that the method parameter `val` of TestClass's reduce method can be accessible inside the Inner's reduce only if it is final or effectively final. Since we have the statement `val--;` in the methodbody outside the class declaration, that means, `val` is not effectively final and therefore, the usage of `val` inside the inner class is illegal. If `val--;` was removed from the method, you could do `return mval-val;` inside the inner class.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

10  |  ID: 1233  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the given options if put at //1 will compile without any error?
    
```
    public class TestClass
    {
       public class A
       {
       }
       public static class B
       {
       }
       public void useClasses()
       {
          //1
       }
    }
```java

  1. `new TestClass().new A();`
  2. `new TestClass.B();`
  3. `new A();`
  4. `new TestClass.A();`
  5. All of these are valid.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    class `A` is not static inner class of `TestClass`. So it cannot exist without an outer instance of `TestClass`.
    class `B` is static inner class and can be instantiated without an outer instance of `TestClass`.
    Now, the method `useClasses() `is an instance method. So, it already has instance of outer class associated with it. So, `new A(); `is also valid. new `TestClass.A();` is same as `new A()`.
    
    Although not related to this question, FYI, an anonymous class can never be static. Even if created in a static method.

11  |  ID: 1466  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    What will be the result of attempting to compile and run the following code?
```
    public class Nesting
    {
        public static void main(String args[])
        {
            B.C obj = new B( ).new C( );
        }
    }
    class A
    {
        char c;
        A(char c) { this.c = c; }
    }
    class B extends A
    {
       char c = 'a';
       B( ) { super('b'); }
       class C extends A
       {
          char c = 'c';
          C( )
          {
             super('d');
             System.out.println(B.this.c);
             System.out.println(C.this.c);
             System.out.println(super.c);
          }
       }
    }`
    

  1. It will not compile.
  2. It will throw an exception at run time.
  3. The program will compile without error, and print `a, c` and `d` in that order when run.
  4. The program will compile without error, and print `a, b` and `d` in that order when run.
  5. The program will compile without error, and print `b, c` and `a` in that order when run.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Every non-static inner class object has a reference to its outer class object which can be accessed by doing `OuterClass.this`.
    So the expression `B.this.c` will refer to B's `c`, whose value is `'a'`.
    Inside a non-static inner class, '`InnerClass.this`' is equivalent to '`this`'. so '`C.this.c`' refers to C's `c` which is '`c`'.
    The expression `super.c `will access the variable from A, the superclass of C whose value is '`d`'.

12  |  ID: 1894  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which variables of the enclosing class can an inner class access, if the inner class is defined in an instance method of the enclosing class?

  1. All static variables
  2. All final instance variables
  3. All instance variables
  4. All automatic variables.
  5. All final and effectively final automatic variables



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 3, 5

**Explanation:**  
Consider the following code: 
    
    
    
    public class TestClass
    {
       static int si = 10;   int ii = 20;
       public  void inner()
       {
          var ai = 30; //automatic variable
          ai = 31;//ai is not effectively final anymore.
          var fai = 40; //automatic final variable. 
          //Yes, a final local variable may also use var declaration
          class Inner
          {
              public Inner()  {  System.out.println(si+"  "+ii+"   "+fai);  }
          }
          new Inner();
       }
       public static void main(String[] args){  new TestClass().inner();  }
    }
    

As method inner is an instance method (i.e. non-static method), si, ii, and fai are accessible in class Inner. Note that ai is not accessible because it is not effectively final. If the line ai = 31; did not exist, ai would have been accessible. If method inner() were a static method, ii would have been inaccessible. Prior to java 8, only final local variables were accessible to the inner class but in Java 8, even effectively final local variables of the method are accessible to the inner defined in that method as well. 

13  |  ID: 1895  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which variables declared in the encapsulating class or in the method, can an inner class access if the inner class is defined in a static method of encapsulating class?
    

  1. All static variables
  2. All final instance variables
  3. All instance variables
  4. All automatic variables.
  5. All final or effectively final static or automatic variables

_Explanation:_ An effectively final variable means even though it is not declared final, it is never assigned a value again throughout the code after being assigned a value at the time of declaration. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 5

**Explanation:**  
Consider the following code: 
    
    
    
    public class TestClass
    {
       static int si = 10;   int ii = 20;
       public static void inner()
       {
          int ai = 30; //automatic variable
          ai = 31; //ai is not effectively final anymore.
          final int fai = 40; //automatic final variable
          class Inner
          {
              public Inner()  {   System.out.println(si+"    "+fai);      }
          }
          new Inner();
       }
       public static void main(String[] args)   {     TestClass.inner();       }
    }
    
    

Since method inner() is a static method, only si and fai are accessible in class Inner. Note that ai and ii are not accessible. If method inner() were a non - static method, ii would have been accessible. If the line ai = 31; did not exist, ai would have been accessible.

14  |  ID: 1893  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code in which `searchBook()` method has an inner class.
    
    `public class BookStore
    {
       private static final int taxId = 300000;
       private String name;
       public String searchBook( final String criteria )
       {
          int count = 0;
          int sum = 0;
          sum++;
          class Enumerator
          {
             String iterate( int k)
             {
                //line 1
                return "";
             }
             // lots of code.....
          }
          // lots of code.....
          return "";
       }
    }
```java
    Which variables are accessible at line 1?

  1. `taxId`
  2. `name`
  3. `criteria`

_Explanation:_ Even though this variable is local to the method, it is accessible in the inner class defined inside the method because it is declared as final.

  4. `count`

_Explanation:_ Although count is a local variable but its value does not change anywhere in the code. Hence, it is effectively final and is therefore accessible from the inner class.

  5. `k`
  6. sum

_Explanation:_ sum is a local variable and its value changes in the code (due to sum++). Hence, it is NOT effectively final and is therefore NOT accessible from the inner class.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 3, 4, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    If the inner class is non static, all the static and non-static members of the outer class are accessible (otherwise only static are accessible) So option 1, 2 are valid.
    Prior to java 8, only final local variables were accessible to the inner class but in Java 8, even effectively final local variables of the method are accessible to the inner defined in that method as well. So option 4 is correct.

    Consider the following code:
```

    Which of the following options can be inserted at //1?

  1. `Insider in = os.new Insider();`

_Explanation:_ You cannot refer to Insider class directly (unless you import the inner class using import statement such as `import p1.Outsider.*;` where p1 is the package name). In the given code, for all purposes, the name of the class is Outsider.Insider. Therefore, it should be referred to as: `Outsider.Insider in = os.new Insider();` 

  2. `os.Insider in = os.new Insider();`

_Explanation:_ `os.Insider` implies that Insider class is in package `os`, which is not the case.

  3. `Outsider.Insider in = os.new Insider();`

_Explanation:_ Since Insider is not a static class, it must have an associated outer class instance.

  4. `Insider in = Outsider.new Insider();`

_Explanation:_ You cannot refer to `Insider` class directly. You must use `Outsider.Insider` to declare the type of variable `in`. `Outsider.new Insider() `is wrong as well because Insider is not static. Further, even if `Insider` were static, the syntax to instantiate it would be: `new Outsider.Insider()`.

20  |  ID: 1360  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    What classes can a non-static nested class extend ?
    (Provided that the class to be extended is visible and is not final.)

  1. Only the encapsulating class.
  2. Any top level class.
  3. Any class.
  4. It depends on whether the inner class is defined in a method or not.
  5. None of the above.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    In general, there is no restriction on what a nested class may or may not extend.
    
    FYI, a nested class is any class whose declaration occurs within the body of another class or interface. A top level class is a class that is not a nested class. An inner class is a nested class that is not explicitly or implicitly declared static.

21  |  ID: 1361  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of these statements concerning nested classes and interfaces are true?
    

  1. An instance of a static nested class has an inherent outer instance.

_Explanation:_ Since static nested class is a static class, it does not have an instance of the outer class associated with it.

  2. A static nested class can contain non-static member variables.

_Explanation:_ It is like any other normal class.

  3. A static nested interface can contain static member variables.

_Explanation:_ Static nested interface is similar to top level interface.

  4. A static nested interface has an inherent outer instance associated with it.

_Explanation:_ A static nested interface is a static interface and so does not have an associated outer instance.

  5. For each instance of the outer class, there can exist many instances of a non-static inner class.
  6. An inner class cannot have static members.

_Explanation:_ Since Java 16, inner class is allowed to have static members.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 3, 5

**Explanation:**  
Note the difference between an inner class and a static nested class. Inner class means a NON STATIC class defined inside a class. Remember: 

  1. A **nested class** is any class whose declaration occurs within the body of another class or interface. 
  2. A **top level class** is a class that is not a nested class. 
  3. An **inner class** is a nested class that is not explicitly or implicitly declared static. 
  4. A class defined inside an interface is implicitly static. 
For example, 
    
    
    
    public class A  // outer class
    {
       static public class B //static nested class. 
           //It can be used in other places: A.B b = new A.B(); There is no outer instance.
       {
       }
       class C //Inner class. It can only be used like this:
           // A.C c = new A().new C(); Outer instance is needed.
       {
       }
    }
    
    

One can define a class as a static member of any top-level class. Now consider the following contents of a file named I1.java: 
    
    
    
    public interface I1
    {
        public void mA();
        public interface InnerI1
        {
            int k = 10;
            public void innerA();
        }
    }
    
    

Here, interface InnerI1 is implicitly STATIC and so is called as static nested interface. 'k' is a static member of this interface. If you do 'javap' on I1 it prints:  
  
Compiled from I1.java 
    
    
    public interface I1
        /* ACC_SUPER bit NOT set */
    {
        public abstract void mA();
        public static interface I1. InnerI1
        /* ACC_SUPER bit NOT set */
        {
            public static int k;
            public abstract void innerA();
        }
    }
    
    

This interface can be referred to in other places like: 
    
    
    
    class MyClass implements I1.InnerI1
    {
    ...
    }
    
    

This is similar to referring a top level class.

22  |  ID: 1394  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    What will be the result when you try to compile and run the following code?
```java
    public class TestClass
    {
       public static void main(String args[] )
       {
          var out = new Outer();
          System.out.println(out.getInner().getOi());
       }
    }
    class Outer
    {
       private int oi = 20;
       class Inner
       {
          int getOi() { return oi; }
       }
       Inner getInner() { return new Inner() ; }
    }
```

  1. The code will fail to compile as the class `Inner` is not defined properly.
  2. The code will fail to compile because the reference of an inner class cannot be passed outside the outer class.
  3. The code will fail to compile because the method `getOi( )` is not visible from the `main( )` method in `TestClass`.
  4. The code will compile without error and will print `20` when run.
  5. None of the above.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    A call to `getInner( )` will return a reference to a newly created Inner class instance. This object is an instance of a non-static inner class and is associated with the outer instance. This means that an object of an inner class has access to the members within the outer instance. Since the inner class is within the class containing the member `oi`, the member will be accessible to the inner instance, even though `oi` is declared private.

23  |  ID: 1385  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which statements concerning the relation between a non-static inner class and its outer class instances are true?
    

  1. Member variables of the outer instance are always accessible to inner instances, regardless of their accessibility modifiers.
  2. Member variables of the outer instance can always be referred to using only the variable name within the inner instance.

_Explanation:_ This is possible only if that variable is not shadowed by another variable in inner class.

  3. More than one inner instance can be associated with the same outer instance.
  4. An inner class can extend its outer class.
  5. A final outer class cannot have any inner classes.

_Explanation:_ There is no such rule.

24  |  ID: 1382  |  Difficulty: Tough

Consider the following code : 
    
    
    
    public class TestClass extends Thread
    {
        class Runner implements Runnable
        {
            public void run()
            {
                var t = new Thread[5];
                for(int i=0; i<t.length; i++) System.out.println(t[i]);
            }
        }
        
       public static void main(String args[]) throws Exception
       {
                var tc = new TestClass();
                new Thread( tc.new Runner() ).start();
       }
    }
    
    

How many Thread objects are created by the above program when it is compiled and run?

  1. 1
  2. 2

_Explanation:_ TestClass is also a Thread because it extends Thread.

  3. 6
  4. 7
  5. None, because the program will not compile.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The first Thread object is created at : `TestClass tc = new TestClass();`
    The second Thread object is created at : `new Thread( tc.new Runner() )`.
    
    `Runner` is not a `Thread` object.
    `Thread[] t = new Thread[5]; `does not create any thread object. It only creates an array that can hold 5 Thread objects. Each of those 5 references are initialized to `null`.
    
    `tc.new Runner()` is a valid construct because `Runner` is a non-static inner class. So you cannot do '`new TestClass.Runner()`'.

27  |  ID: 3515  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code:
    
```java
    class A
    {
    }
    public class TestClass
    {
       public class A
       {
           public void m() { }
       }
       class B extends A
       {
           B(){ m(); }
       }
       public static void main(String args[])
       {
          new TestClass().new A() {   public void m() { } }; //1
          var tc = new TestClass();
          //insert call to A's m() here
       }
    }
```

  1. This program will not compile.
  2. class created inside the main method at //1 is static.
  3. class created inside the main method at //1 is final.

_Explanation:_ It is indeed final because it is anonymous. Anonymous classes are implicitly final.

  4. Objects of class B cannot be created inside the main method just by doing "new B()"

_Explanation:_ you'll need to do: `new TestClass().new B()` or `tc.new B()` because inner class B is not static. By the way, class B extends the inner class A (and not the top level class A) because the inner class A shadows the outer class A.

  5. `new TestClass().new A().m();` can be inserted in main to invoke A's m().
  6. `new A().m();` can be inserted in main to invoke A's m().

_Explanation:_ An instance of inner class `A` can only exist in the context of an instance of `TestClass`. Since the main method is static, it cannot serve as a valid context. Therefore, you need to create an instance of `TestClass` first as shown in the previous option or use an existing `TestClass` instance as shown in the last option.

  7. `tc.new A().m();` can be inserted in main to invoke A's m().



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 4, 5, 7

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    An anonymous class can never be static, even if it is created in a static method.

28  |  ID: 1586  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which statements regarding the following code are correct?
    
    `class Outer
    {
       private void Outer() { }
       protected class Inner
       {
       }
    }`
    

  1. This code will not compile.
  2. Constructor for Outer is public.
  3. Constructor for Outer is private.
  4. Constructor for Inner is public.
  5. Constructor for Inner is protected.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    1. Putting a return type makes `private void Outer() { }` a method and not a constructor.
    2. When a programmer does not define ANY constructor, the compiler inserts one automatically, the access modifier of which is same as that of the class.
    
    For example, compile the given  code and run javap on the class file. This is what you should see:
    `class Outer extends java.lang.Object {
        Outer(); //****Notice this: compiler added it. 
         //It has no access modifier. Same as class.
        private void Outer(); // Our private method.
        protected class Outer. Inner extends java.lang.Object
        /* ACC_SUPER bit NOT set */
    {
            private final Outer this$0;
            protected Outer.Inner(Outer); //****Notice this: compiler added it. 
                                      //It has protected access modifier. Same as class.
        }
    }
```java
    

29  |  ID: 3522  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code:
```
    class OuterWorld
    {
        public InnerPeace i = new InnerPeace("none"); //1
        class InnerPeace
        {  
            private String reason = "none";
            InnerPeace(String reason){ this.reason = reason; } 
        }
        public static void main(String[] args){
            var ip = new InnerPeace("yoga"); //2
            var out = new OuterWorld();
            System.out.println(out.i.reason); //3
        }
    }
```java
    Identify correct statements about the above code.

  1. Compilation error at //1
  2. Compilation error at //2
  3. Compilation error at //3

_Explanation:_ Even though `reason` is a private member of `InnerPeace`, `InnerPeace` itself is a member of `OuterWorld` and so `OuterWorld` can access '`reason`'. You can also have a method defined inside `OuterWorld` and call it from outside `OuterWorld`: `class OuterWorld { public InnerPeace i = new InnerPeace(); private class InnerPeace { private String reason = "none"; } void m(){ System.out.println(i.reason); } }` This shows that an object of class `OuterWorld` can access the `reason` variable of `InnerPeace` even though it is private.

  4. code at //2 can be compiled if `InnerPeace` definition changed to: `private static class InnerPeace ... `
  5. code at //2 can be compiled if `InnerPeace` definition is moved to a separate `InnerPeace.java` file without impacting other parts of the code.

_Explanation:_ If you move `InnerPeace` outside `OuterWorld`, `InnerPeace`'s `reason` field will not be accessible from inside `OuterWorld`.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The wordings of this question are difficult to understand. However, expect similar questions on the exam. The question is essentially asking where all is the '`reason`' field accessible. Obviously, it is accessible within `InnerPeace` class. But it is also accessible in `OuterWorld` as shown in option 3.

30  |  ID: 1100  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Anonymous inner classes always extend directly from the Object class.
    

  1. True
  2. False



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    When you create an anonymous class for an interface, it extends from Object. For example,
    
    `button.addActionListener( new ActionListener() {  
       public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent ae) { } }  );`
    
    But if you make an anonymous class from another class then it extends from that class. For example, consider the following class:
    
    
    `class MyListener implements ActionListener
    {
      public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent ae) { 
                   System.out.println("MyListener class"); }
    }
    
    button.addActionListener( new MyListener() {
      public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent ae) { 
                   System.out.println("Anonymous Listener class"); }
     } );
```
    
    Here the anonymous class actually extends from `MyListener` class and successfully overrides the `actionPerformed()` method.
