## 📖 07 - Java I/O API - Serialization

14 questions

1  |  ID: 3706  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Given:
```java
    class Person implements Serializable{
        String name;
        Person(String name){
            this.name = name;
        }
    }
    
    class Student extends Person{
        String school;
        public Student(String name, String school){
            super(name);
            this.school = school;
            
        }
        public String toString(){
            return name+" "+school;
        }
    }
    public class TestClass {
        public static void main(String[] args)  {
            Person p = new Student("Bob Dylan", "NYU");
            try(
                    ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(
                                 new FileOutputStream("student.ser"));
    
                    ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(
                                  new FileInputStream("student.ser"));
                    ){
                oos.writeObject(p);
                oos.flush();
                System.out.println((Student)ois.readObject());
            }
        }
    }
```
    What can be done so that the above code prints `Bob Dylan NYU` when run? 
    

  1. Add `implements Serializable` clause to `Student` class.
  2. Add `implements Serializable, AutoCloseable` clause to `Student` class.
  3. Add `throws IOException` clause to the main method.
  4. Add `throws IOException, ClassCastException` clause to the main method.

_Explanation:_ java.lang.ClassCastException is an unchecked exception, so, it need not be declared in the throws clause.

  5. Add `throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException` clause to the main method.

_Explanation:_ The `readObject` method throws `IOException` and `ClassNotFoundException` in its throws clause. So, either you need to catch those exceptions or declare them in the calling method's `throws` clause as well.

  6. Replace `(Student)ois.readObject()` with `(Person)ois.readObject()`.

_Explanation:_ This is not necessary because the object that was serialized was of type `Student` and so, the desealized object will also be of type `Student`. So, the cast to `Student` is fine. Change the cast to `Person` will not affect the output.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

2  |  ID: 1712  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    As a part of an application, you have serialized and stored some objects of a class in the database. At another place in the same application, you deserialize those objects.
    
    After a few months you determine that you need to add one new String field in the class. 
    
    Which of the following statements are correct regarding the above described situation?

  1. The objects serialized earlier cannot be deserialized to the updated class objects.
  2. Objects serialized earlier can be deserialized to the updated class objects by adding a new `serialVersionUID` field with a value of 0 to the updated class.
  3. No special change is necessary in the updated class. Objects serialized earlier will be deserialized to the updated class objects but the newly added field will be null.
  4. Old serialized objects can be deserialized only if the original class had explicitly defined a `serialVersionUID` field and if the updated class maintains the same value for that field.

_Explanation:_ Ideally, every class that implements Serializable should explicitly define a static final `serialVersionUID` field of type `long`. For example, `public static final long serialVersionUID = 1;` If you make changes to the class and if you still want old objects to be successfully deserialized into the updated class objects, you should keep the same value for `serialVersionUID`. However, this is not a must because even if you did not define `serialVersionUID` explicitly in the old class, the compiler will create this field and provide a value for it on its own. You can inspect this value and use it to define `serialVersionUID` in your updated class. Note `serialVersionUID` must be static, final, and of type `long`, i.e., `ANY-ACCESS-MODIFIER static final long serialVersionUID = 42L;` Although it can have any access modifier, `private` is recommended.

  5. It is possible to deserialize the older objects into the update class objects even if the original class did not explicitly define the `serialVersionUID` field.

_Explanation:_ If a class that implements `Serializable` does not explicitly define `serialVersionUID` field, the compiler automatically adds this field. It assignes this field a value that is computed based on the attributes of the class such as the fields and the implemented interfaces. It is possible to determine this value by various means. For example, by using the serialver tool provided by the JDK: `serialver MyClass` Or by using `ObjectInputStream.readClassDescriptor()` method. Once you get this number, you can assign the same number to `serialVersionUID` in your updated class.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

3  |  ID: 1752  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Identify the correct statement(s).

  1. To customize the behavior of class serialization, the `readObject` and `writeObject` methods should be implemented.

_Explanation:_ Developers can change how a particular class is serialized by implementing two methods inside the class file. These methods are: `private void writeObject(ObjectOutputStream out) throws IOException; private void readObject(ObjectInputStream in) throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException;` Notice that both methods are (and must be) declared private, proving that neither method is inherited and overridden or overloaded. The trick here is that the virtual machine will automatically check to see if either method is declared during the corresponding method call. The virtual machine can call private methods of your class whenever it wants but no other objects can. Thus, the integrity of the class is maintained and the serialization protocol can continue to work as normal. 

  2. To customize the behavior of class serialization, the `defaultReadObject` and `defaultWriteObject` methods should be overridden.

_Explanation:_ These methods actually belong to `ObjectInputStream` and `ObjectOutputStream` respectively and they contain the logic of reading and writing an objects state from/to the stream. You don't need to implement these methods in your class. 

  3. Objects of a final class cannot be serialized.

_Explanation:_ Object of any class that implements Serializable interface can be serialized.

  4. Constructor of the class for an object being deserialized is never invoked except for record classes.

_Explanation:_ If an object is being deserialized, that means the class of that object implements `Serializable`. Therefore, its constructor will never be called. However, constructor for the super class may be invoked if the super class does not implement serializable interface. While deserializing a record, a new record object is created by invoking the record class’s canonical constructor, passing values deserialized from the stream as arguments to the canonical constructor. This is secure because it means the record class can validate the values before assigning them to fields, just like when an ordinary Java program creates a record object via new. 

  5. While serializing an object, only those objects in the object graph that implement `Serializable` are serialized and the rest are ignored.

_Explanation:_ If the object graph contains non-serializable objects, an exception is thrown and nothing is serialized. Object graph means all the objects that are linked/referenced by the first object (directly or indirectly) that is being serialized. Fields of an Object that are marked as transient are not serialized and so they do not cause an exception. Any field that is not marked transient but points to an object of a class that does not implement Serializable, will cause an exception to be thrown. Thus, the given statement is wrong. 

  6. To ensure that future versions of a class are compatible with the serialized data of a previous version of the class, a `serialVersionUID` field of type long must be used and it must be `private` and non-final.

_Explanation:_ The `serialVersionUID` field used for this purpose must be `final` but it can have any access modifier.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    You may need to customize the serialization/deserialization of a class when, for example, you want to set the value of a field using some computation. For example,
    `class MyClass implements Serializable{
       int someInt;  //want to serialize/deserialize this field in the regular fashion
    
       //don't want to serialize ld field value 
       //so, making it transient.
       transient java.time.LocalDate ld;  
    
       private void readObject(java.io.ObjectInputStream stream) //it must be private
             throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException {
    
             //this call sets someInt field to value from the serialized data 
             //but ld field is not set because the value of ld was never serialized
             stream.defaultReadObject(); 
          
             ld = LocalDate.now();//setting ld field to custom value
       }
    }`
    
    When a `MyClass` object is serialized, the value of `someInt` will be put in the stream but the value of `ld` will not be put in the stream because `ld` is transient. When the object is deserialized, the `readObject()` will be called. This method will execute the default logic of deserialization due to the call to `stream.defaultReadObject()` method. Next, it will set the value of `ld` field on its own.

4  |  ID: 1700  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Consider the following classes:
    
    `class Boo {
        public Boo(){ System.out.println("In Boo"); }
    }
    class BooBoo extends Boo {
        public BooBoo(){ System.out.println("In BooBoo"); }
    }
    
    class Moo extends BooBoo implements Serializable {
        int moo = 10; { System.out.println("moo set to 10"); }
        public Moo(){ System.out.println("In Moo"); }
    }`
    
    First, the following code was executed and the file moo1.ser was created successfully:
    
    `  Moo moo = new Moo();
      moo.moo = 20;
      FileOutputStream fos = new FileOutputStream("c:\\temp\\moo1.ser");
      ObjectOutputStream os = new ObjectOutputStream(fos);
      os.writeObject(moo);
      os.close();`
    
    Next, the following code was executed. 
    
    `  FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\moo1.ser");
      ObjectInputStream is = new ObjectInputStream(fis);
      Moo moo = (Moo) is.readObject();
      is.close();
      System.out.println(moo.moo);`
    
    Which of the following will be a part of the output of the second piece of code?
    
    

  1. In Boo
  2. In BooBoo
  3. In Moo
  4. 10
  5. 20
  6. moo set to 10



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    During deserialization, the constructor of the class (or any static or instance blocks) is not executed. However, if the super class does not implement Serializable, its constructor is called. So here, `BooBoo` and `Boo` are not Serializable. So, their constructor is invoked.

5  |  ID: 1701  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    What will the following program print when compiled and run?
    
    `class Boo implements Serializable {
        transient int ti = 10;
        static int si = 20;
    }
    
    public class TestClass
    {
        public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception
        {
         
            Boo boo = new Boo();
            boo.si++;
            System.out.println(boo.ti+" "+boo.si);
            var fos = new FileOutputStream("c:\\temp\\boo.ser");
            var os = new ObjectOutputStream(fos);
            os.writeObject(boo);
            os.close();
            
            var fis = new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\boo.ser");
            var is = new ObjectInputStream(fis);
            boo = (Boo) is.readObject();
            is.close();	        
            System.out.println(boo.ti+" "+boo.si);
        }
    }`

  1. It will not compile.
  2. It will throw an exception at run time.
  3. 10 21 10 21
  4. 10 21 10 20
  5. 10 21 0 20
  6. 10 21 0 21

_Explanation:_ Remember that transient fields and static fields are never serialized. Constructor, instance blocks, and field initialization of the class being deserialized are also not invoked. So, when boo is deserialized, the value of `ti` is set to 0. The class `Boo` is loaded as soon as the code refers to the class (here, it happens at `Boo boo = new Boo();` ), and so the static int `si` is initialized to the value given in the class code i.e. 20 and then it is incremented to 21 because of ` boo.si++;`. This part has nothing to do with serialization. So when you deserialize an instance of `Boo, Boo.si `is not affected and is not reset to 20. Therefore, if you run the program again with just the deserialization part, you will see that si is 20 and not 21.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 6

6  |  ID: 1702  |  Difficulty: Level 5
    
    
    What will be a part of the output when the following code is compiled and run?
    
    `class Boo {
        int boo = 10;
        public Boo(int k){ System.out.println("In Boo k = "+k); boo = k;}
    }
    
    class BooBoo extends Boo {
         public BooBoo(int k ){ super(k); System.out.println("In BooBoo k = "+k); }
    }
        
    class Moo extends BooBoo implements Serializable {
        int moo = 10;
        public Moo(){ super(5); System.out.println("In Moo"); }
    }
        
    public class TestClass {
        
        public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{
    	
            var moo = new Moo();
            var fos = new FileOutputStream("c:\\temp\\moo1.ser");
            var os = new ObjectOutputStream(fos);
            os.writeObject(moo);
            os.close();
            var fis = new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\moo1.ser");
            var is = new ObjectInputStream(fis);
            moo = (Moo) is.readObject();
            is.close();	
          }
    }`
    	

  1. `In Boo k = 5 In BooBoo k = 5 `
  2. `In Boo k = 0 In BooBoo k = 0 `
  3. `In Moo`
  4. It will throw an exception at runtime.
  5. It will not compile.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 3, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    While instantiating a new Moo, all the constructors will be executed. So it  will print 
    `In Boo k = 5
    In BooBoo k = 5
    In Moo`
    There will not be any problem while serializing the Moo object. 
    However, while deserializing, the JVM will not find any no-arg constructor that can be invoked to initialize `BooBoo`. No-args constructor is required in `BooBoo` because `BooBoo` is the most specific class in the class hierarchy that doesn't implement Serializable. So it will throw an `InvalidClassException`. 

7  |  ID: 1703  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Consider the following class:
    `public class Student implements Serializable{
        public static final long serialVersionUID = 1;
        public String name;
        public String grade;
        public String toString(){ return "["+name+", "+grade+"]"; }
    }`
    
    An object of this class was created as follows and was serialized to a file `c:\temp\bob.ser`:
    `        Student s = new Student();
            s.name = "bob";
            s.grade = "10";`
    After some time the Student class was changed as follows:
    `public class Student implements Serializable{
        public static final long serialVersionUID = 1;
        public String id="S111";
        public String name;
        public String grade;
        public int age=15;
        public String toString(){ return "["+id+", "+name+", "+grade+", "+age+"]"; }
    }`
    Now, the serialized file is read back as follows:
    `   FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\bob.ser");
       ObjectInputStream is = new ObjectInputStream(fis);
       s = (Student) is.readObject();
       is.close();	        
       System.out.println("Loaded "+s);`
    
    What will it print?
    

  1. It will throw an exception while deserializing the file because the `serialVersionUID` field must not be `public`.
  2. Loaded [null, bob, 10, 0]

_Explanation:_ Since the serialVersionUID of the serialized class and the new class are same, the file will be deserialized without any issue. The new fields will be initialized to their Java defaults (because constructors and initializers are not invoked during deserialization). So the values for id and and age will remain null and 0 respectively. 

  3. Loaded [S111, bob, 10, 15]
  4. It will have unpredicable values for id and age.
  5. It will throw an exception while serializing the file because the `serialVersionUID` field must not be `final`.

_Explanation:_ In fact, if a serializable class declares the `serialVersionUID` field, it must be `final` and must be of type `long`. It can have any access modifier but `private` is recommended.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    1. When a file is deserialized into an object, the class's constructor and instance initializers are not called. So the fields for which no value is available in the serialized file, are initialized to their default values (i.e. number fields to `0`, boolean to `false`, and references to `null`). Note that the fields of a non-serializable super class are initialized by the instance initializers and the no-args constructor of that super class.
    
    2. `serialVersionUID` denotes the version number of the class. If you don't specify `serialVersionUID` for a class that implements Serializable, Java compiler automatically adds this field. It computes a value based on the attributes of the class such as the fields and interfaces, and assigns that value to `serialVersionUID`.
    
    It is used during deserialization to verify that the sender and receiver of a serialized object have loaded classes for that object that are compatible with respect to serialization. If the receiver has loaded a class for the object that has a different `serialVersionUID` than that of the corresponding sender's class, then deserialization will result in an `InvalidClassException`.
    
    Note that `serialVersionUID` must be static, final, and of type long, i.e.,
    
    `ANY-ACCESS-MODIFIER static final long serialVersionUID = 42L;`
    
    Although it can have any access modifier, `private` is recommended.
    
    
    3. If the `serialVersionUID` for the serialized object and the actual class is same then the deserialization completes successfully but any additional fields that are not present in the serialized file are initialized to their default value (as per point 1. above).  Any fields that are missing in the class but are present in the serialized file are ignored.

8  |  ID: 3698  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following is/are valid record definition(s)?

  1. `record Student(int id, String... subjects){ @Override public int id(){ return 10; } @Override public boolean equals(Object obj){ return false; } }`
  2. `record Student(int id, String... subjects){ public void reset(){ id = 0; subjects = null; } }`

_Explanation:_ Record fields are final. So, any method that tries to mutate them will not compile. Here, the `reset` method is trying to mutate the `id` and `subjects` fields.

  3. `record Student(int id, String... subjects){ private String address; public void setAddress(String address){ this.address = address; } }`

_Explanation:_ A record cannot define an instance field explicitly. All instance fields of a record are created automatically by the compiler using the parameters specified in the header.

  4. `record Student(int id, String... subjects) implements java.io.Serializable{ public static final long serialVersionUID = 0L; }`

_Explanation:_ A record may have static fields and may implement any interface. Note that a serializable class can declare its own `serialVersionUID` explicitly by declaring a field named "serialVersionUID" that must be static, final, and of type long, i.e., `ANY-ACCESS-MODIFIER static final long serialVersionUID = 42L;` Although it can have any access modifier, `private` is recommended.

  5. `interface Person{ int id(); String name(); } record Student(int id, String... subjects) implements Person{ public String name(){ return "none"; } }`

_Explanation:_ Observe that both the methods of Person interface (i.e. `id()` and `name()`) are correctly implemented by `Student`. There is no explicitly defined `id()` method but this method is provided automatically by the compiler because of the `id` component defined in the `Student` record.

  6. `abstract record Student(int id, String... subjects) { }`

_Explanation:_ A record cannot be abstract, sealed, or non-sealed. It is always final.

  7. `sealed record Student(int id, String... subjects) { }`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4, 5

9  |  ID: 1403  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    How many methods have to be provided by a class that is not abstract and that says it implements `Serializable` interface?

  1. 0

_Explanation:_ Serializable interface does not declare any methods. That is why it is also called as a "marker" interface.

  2. 1
  3. 2
  4. 3



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Note that a serializable class can also declare its own `serialVersionUID` explicitly by declaring a field named "serialVersionUID" that must be static, final, and of type long, i.e.,
    
    `ANY-ACCESS-MODIFIER static final long serialVersionUID = 42L;`
    
    If a serializable class does not explicitly declare a serialVersionUID, then the serialization runtime will calculate a default serialVersionUID value for that class based on various aspects of the class, as described in the Java(TM) Object Serialization Specification. However, it is strongly recommended that all serializable classes explicitly declare serialVersionUID values, since the default serialVersionUID computation is highly sensitive to class details that may vary depending on compiler implementations, and can thus result in unexpected InvalidClassExceptions during deserialization. Therefore, to guarantee a consistent serialVersionUID value across different java compiler implementations, a serializable class must declare an explicit serialVersionUID value. It is also strongly advised that explicit serialVersionUID declarations use the private modifier where possible

10  |  ID: 4044  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Given:
    `class Data implements Serializable{
       int id = 20;  
       static LocalDate dummyDate = LocalDate.of(2000, 01, 01);
       transient LocalDate ld = LocalDate.of(2010, 01, 01);
    
       private void readObject(ObjectInputStream stream) 
             throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException {
             System.out.println("in readObject before " +id+" "+ld);
             stream.defaultReadObject(); 
             System.out.println("in readObject after " + id+" "+ld);
             ld = dummyDate;
       }
       public Data(int id, LocalDate ld){
           this.id = id; this.ld = ld;
       }
       public String toString(){
           return "Data "+id+" "+ld;
       }
    }
    public class TestClass {
        public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException{
            
            Data d = new Data(10, LocalDate.of(2024, 10, 10));
            ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream( new FileOutputStream("c:\\temp\\data.ser"));
            oos.writeObject(d); oos.close();
            
            ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream( new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\data.ser"));
            d = (Data) ois.readObject();
            System.out.println(d);
        }
    }
```java
    
    What will be the output?

  1. `in readObject before 0 null in readObject after 10 2010-01-01 Data 10 2000-01-01`
  2. `in readObject before 0 2010-01-01 in readObject after 10 2010-01-01 Data 10 2000-01-01`
  3. `in readObject before 0 null in readObject after 10 null Data 10 2000-01-01`
  4. `Data 10 2010-01-01`
  5. `Data 10 null`
  6. `in readObject before 20 null in readObject after 10 null Data 10 2000-01-01`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    This example shows how you may customize the serialization/deserialization of a class when you want to set the value of a field using some other business logic instead of from the serialized stream. 
    
    1. When you serialize an object of the `Data` class, the data members (i.e. their non-transient instance fields) of `Data` will be serialized, which means their values will be persisted in the serialized stream. 
    
    2. When serialized data is deserialized into an object, the serialization framework creates an object of the class present in the serialized data. However, the class's constructor and instance initializers are not called. It sets the values of those fields from the serialized data. These values are set when the `stream.defaultReadObject()` is invoked, which means, before this method is invoked, you will see default values for instance fields of the object (i.e. number fields will be `0`, boolean fields will be `false`, and reference fields will be set to `null`). After the call to `stream.defaultReadObject()` is complete, you will see their values set to the values present in the serialized data.
    
    (Note that the fields of a non-serializable super class are initialized by the instance initializers and the no-args constructor of that super class. )
    
    

11  |  ID: 3795  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    What will be the output when the following code is compiled and run?
```
    class Person implements Serializable{
        private String name;
        Person(String name){
            this.name = name;
            System.out.print("Person ");
        }
        public String toString(){ return name; }
    }
    
    class Student extends Person{
        public String school;
        public Student(String name, String school){
            super(name);
            this.school = school;
            System.out.print("Student ");
        }
        public String toString(){
            return super.toString()+" "+school;
        }
    }
    public class SerialTest {
        public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
            Person p = new Student("Bob Dylan", "NYU");
            try(
                    ObjectOutputStream oos = new ObjectOutputStream(
                                 new FileOutputStream("c:\\temp\\student.ser"));
    
                    ObjectInputStream ois = new ObjectInputStream(
                                  new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\student.ser"));
                    ){
                oos.writeObject(p);
                oos.flush();
                System.out.println((Person)ois.readObject());
            }
        }
    }`

  1. `Person Student null NYU`
  2. `Person Student Student Person Bob Dylan NYU`
  3. `Student Person Person Student Bob Dylan NYU`
  4. `Person Student Person Student null null`
  5. `Person Student Bob Dylan NYU`
  6. `Person Student null null`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    1. Once a class implements `Serializable`, that class and its subclasses become serializable. In the given code, `Person` implements `Serializable` and so, `Person` as well as its subclass `Student` are serializable.
    
    2. When you serialize an object of the `Student` class, the data members (i.e. their non-transient instance fields) of `Student` and `Person` will be serialized, which means their values will be persisted in the serialized stream. 
    
    3. When reading the object back (i.e. deserializing), the constructors of serializable classes are not invoked. Their data members are set directly from the values present in serialized data. Thus, in the given code, the constructors of `Student` and `Person` will not be invoked but their instance fields `name` and `school` will be set to `"Bob Dylan"` and `"NYU"` respectively by the deserialization mechanism provided by the `readObject` method.  (Not relevant for this question, but the constructors for unserializable super classes will be invoked first. Thus, the constructor of the `Object` class, which is the super class of `Person`, will be invoked.)
    
    The rest of the question involves the concepts of polymorphism and the identyfing the chain of constructor invocation while instantiating a class. Since `Student` is-a Person, a `Student` object can be assigned to a variable of type `Person` without any cast.
    
    The call to `new Student("Bob Dylan", "NYU")` causes the constructor of the `Student` class to be executed. This requires the constructor of `Person` to be executed first, which outputs `Person `. The call then returns to the constructor of `Student`, which outputs `Student `.
    
    When you serialize this `Student` object, the values of the `name` and the `school` fields are stored in the serialized file.
    
    Now, when you read the serialized object back, you get a `Student` object (because that what you serialized), whose `name` and `school` fields are set to the same values as before. When you print the `Student` object, its `toString` is invoked, which prints  `Bob Dylan NYU`. 
    
    Thus, the final output is `Person Student Bob Dylan NYU`.
    
    

12  |  ID: 3508  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given:
    
    `public class Data implements Serializable{
       int id;  
    
       transient LocalDate ld;  
    
       private void readObject(ObjectInputStream stream) 
             throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException {
    
             stream.defaultReadObject(); 
          
             ld = LocalDate.of(2020,1,1);
       }
    }
```java
    Identify the correct statements about the above code.

  1. This class does not perform serialization/deserialization correctly because it does not implement `writeObject` method.

_Explanation:_ It is not necessary for a class to implement `readObject` and `writeObject` methods. It may implement neither, either, or both the methods depending on what aspect of serialization it wants to customize. But note that, if provided, the `readObject` and `writeObject` methods must be private.

  2. The `readObject` method will be invoked after a Data object has been deserialized.
  3. The `readObject` method will be invoked during the deserialization of a Data object.

_Explanation:_ When you try to read an object from an `ObjectInputStream` using `ObjectInputStream`'s `readObject` method, it will check whether the class that is being read implements `readObject` method. If it does, then that class's `readObject` method will be invoked. The class can have custom deserialization logic in this method. In the given code, the `readObject` method invokes `stream.defaultReadObject()`, which reads whatever state was present in the input stream into this `Data` object. Next, it sets the `ld` object to a particular date.

  4. The `readObject` method will never be invoked.
  5. The `readObject` method will be invoked after a Data object is serialized.
  6. The `readObject` method will be invoked before a Data object is serialized.
  7. Deserialization will fail because the `readObject` method is not public.

_Explanation:_ In fact, the `readObject` and `writeObject` methods must be private.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

13  |  ID: 3513  |  Difficulty: Level 5
    
    
    What will the following code print when compiled and run?
```
    import java.io.Serializable;
    class Booby{
        int i; public Booby(){ i = 10; System.out.print("Booby"); }
    }
    class Dooby extends Booby implements Serializable {
        int j; public Dooby(){ j = 20; System.out.print("Dooby"); }
    }
    class Tooby extends Dooby{
        int k; public Tooby(){ k = 30; System.out.print("Tooby"); }
    }
    public class TestClass {
      public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{
        
        var t = new Tooby();
        t.i = 100;
        var oos  = new ObjectOutputStream(new FileOutputStream("c:\\temp\\test.ser"));
        oos.writeObject(t); oos.close();
        
        var ois = new ObjectInputStream(new FileInputStream("c:\\temp\\test.ser"));
        t = (Tooby) ois.readObject();ois.close();
        
        System.out.println(t.i+" "+t.j+" "+t.k);
      }
    }
```java

  1. `Booby Dooby Tooby 100 20 30`
  2. `Booby Dooby Tooby Booby Dooby Tooby 10 20 30`
  3. `Booby Dooby Tooby Booby 10 20 30`
  4. `Booby Dooby Tooby Booby 0 20 30`
  5. `Booby Dooby Tooby Booby 100 20 30`
  6. `Booby Dooby Tooby Booby Dooby Tooby 100 20 30`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Objects of a class that is not marked `Serializable` cannot be serialized. In this question, class `Booby` does not implement `Serializable` and so, its objects cannot be serialized. Class `Dooby` implements `Serializable` and since `Tooby` extends `Dooby`, it is Serializable as well.
    
    Now, when you serialize an object of class `Tooby`, only the data members of `Dooby` and `Tooby` will be serialized. Data members of `Booby` will not be serialized. Thus, the value of `i` (which is `100`) at the time of serialization will not be saved in the file.
    
    When reading the object back (i.e. deserializing), the constructors of serializable classes are not called. Their data members are set directly from the values present in serialized data. Constructor for unserializable classes is called. Thus, in this case, constructors of `Tooby` and `Dooby` are not called but the constructor of `Booby` is called. Therefore, `i` is set in the constructor to `10` and `j` and `k` are set using the data from the file to `20` and `30` respectively.
    

14  |  ID: 3530  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code:
```
    class Bond
    {
        String ticker; double coupon; java.time.LocalDate maturity;
    }
    
    class Portfolio implements Serializable
    {
        String accountName;
        Bond[] bonds;
    }
    
    public class TestClass {
      public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception{
         Portfolio portfolio = // get portfolio somehow 
         // serialize portfolio
      }
    }`
    
    Which of the following approaches can be taken independent of each other so that a Portfolio object can be serialized while preserving the state of the Bond objects contained in Portfolio?
    

  1. It can be serialized as it is without any modification.
  2. Just have `Bond` class implement `Serializable`.
  3. Just make '`bonds`' field in `Portfolio` transient.

_Explanation:_ Making it transient will leave 'bonds' unserialized. So, its state will be lost when Portfolio is serialized.

  4. Change the type of `bonds` from `Bond[]` to `ArrayList<Bond> bonds;`

_Explanation:_ It will not have any bearing on the fact that Bond is not Serializable.

  5. Make `bonds` array transient in `Portfolio` and implement `readObject(ObjectInputStream os)` and `writeObject(ObjectOutputStream os)` methods to read and write the state of Bond objects explicitly.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 5

**Explanation:**  
If for any reason, you want to serialize Portfolio objects without making Bond class Serializable, you can customize the serialization of a Portfolio class by implementing readObject and writeObject methods as shown below: 
    
    
    
    class Bond  // does not implement Serializable
    {
        String ticker = "bac"; double coupon = 8.3; java.time.LocalDate maturity = new Date();
    }
    
    class Portfolio implements Serializable
    {
        String accountName;
        transient Bond[] bonds = new Bond[]{ }; // must be transient
        //because Bond class does not implement Serializable
        
        private void writeObject(ObjectOutputStream os)  //must be private
           throws Exception{
    	os.defaultWriteObject();
    	os.writeInt(bonds.length);
    	//write the state of bond objects
    	for(int i=0; i<bonds.length; i++) {
    		os.writeObject(bonds[i].ticker); 
    		os.writeDouble(bonds[i].coupon);
    		os.writeObject(bonds[i].maturity);
    	}
        }
    
        private void readObject(ObjectInputStream os)  //must be private
             throws Exception{
    	os.defaultReadObject();
    	int n = os.readInt();
    	this.bonds = new Bond[n];
    	//read the state of bond objects.
    	for(int i=0; i<bonds.length; i++) {
    		bonds[i] = new Bond();
    		bonds[i].ticker = (String) os.readObject();	
    		bonds[i].coupon = os.readDouble();
    		bonds[i].maturity = (java.util.Date) os.readObject();
    	}
    	
        }    
    }

## 📖 08 - Modules - Basics

48 questions

1  |  ID: 3031  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following command line options can be used to find out all the dependencies of a module while execution?
    
    Note: Although identifying module dependency is not explicitly mentioned in the exam objectives, we have seen questions on the exam that require you to know about this.

  1. \--show-module-resolution

_Explanation:_ This option shows all the dependencies of a module and how they are resolved while execution of a class. For example, lets say you have a `moduleA` that requires `moduleB` and `moduleB` requires `moduleC`. Now, if you run the following command: `java --module-path out --show-module-resolution --module moduleA/test.A` the following output is produced: `root moduleA file://C:/test/java/out/moduleA/ moduleA requires moduleB file://C:/test/java/out/moduleB/ moduleB requires moduleC file://C:/test/java/out/moduleC/ java.base binds java.xml.crypto jrt:/java.xml.crypto ... ... java.rmi requires java.logging jrt:/java.logging test.A ` Observe the top three lines of the output. They show how `moduleA, moduleB,` and `moduleC` are resolved.

  2. \--show-module-dependencies

_Explanation:_ There is no such option.

  3. \--list-dependencies

_Explanation:_ There is no such option.

  4. \--list-deps

_Explanation:_ There is no such option.

  5. \--describe

_Explanation:_ There is no such option in java command. But it is there in jmod, where it prints module details for a jmod file. For example: `jmod describe jmods/ma.jmod` Observe that there are no hyphens (or dashes or minus sign) before describe. 

  6. \--describe-module

_Explanation:_ The `--describe-module` option shows the information given in the module-info of a particular module. It does not traverse the dependency graph. For example, if `moduleA` requires `moduleB` and `moduleB` requires `moduleC`, then the command `java --describe-module moduleA` will only show `moduleB`.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

    Given the following command:
```java
    javac --module-source-path c:\java\a -d c:\java\b -p  c:\java\c -m x.y`
    
    Which of the following statements are correct?

  1. A directory named `out` will be created under `c:\java\b`
  2. A directory named `x/y` will be created under `c:\java\b`
  3. A directory named `x.y` will be created under `c:\java\c`
  4. Class files will be created under `c:\java\b\x.y`
  5. Class files will be created under `c:\java\a`
  6. This command is not valid because `-classpath` option is missing.

_Explanation:_ `-classpath` is not a required option. It is used only when you want to make non-modular classes accessible to other non modular classes.

3  |  ID: 3032  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which of the following commands can be used to identify class and module dependencies of a class named `test.A` of module named `moduleA` without executing it?
    
    Assume that all module files are stored in `out` directory.
    Note: Although identifying module dependency is not explicitly mentioned in the exam objectives, we have seen questions on the exam that require you to know about the tool that is used to identify module dependencies.

  1. `javac --module-path out --list-dependencies moduleA/test.A`
  2. `jmod --module-path out --describe test.A`

_Explanation:_ The jmod tool is used to package module files into jmod archives. You don't need to know about jmod tool for this exam but you may see it as an option. For example, ` jmod describe modfile.jmod` will print out the modules used by `modfile.jmod` (Observe that there is no - sign before describe)

  3. `javap --module-path out --show-module-resolution test.A`

_Explanation:_ The javap tool is used to inspect a class file to see its fields and methods. You don't need to know javap tool for this exam.

  4. `jdeps --module-path out out\moduleA\test\A.class`

_Explanation:_ The jdeps tool is used to find out all dependencies of a class file or a jar file. It inspects the given class file (or all class files inside a jar files) and finds out all the required modules and packages that are referred to by this class or jar file. You can add module jars and other jars in its search path using `--module-path` and `--classpath` options. 

  5. `jdeps --module-path out moduleA\test.A`

_Explanation:_ Remember that jdeps needs the path to the file that you want to inspect. `moduleA\test.A` is not the path to the file `A.class`. `out\moduleA\test\A.class` or `c:\test\java\out\moduleB.jar` are the kind of paths that can be used for `jdeps` tool.

4  |  ID: 3028  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    Given that your module named `foo.bar` contains the following two source files: `src/foo.bar/f/b/Baz1.java` and `src/foo.bar/f/c/Caz1.java`.
    
    Which of the following options can be used to compile this module?
    
    (Assume that all directory paths are relative to the current directory.)

  1. `javac --module-source-path src src/*/*.java`
  2. `javac --module-source-path src -d out src/foo.bar/*/*.java`

_Explanation:_ You can use `*.java` for compiling all the files in a directory. However, writing `*` for a directory is incorrect. For example, the following is correct: `javac --module-source-path src -d out src/foo.bar/f/b/*.java src/foo.bar/f/c/*.java`/ 

  3. `javac --module-source-path src -d out src/foo.bar`

_Explanation:_ You must either specify the files that you want to compile or the module that you want to compile. For compiling specific files, you have to simply list the files (without any option) and for compiling a module (i.e. all the files of a module), you have to use the --module (or -m) option. For example, `javac --module-source-path src -d out src/foo.bar/f/b/Baz1.java src/foo.bar/f/c/Caz1.java ` or `javac --module-source-path src -d out --module foo.bar `

  4. `javac --module-source-path src -d out --module foo.bar`

_Explanation:_ By using `--module` option, you are telling the compiler that you want to compile `foo.bar` module. It will, therefore, look for `foo.bar` directory under `src` and compile all the java files stored anywhere under `src/foo.bar` directory.

  5. `javac --module-source-path src --module foo.bar`

_Explanation:_ javac requires you to specify the output directory using the -d option when you use module specific options such as `--module-source-path` or `--module` for compilation.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 6_module_compilation_options**

6  |  ID: 3029  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    You are trying to compile your module named `mycompany.finance`. You have put all the source files for your module in `src` directory. However, your module requires a `mycompany.utils` module, which is delivered by another team of your company in the form of `mycompany.utils.jar` file. You have kept this jar in `libs` directory.
    
    Which of the following commands can be used to compile your module?

  1. `javac --module-source-path src -classpath libs/mycompany.utils.jar --module src`
  2. `javac --module-source-path src -p libs/mycompany.utils.jar -d out -m mycompany.finance`

_Explanation:_ 1\. `-p` is a short form for `--module-path` and is used to specify the location of modules required by the module that you are trying to compile. If a module is packaged in a jar file, then you must specify the path of the jar file (or its parent directory). If the required module is in exploded format, you can specify the parent directory of the module's directory. 2\. `-m` is a short form for `--module` and is used to specify the module name of the module that you want to compile.

  3. `javac -source-path src -classpath libs -d out --module mycompany.finance`

_Explanation:_ `-sourcepath` and `-classpath` options are used when you want to compile non-modular code. If you want to compile a module, then `--module-source-path` and `-d` options are required.

  4. `javac --module-source-path src -m libs -d out -s mycompany.finance`
  5. `javac -s src -p libs -d out -m mycompany.finance`

_Explanation:_ `-s` is not a valid option.

7  |  ID: 3024  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    You have created a module named `mycompany.smartschool` packaged in `smartschool.jar`. This module uses a third party non-modular java library packaged as `utils.jar`. 
    
    How will you execute your module's main class named `mycompany.smartschool.Main`?
    
    (Assume that the jar files are located in the current directory.)

  1. `java --module-path smartschool.jar;utils.jar \--module mycompany.smartschool/mycompany.smartschool.Main`

_Explanation:_ Remember that a module cannot access non-modular classes from the classpath. You must convert non-modular third party jar to an automatic module by putting that jar on module-path. (You must have already added an appropriate requires clause in your module-info while compilation of your module). Other options of the command remain the same i.e. `--module-path` (or `-p`) for specifying the location of module jars and `--module` (or `-m`) for specifying the module and class that you want to run.

  2. `java -classpath utils.jar --module-path smartschool.jar \--module mycompany.smartschool/mycompany.smartschool.Main`
  3. `java -cp utils.jar;smartschool.jar --module mycompany.smartschool/mycompany.smartschool.Main`
  4. `java -classpath utils.jar --module-jar smartschool.jar -main-class mycompany.smartschool/mycompany.smartschool.Main`

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 7_module_execution_options**

8  |  ID: 3023  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Given that a class named `com.xyz.fx.Main` is part of a module named `xyz.fx` packaged in `fx.jar`, which of the following commands can be used to execute this class?
    
    (Assume that the jar file is located in the current directory.)

  1. `java -classpath fx.jar --m xyz.fx/com.xyz.fx.Main`

_Explanation:_ 1\. Instead of -classpath, --module-path should be used. 2\. There should be a single dash for the m switch i.e. -m instead of --m. There is a double dash for module switch i.e. --module.

  2. `java --p fx.jar --m xyz.fx/com.xyz.fx.Main`
  3. `java -p fx.jar --module xyz.fx/com.xyz.fx.Main`

_Explanation:_ `-p` is the short form for `--module-path` `-m` is the short form for `--module` 

  4. `java --module-path fx.jar --module com.xyz.fx.Main`

_Explanation:_ You must specify the module name in which the class exists also in --module option along with the class name. Module name and class name are separated by /.

  5. `java -classpath fx.jar com.xyz.fx.Main`

_Explanation:_ Structure of a module jar file is same as the regular jar file. That is why it is possible to treat the jar file of a module as a regular jar file containing classes and run a class that is present in the jar.

9  |  ID: 3026  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about following command:
    
    `java -p .\dir1 -cp .\dir2 -m a.b/a.b.c.Main`
    

  1. `dir1` should have a directory named `a.b` or a jar file named `a.b.jar`.

_Explanation:_ Since the value given in `-p` option is a directory, that means, the module could be present in `dir1` in exploded format also instead of a jar file. Even if the module is packaged in a jar file, the name of the jar file need not necessarily be `a.b.jar`. It can be anything.

  2. If classes from a third party non-modular jar are required by `Main`, then the third party jar file should be present in `dir2` directory.

_Explanation:_ If a module requires a third party non-modular jar, then that third party jar file must be added to the module-path so that it can be assumed to be an automatic module. Further, the module must also add an appropriate requires clause in its module-info. 

  3. If the `a.b` module requires any other module, then that module's jar file must be present in `dir1` directory.

_Explanation:_ It is not necessary to have a jar file of the module. In this case, dir1 may contain the module in the exploded format also. 

  4. `Main.class` should be present in `dir1\a.b\a\b\c` directory or, if it is inside a jar, then its path should be \a\b\c.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    In the given command, classes present on the classpath (i.e. under `.\dir2`) can be accessed only by an automatic module. For example, if the module `a.b` uses an automatic module then that automatic module may access classes stored under `.\dir2`. Classes from `a.b` module cannot access classes on the classpath.

  4. `java -classpath .;abc.utils.jar Main`

14  |  ID: 3017  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the module system of Java.

  1. Every module must reside in a directory (or a jar) of its own.

_Explanation:_ You cannot have more than one module in a directory. Although you may package a module into a jar, put multiple such module jars in a directory, and put that directory (which contains multiple module jars) on the module path.

  2. A module can specify packages as well as services.

_Explanation:_ Packages are exported and used using the `exports` and `requires` clauses respectively, while services are exported and used using the `provides` and `uses` clauses respectively.

  3. Modular JDK is helpful in improving performance of an application.

_Explanation:_ The JVM uses various optimization techniques to improve application performance. JSR 376 indicates that these techniques are more effective when it is known in advance that required types are located only in specific modules.

  4. Modules allow service implementations to be hooked up with service users through dependency injection.

_Explanation:_ Java modules do allow a service consumer to find a service implementation, however, it is not done using dependecy injection. A service consumer has to loop through each available service implementation and pick the one it needs.

  5. A module can export sealed classes to other modules for extension.

_Explanation:_ All subclasses of a sealed class must belong to the same module (or to the same package if the sealed class is not a part of a named module). So, even if a sealed class is visible outside a module, it cannot be extended by classes of other modules.

  6. Modules allow a JImage to be produced to run on multiple platforms.

_Explanation:_ A JImage is nothing but a customized JRE and a JRE is specific to a platform. So, a JImage cannot be used to run on multiple platforms.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Please go through Oracle's blog on Modules to get a general idea about modules: https://blogs.oracle.com/java/modular-development
    
    A JImage is a customized JRE that containts just those parts of a standard JRE that are required for a particular application to to run. Removing unwanted parts of the standard JRE reduces the files size of the resulting JRE. Such a custom JRE image plus the application jar files are packaged together in a .jimage file. A JImage file can be created for a modular or a non-modular app.

15  |  ID: 3016  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    You are creating a `abc.payroll` module that makes its two classes -  `com.abc.payroll.Hourly` and `com.abc.payroll.Yearly` - available to all other modules for use.
    
    Which of the following files correctly defines this module?

  1. `module abc.payroll{ exports com.abc.payroll.*; } `

_Explanation:_ It is not possible to export classes in a module. You can only export a package in an exports clause.

  2. `module abc.payroll{ exports com.abc.payroll; } `

_Explanation:_ Since both the classes belong to `com.abc.payroll` package, exporting this package is the only option.

  3. `module abc.payroll{ exports com.abc.payroll to all; } `

_Explanation:_ The "exports - to" clause is used to define a qualified export, which means you want to make a package accessible only to those particular modules mentioned after the to clause. But "all" is not valid here. You must specify a module name for a qualified export. For example, if you want to make this package accessible only to `com.abc.internal1` and `com.abc.internal2`modules, then you would write - `exports com.abc.payroll to com.abc.internal1, com.abc.internal2;` The modules specified in the `to` clause are known to as "friends" of the current module. 

  4. `module abc.payroll{ exports com.abc.payroll.Hourly, com.abc.payroll.Yearly; } `

_Explanation:_ You can have only one package per exports clause.

  5. `module abc.payroll{ exports com.abc.payroll.Hourly; exports com.abc.payroll.Yearly; } `



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

17  |  ID: 3018  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the modular system of Java.

  1. A module can allow other modules to access all its types and members of its type through reflection.

_Explanation:_ This can be done using the `open`, `opens`, and `opens...to` clauses.

  2. By default, a module exhibits strong encapsulation by preventing reflective access to it types.

_Explanation:_ Before Java 9, reflection could be used to learn about all types in a package and all members of a type—even its private members—whether you wanted to allow this capability or not. Thus, nothing was truly encapsulated. This is not so with modules. By default, no such access is allowed. 

  3. A module can be used by non-modular code by putting that module on the classpath.
  4. An application can either use module-path or classpath but not both.
  5. A sealed class belonging to a module may list classes belonging to other modules in its permits clause.

_Explanation:_ Normally, the direct subclasses of a seal class must reside in the same package as the package of the sealed class. However, if a sealed class belongs to a named module, it is allowed to list classes from different packages of the same module in its permits clause.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 3

18  |  ID: 3013  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    You are creating an `acme.db` module. This module contains a `com.acme.DB` class that will be used by other modules of your application. Which of the following files correctly defines the acme.db module?

  1. `module acme.db{ exports com.acme/DB; } `
  2. `module acme.db{ exports com.acme.*; } `

_Explanation:_ There cannot be any * in exports or requires clause.

  3. `module acme.db{ public com.acme; } `
  4. `module acme.db{ exports com.acme.DB; } `

_Explanation:_ 1\. Only the package name in which the required class exists needs to be mentioned in the exports clause. 

  5. `module acme.db{ exports com.acme; } `

_Explanation:_ Remember that "packages are exported" and "modules are required". A module exports packages to other modules. It cannot export individual classes. On the other hand, a module cannot specify a particular package or a class in the requires clause. It can only specify the name of the module in which the required package exists. 

  6. `module acme.db{ exports com.acme to *; } `

_Explanation:_ The `exports-to` clause exports a package to a specific list of modules. For example, if you have : ` exports com.acme.db to com.acme.dto, com.acme.service; ` then only the classes in `com.acme.dto` and `com.acme.service` modules will be allowed to access the classes in package `com.acme.db`. The modules after the `to` clause have to be listed explicitly. You can't use * to mean all modules. If you want to expose a package to all modules, just leave the `to` clause.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

19  |  ID: 3012  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    You are creating an `inventory` module. This module contains a `com.inventory.Queries` class that uses a `com.acme.DB` class. This `com.acme.DB` class is contained in `acme.db` module. Which of the following files correctly defines the inventory module?

  1. `module inventory{ requires com.acme.db.*; } `

_Explanation:_ 1\. Only the module name in which the required class exists needs to be mentioned in the requires clause. 2\. There shouldn't be any * in a requires or an exports clause.

  2. `module inventory{ requires acme.db; } `

_Explanation:_ Remember that "packages are exported" and "modules are required". A module cannot specify a particular package or a class in the requires clause. It can only specify the name of the module in which the required package exists. On the other hand, a module exports packages to other modules. It cannot export individual classes.

  3. `module inventory{ requires acme.db/com.acme.DB; } `
  4. `module inventory{ uses com.acme.DB; } `

_Explanation:_ `uses` clause is a not valid here. It must be `requires`. A `uses` clause is used for declaring dependence on a service, while a `requires` clause is used to declare dependence on a module.

  5. `module inventory{ requires com.acme.DB; } `
  6. `module inventory{ requires com.acme.DB transitively; } `

_Explanation:_ It should be `requires transitive acme.db;` because you can only specify a module in the requires clause (and not an individual class or a package).




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The `requires-transitive` clause means that if any module that requires this module will also automatically require (hence, will be able to read, without explicitly requiring it) the module mentioned in the requires transitive clause. For example, if you have :
```
    module com.amazing.app{
       requires transitive com.amazing.customer;//4
    }
```java
    
    then any module that contains `requires com.amazing.app;` clause will automatically be able to read the `com.amazing.customer` module without any additional `requires` clause.
    
    The `exports-to` clause exports a package to a specific list of modules. For example, if you have :
    
    `exports com.acme.db to com.acme.dto, com.acme.service;
```
    then only the classes in `com.acme.dto` and `com.acme.service` modules will be allowed to access the classes in package `com.acme.db`.
    
    The modules after the `to` clause have to be listed explicitly. You can't use * to mean all modules. If you want to expose a package to all modules, just leave the `to` clause.
    
    

20  |  ID: 3015  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    You are creating an `enthu.questionbank` module that depends on `enthu.internal.db` and `enthu.external.feeds` modules. Which of the following files correctly defines this module?

  1. ` //In file module-info.java: module enthu.questionbank{ requires enthu.internal.db, enthu.external.feeds; } `

_Explanation:_ Each required module must be specified on a separate requires clause.

  2. ` //In file module-info.java: module enthu.questionbank{ uses enthu.internal.db, enthu.external.feeds; } `

_Explanation:_ `uses` clause is a not valid here. It must be `requires`. A `uses` clause is used for declaring dependence on a service, while a `requires` clause is used to declare dependence on a module.

  3. ` //In file module-info.java: module enthu.questionbank{ import enthu.internal.db; import enthu.external.feeds; } `

_Explanation:_ "import" is a not a valid clause. It must be "requires".

  4. ` //In file module-info.java: module enthu.questionbank{ require enthu.internal.db; require enthu.external.feeds; } `

_Explanation:_ It must be "requires" not "require".

  5. None of the above are valid.

_Explanation:_ The correct definition would be: ` //In file module-info.java: module enthu.questionbank{ requires enthu.internal.db; requires enthu.external.feeds; } `




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

21  |  ID: 3014  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given the following contents of `module-info.java`, 
```java
    module enthu.finance{
      exports com.enthu.Reports;
      requires enthu.utils;
    } `
    
    Select correct statements.

  1. Module name is `finance`.

_Explanation:_ Module name is `enthu.finance`.

  2. `com.enthu.Reports` is the name of the class that this module exports.

_Explanation:_ Remember that only packages can be exported, not individual classes. Therefore, `com.enthu.Reports` is would be the name of the package that this module exports.

  3. This module depends on `enthu.utils` package.

_Explanation:_ Remember that dependency is always on modules, not on individual classes or packages. Therefore, `enthu.utils` must be a module name.

  4. This file must be present in `enthu.finance` directory if the source code is to be compiled using the `--module-source-path` option.
  5. Other modules that depend on this module will be able to access `com.enthu.Reports` package as well as `enthu.utils` package.

_Explanation:_ Only the packages mentioned in exports clauses of the module-info are made accessible to other modules.

22  |  ID: 3011  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which of the following are valid module definitions?

  1. ` //In file module.java: module autos{ } `

_Explanation:_ File name of the file that contains module definition must be `module-info.java`.

  2. ` //In file autos.java: module autos{ } `
  3. ` //In file module-info.java: module autos{ } `
  4. ` //In file module-info.java: module cars{ exports com.car; } module trucks{ requires cars; } `

_Explanation:_ There can be only one module definition in a module-info.java file because a module must reside in a directory with the same name as the module name.

  5. ` //In file module-info.java: module-info autos{ } `

_Explanation:_ Module definition must start with `module` keyword, not `module-info`.

  6. ` //In file module-info.java: sealed module autos{ } `

_Explanation:_ `sealed` and `non-sealed` keywords are applicable only to classes and interfaces, not to modules.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

23  |  ID: 3010  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    You are creating an `acme.accounting` module that depends on `acme.math` module and makes its `com.acme.accounting` package available to all other modules. Which of the following files correctly defines this module?

  1. ` //In file module.java: module acme.accounting{ requires acme.math; exports com.acme.accounting; } `

_Explanation:_ File name of the file that contains module definition must be `module-info.java`.

  2. ` //In file module.java: module acme.accounting{ requires acme.math.*; exports com.acme.accounting.*; } `
  3. ` //In file module.java: module acme.accounting{ requires acme.math; exports com.acme.accounting.*; } `
  4. ` //In file module-info.java: module-info acme.accounting{ requires acme.math; exports com.acme.accounting; } `

_Explanation:_ Module definition must start with `module` keyword, not `module-info`.

  5. ` //In file module-info.java: module acme.accounting{ requires acme.math; exports com.acme.accounting.*; } `

_Explanation:_ The exports clause exports a package and not individual classes of a package. All public classes of the mentioned package are exported. Therefore, only the complete package name needs to be mentioned in the exports clause. There shouldn't be any *. 

  6. ` //In file module-info.java: module acme.accounting{ requires acme.math; exports com.acme.accounting; } `



✓ Correct Answer(s): 6

24  |  ID: 3006  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the modular JDK.

  1. Modular JDK allows modular as well as non-modular applications to co-exist.
  2. Modular JDK facilitates aggressive API deprecation.

_Explanation:_ Although Java 9 has enhanced `@Deprecated` annotation to better manage API deprecation, it has nothing to do with modularization.

  3. With modular JDK it is possible to assemble and optimize a set of modules and their dependencies into a custom runtime image.

_Explanation:_ This is done using the jlink tool, which was introduced in JDK 9. It can generate a custom Java runtime that only contains the modules that are required for a given application. jlink enables developers to move away from a centralized Java runtime, with all the associated issues of ensuring the correct version is installed, to one runtime per application (or service if you are using a microservices architecture).

  4. Modular JDK allows a module to export a package only to a specific module as well.

_Explanation:_ This is done using qualified exports. For example, in the following module-info, module A exports the internal.utils package only to module B: ` module A { exports internal.utils to B; }`

  5. The foundational APIs of the Java SE platform are in java.lang module.

_Explanation:_ They are in `java.base` module. This module is always read by all other modules and therefore, there no need to add "`requires java.base;`" clause in any module-info. This module exports a lot of packages. Important ones are: `java.io, java.lang, java.math`, `java.net, java.nio, java.security`, `java.time, java.util`, `java.util.concurrent, `and` java.util.function`. The full list can be seen here: http://cr.openjdk.java.net/~mr/jigsaw/ea/module-summary.html Note that "fundamental APIs" and "core packages" mean the same thing. You may see either of the phrases in the exam.

  6. Only a modular application can be packaged as a jimage.

_Explanation:_ Creation of a JImage does not require an application to be modular.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 3, 4

25  |  ID: 3005  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which of the following statements are correct regarding the module system of Java.

  1. Although not recommended, it is possible to customize what packages a module exports from the command line.
  2. An advantage of java modules functionality is that users of a module cannot alter the list of exported packages of that module.
  3. If a module does not export a package, there is no way to export that package to all other modules from the command line.

_Explanation:_ It can still be exported using `--add-exports` command line option.

  4. If a module wants to read another module but only temporarily, it can request such access using command line options.
  5. A module can request access to another module temporarily using command line options but only if the target module already exports that package in its module-info.

_Explanation:_ Even packages that have not been exported in module-info can be made accessible using `--add-exports` command line option.

  6. Modules allows a sealed class and its direct subtypes to be members of different packages.

_Explanation:_ Normally, the direct subclasses of a seal class must reside in the same package as the package of the sealed class. However, if a sealed class belongs to a named module, it is allowed to list classes from different packages of the same module in its permits clause.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    There are three command line options applicable to `javac` and `java` that can be used for customizing exports and requires configurations of modules temporarily (temporarily means only for that particular command execution). These are: `add-reads`, `add-exports`, and, `add-opens`. For example, if you want `moduleA` to be able to read public packages of `moduleB` and neither of the modules have appropriate information in their respective module-info files, then you can use the following commands to enable such access :
    
    `javac --add-reads moduleA=moduleB --add-exports moduleB/com.modB.package1=moduleA ...
    java --add-reads moduleA=moduleB --add-exports moduleB/com.modB.package1=moduleA ...
```
    `--add-reads moduleA=moduleB` implies that `moduleA` wants to read all exported packages of `moduleB`.
    `--add-exports moduleB/com.modB.package1=moduleA` implies that `moduleB` exports package `com.modB.package1` to `moduleA`. 
    
    `--add-opens` is used to provide access to private members of classes through reflection but is not required for 1Z0-829 exam.

26  |  ID: 3008  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements regarding Java module system.

  1. The file in which module information is specified must be named `moduleinfo.java`.

_Explanation:_ The name of the file must be `module-info.java`.

  2. Module information can also be specified using `@Module` annotation

_Explanation:_ There is no such annotation.

  3. Every class of a module must specify which module it belongs to either using @Module annotation or using a command line option.

_Explanation:_ A Module is higher level concept than classes and packages. Class code is not aware of the module in which the class exists. For a class to belong to a module, its package structure must reside under the module directory. For example, if a class `test.A` belongs to `moduleA`, then `A.java` must reside under `moduleA\test\` and `module-info.java` must be present in `moduleA` directory. 

  4. `module-info.java` must list all packages that you want to add to that module.
  5. The only information required in the file that specifies module information is the module name.

_Explanation:_ `module-info.java` cannot be empty and, at a minimum, it must specify the name of the module. For example, the following are the complete contents of a valid `module-info.java` file: ` module com.xyz.hr{ }` It defines a module named com.xyz.hr. Observe that the above module definition is empty. That is, it does not have any clause inside the { }. This is valid.

  6. A module must specify all sealed classes belonging to that module in its module-info.

_Explanation:_ There is no such requirement. Normally, the direct subclasses of a seal class must reside in the same package as the package of the sealed class. However, if a sealed class belongs to a named module, it is allowed to list classes from different packages of the same module in its permits clause.

  7. Only a modular app can be packaged as a jimage.

_Explanation:_ A JImage is a customized JRE that containts just those parts of a standard JRE that are required for a particular application to to run. Removing unwanted parts of the standard JRE reduces the files size of the resulting JRE. Such a custom JRE image plus the application jar files are packaged together in a .jimage file. A JImage file can be created for a modular or a non-modular app.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Multi-module mode is used to compile one or more modules, whose source code is laid out in exploded-module directories on the module source path. In this mode the module membership of a type is determined by the position of its source file in the module source path, so each source file specified on the command line must exist within an element of that path. 

27  |  ID: 3007  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Identify correct statements about java module descriptor.

  1. `module-info.java` may be placed inside any folder of the module.

_Explanation:_ It must be placed in the root directory of a module. For example, if you are defining a module named `com.xyz.hr`, then you must keep `module-info.java` inside `com.xyz.hr` folder.

  2. `module-info.java` must not be empty. 

_Explanation:_ Note: Some candidates have reported a seeing similar option in the real exam. It is not clear whether by `module-info.java` they really mean the java file or the module definition contained in `module-info.java` file. Technically, `module-info.java` cannot be empty and, at a minimum, it must specify the name of the module. For example, the following are the complete contents of a valid `module-info.java` file: ` module com.xyz.hr{ }` It defines a module named `com.xyz.hr`. Observe that the above module definition is empty. That is, it does not have any clause inside the { }. This is valid. In the real exam, carefully evaluate other options and select this option only if you are sure that other options are definitely incorrect.

  3. If `module-info.java` is absent, then the module name is inferred from the directory name in which the source code resides.

_Explanation:_ If you are creating a module, then `module-info.java` must be present. There is a way to create an automatic module but is is created using the name of the jar file in which the classes reside.

  4. `module-info.java` is compiled into `module-info.class` by the compiler.
  5. `module-info.java` always belongs to the default package.

_Explanation:_ module-info defines a module. A module does not depend or belong to any package. Packages belong to a module.
