28  |  ID: 3002  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about Java platform module system.

  1. A module is a set of packages that make sense being grouped together and is designed for reuse.
  2. The module system ensures that code that is internal to a platform implementation is not accessible from outside the implementation.
  3. The module system uses only two phases - compile time and run time - for building an application.

_Explanation:_ Besides compile time and run time, the module system adds the notion of "link time", which is an optional phase between the two in which a set of modules can be assembled and optimized into a custom run-time image. The benefit of this phase is that the size of the resulting application is reduced because only those classes that are actually used are included in the application artifacts.

  4. All classes in a module are concealed and cannot be accessed by code from other modules. Only interfaces are visibile outside the package.

_Explanation:_ What is visibile and what not visible to the outside world is controlled by the module developer through the information that he provides in module-info.java. He can choose to export any member of a module through exports clauses in module-info.java file of the module. Nothing is exported by default. I.e. if a module’s declaration contains no exports clauses then it will not export any types at all to any other modules.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Please go through Oracle's blog on Modules to get a general idea about modules: https://blogs.oracle.com/java/modular-development

29  |  ID: 3001  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the module system of Java.

  1. A JImage can only include java artifacts such as compiled classes and properties files.

_Explanation:_ A JImage is used to package a complete application including a customized JRE. Therefore, it can include not just classes and properties files but all sorts of resources such as binary files and images.

  2. Main goals of the module system are to improve security with strong encapsulation and stability with reliable dependencies.
  3. Code in modules and traditional JARs on the classpath cannot coexist in an application. 
  4. Modules have concealed packages for internal use and exported packages for shared code with other modules. 
  5. Only an application structured as modular can be run on a modular JDK.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Please go through Oracle's blog on Modules to get a general idea about modules: https://blogs.oracle.com/java/modular-development
    
    Normally, running a Java application requires the presence of the standard JRE, which includes all the standard modules and packages and is therefore, quite large in terms of disk size. The modularized JDK introduced with Java 9 allows you to create a customized JRE image containing just the parts that are required for your application thereby reducing the overall size by a lot. Such a custom JRE image is packaged in a .jimage file. A JImage file can be created and managed using tools such as `jlink` and `jimage`, which are available in the JDK. Creation of a JImage does not require an application to be modular.

30  |  ID: 3004  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which of the following statements are correct regarding the module system of Java.

  1. A module can access all public classes of another module.

_Explanation:_ public classes of only the packages that are exported by a module become accessible.

  2. A module can access all public classes of another module if it declares that it requires the other module.

_Explanation:_ The other module has to export its packages first. Merely stating that a module requires another module is not sufficient.

  3. A module can access all public classes of another module if those classes are exported by the other module.

_Explanation:_ A module can export only packages (not individual classes). Public classes of the packages that are exported can then be accessed by other modules.

  4. A module can access public classes of only those packages of another module that the other module exports.
  5. A module can access another module if both are in the same folder.

_Explanation:_ This option doesn't make any sense because every module resides in its own independent folder. Two modules cannot reside in the same folder.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Here are a few things that you must remember about modules - 
    1. A module can export only packages (not individual types(classes/interfaces/enums). All public types of exported packages can be accessed by other modules.
    2. To export a package, a module must write an exports clause for that package in its module-info.java
    
    For example, in the following module-info.java, module com.foo.bar exports com.foo.bar.alpha package to others. This means other modules can access all public types of com.foo.bar.alpha package.
```java
    module com.foo.bar {
        exports com.foo.bar.alpha;
    }`
    
    3. To access another module's exported packages, a module must specify that it requires the other module using the requires clause. For example, in the following module-info.java, module com.foo.app specifies that it requires module com.foo.bar. It can therefore access all public types of all the packages that are exported by module com.foo.bar.
```
    module com.foo.app {
        requires com.foo.bar;
    }`

31  |  ID: 3003  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the modular JDK.

  1. The foundational APIs of the Java SE platform are found in java.base module.

_Explanation:_ As per https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/21/docs/api/java.base/module-summary.html java.base module defines the "foundational APIs" of the Java SE Platform. The exam also uses the term "core packages" for the same.

  2. The modular JDK is composed of two modules - the java module and the jdk module.

_Explanation:_ There are several modules in the Java SE platform. These modules are categorized into two categories - java se and jdk. These two are not really modules but just names of two categories. Java SE The Java Platform, Standard Edition (Java SE) APIs define the core Java platform for general-purpose computing. These APIs are in modules whose names start with java. It contains modules such as: java.base, java.logging, java.sql, java.desktop, java.xml, and java.se. JDK The Java Development Kit (JDK) APIs are specific to the JDK and will not necessarily be available in all implementations of the Java SE Platform. These APIs are in modules whose names start with jdk. It contains modules such as: jdk.accessibility, jdk.javadoc, jdk.jartool, jdk.jlink, and jdk.net.

  3. JDK is divided into a set of modules that can be combined at compile time, build time, and run time into a variety of configurations.
  4. The modular JDK is divided of two kinds of modules - the standard modules and the non-standard modules.

_Explanation:_ The modular structure of the JDK implements the following principles: 1\. Standard modules, whose specifications are governed by the JCP, have names starting with the string "java.". 2\. All other modules are merely part of the JDK, and have names starting with the string "jdk.".




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 3, 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The base module, which is named as java.base, defines and exports all of the platform’s core packages, including the module system itself:
```java
    module java.base {
        exports java.io;
        exports java.lang;
        exports java.lang.annotation;
        exports java.lang.invoke;
        exports java.lang.module;
        exports java.lang.ref;
        exports java.lang.reflect;
        exports java.math;
        exports java.net;
        ...
    }`
    The base module is always present. Every other module depends implicitly upon the base module, while the base module depends upon no other modules.
    
    The remaining platform modules share the “java.” name prefix and are likely to include, e.g., java.sql for database connectivity, java.xml for XML processing, and java.logging for logging. Modules that are not defined in the Java SE 9 Platform Specification but instead specific to the JDK will, by convention, share the “jdk.” name prefix.

32  |  ID: 3633  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Given the following code:
```
    class TestClass{
       public static void main(String args[]){
          int time = 100;
          java.sql.Timestamp ts = new java.sql.Timestamp(time);
          java.util.Date d = new java.util.Date();
          ts = new java.sql.Timestamp(d.getTime());
          System.out.println(ts);
       }
    }
```java
    and the following commands:
```
    javac TestClass.java
    jdeps -summary TestClass.class
```java
    What will be the output?

  1. `TestClass.class -> java.base TestClass.class -> java.sql TestClass.class -> java.util`
  2. `TestClass.class -> java.base `
  3. `TestClass.class -> java.base TestClass.class -> java.sql `
  4. `TestClass.class -> java.sql TestClass.class -> java.util`
  5. Compilation will be successful but the jdeps command will generate an error.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    You need to remember that there is no such module as `java.util`. The `java.util` package belongs to the `java.base` module while `java.sql` package belongs to the `java.sql` module. Both the modules are a part of the standard Java SE platform. (You can check out the constituents of JSE here: https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/21/docs/api/index.html ).
    
    Thus, when you try to find out the dependencies of the given code, you will only see two modules - `java.base` (because all Java classes have a dependency on the `java.base` module) and `java.sql` (because of the usage of `java.sql.Timestamp` class.)

33  |  ID: 3634  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Given the following code:
```
    class TestClass{
       public static void main(String args[]){
          java.sql.Timestamp ts = new java.sql.Timestamp(1);
          java.awt.Label lb = new java.awt.Label("Time is "+ts);
          System.out.println(lb);
       }
    }
```java
    and the following commands:
```
    javac TestClass.java
    jdeps -summary TestClass.class
```java
    What will be the output?

  1. `TestClass.class -> java.desktop TestClass.class -> java.sql TestClass.class -> java.util`
  2. `TestClass.class -> java.base `
  3. `TestClass.class -> java.base TestClass.class -> java.se`
  4. `TestClass.class -> java.base TestClass.class -> java.desktop TestClass.class -> java.sql`
  5. `TestClass.class -> java.base TestClass.class -> java.sql TestClass.class -> java.util TestClass.class -> java.desktop`
  6. Compilation will be successful but the jdeps command will generate an error.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    You need to remember that there is no such module as `java.util`. The `java.util` package belongs to the `java.base` module while `java.sql` package belongs to the `java.sql` module.  Both the modules are a part of the standard Java SE platform. 
    
    GUI classes contained in `java.awt` as well as `javax.swing` packages belong to the `java.desktop` module, which is also a part of Java SE.
    
    (You can check out the constituents of JSE here: https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/21/docs/api/index.html ).
    
    Thus, when you try to find out the dependencies of the given code, you will only see three modules - `java.base` (because all Java classes have a dependency on the `java.base` module) and `java.sql` (because of the usage of `java.sql.Timestamp` class) and `java.desktop` module (because of the usage of java.awt.Label class).

34  |  ID: 3152  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which of the following commands can be used to identify class and module dependencies?

  1. jar -describe
  2. java --describe
  3. jmod describe
  4. jmod --describe
  5. jmod --show-module-resolution

_Explanation:_ The `--show-module-resolution` option is available in the java command. It prints out the complete module dependency tree for a given module.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    `jmod` is used for mod files, which are not on the exam. However, you might be asked about the describe option which prints module details for a jmod file. For example:
    
    `jmod describe jmods/ma.jmod`
    Observe that there are no hyphens (or dashes or minus sign) before describe.
    
    It prints the modules required by the `ma.jmod` file. However, it does not show complete module resolution.

35  |  ID: 3151  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given the following contents of `module-info.java`, 
```
    module enthu.finance{
      exports com.enthu.Reports to com.enthu.tax;
      requires transitive enthu.utils;
    } `
    
    Select correct statements.

  1. Which ever module requires `enthu.finance`, must require `enthu.utils` as well.

_Explanation:_ It means which ever module requires `enthu.finance`, will automatically require `enthu.utils` as well. It will not need an additional `requires enthu.utils;` clause to read `enthu.utils` module.

  2. The `enthu.finance` module requires all modules required by `enthu.utils`.
  3. The `enthu.finance` module requires only those modules that are required by `enthu.utils` but does not require `enthu.utils` itself.
  4. Only classes from the `com.enthu.tax` module are allowed to read classes in the `com.enthu.Reports` package.
  5. The `enthu.finance` module exports only those classes that are used by the `com.enthu.tax` module.

_Explanation:_ This option does not make any sense because there is no way for a module to know which of its classes are actually being used (or will actually be used) by another module. A module can only specify which of its classes [b]can be used[/b] by others by using an appropriate exports clause.

  6. This is an invalid module definition because exports-to and requires-transitive clauses cannot be used together.

_Explanation:_ There is no such rule.

  7. Only non-sealed classes from `com.enthu.Reports` are exported.

_Explanation:_ All public classes from com.enthu.Reports package are exported. This has nothing to do with sealed/non-sealed.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The `requires-transitive` clause means that if any module that requires this module will also automatically require (hence, will be able to read, without explicitly requiring it) the module mentioned in the requires transitive clause. For example, if you have :
```java
    module com.amazing.app{
       requires transitive com.amazing.customer;//4
    }
```
    
    then any module that contains `requires com.amazing.app;` clause will automatically be able to read the `com.amazing.customer` module without any additional `requires` clause.
    
    The `exports-to` clause exports a package to a specific list of modules. For example, if you have :
    
    `exports com.acme.db to com.acme.dto, com.acme.service;
```java
    then only the classes in `com.acme.dto` and `com.acme.service` modules will be allowed to access the classes in package `com.acme.db`.
    
    The modules after the `to` clause have to be listed explicitly. You can't use * to mean all modules. If you want to expose a package to all modules, just leave the `to` clause.
    
    

36  |  ID: 3144  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code appearing in a module-info.java
```
    module com.amazing.movies{ 
       requires transitive com.amazing.customer;
    }
```java
    Identify correct statements.

  1. Any module that requires the `com.amazing.movies` module must also require the `com.amazing.customer` module.
  2. Any module that requires the `com.amazing.movies` module can use the `com.amazing.customer` module without requiring it.
  3. Only a module that requires the `com.amazing.movies` module can use the `com.amazing.customer` module.
  4. Any module that requires the `com.amazing.customer` module must also require the `com.amazing.movies` module.
  5. Any module that requires the `com.amazing.movies` module must require the `com.amazing.customer` module instead of `com.amazing.movies`.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    `requires transitive <module name>` means that any module that reads the module containing this clause implicitly also reads the transitive module. This is very helpful when a module class's method returns a type defined in another module. 
    
    For example, let's say you have two modules `finance` and `accounts`. `finance` module has a class named `Report`, which returns a list of `Accounts` through its `getAccounts()` method. The `Account` class, on the other hand, is defined in the `accounts` module.
    
    Now, any other module that calls `Report.getAccounts()`, will normally need to have two requires clauses - `requires finance;` and `requires accounts;`. But if the `finance` module contains `requires transitive account;` clause, the `requires accounts;` clause will not be necessary. Any module that requires the `finance` module will automatically allowed to use the `accounts` module.
    

37  |  ID: 4023  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Consider the following module descriptors:
```
    module movies{ //1
       exports com.amazing.movies to rentals;//3
    }`
    
    and 
    
    `module rentals{
    }
```java
    Identify correct statements.

  1. All members in the `movies` module are accessible only to the members of the `rentals` module.
  2. Public members of the `movies` module are accessible only to the `rentals` module.
  3. All members of the `com.amazing.movies` package are accessible only to the members of the `com.amazing.rentals` package.

_Explanation:_ Non-public members of a package are never allowed to be accessed from outside a module. The `exports/exports-to` clauses are only meant for the public members of a package.

  4. Only public members of the `com.amazing.movies` package are accessible only to the members of the `rentals` module.
  5. Inserting `requires movies;` in `rentals` module-info enables the `rentals` module to access members of the `com.amazing.movies` package of the `movies` module.

_Explanation:_ The `exports` and `exports-to` clauses only make the public members of a package eligible to be accessed from outside the module. But for another module to access the exported packages, it must also have an appropriate `requires` clause.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

38  |  ID: 4022  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the modular JDK.

  1. The base module does not depend on any module while every other module depends on the base module.
  2. The set of modules of the modular JDK can be combined to create configurations corresponding to the full Java SE Platform, the full JRE, and the full JDK.
  3. The standard modules of the modular JDK are governed by the Java Community Process while non-standard ones are not.
  4. A standard module may contain a standard or non-standard API package but must not export any non-standard package.

_Explanation:_ A standard module may export a non-standard package but that export must be qualified.

  5. The modularized JDK allows you to create a customized JRE image containing just the parts that are required for a modular or a non-modular application to run.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 2, 3, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Remember that the modular structure of the JDK implements the following principles:
    
    . Standard modules, whose specifications are governed by the JCP, have names starting with the string "java.".
    
    . All other modules are merely part of the JDK, and have names starting with the string "jdk.".
    
    . A standard module may contain both standard and non-standard API packages.  If a standard module exports a standard API package then the export may be qualified; if a standard module exports a non-standard API package then the export must be qualified.
    
    . A standard module may depend upon one or more non-standard modules. It must not grant implied readability to any non-standard module. If it is a Java SE module then it must not grant implied readability to any non-SE module.
    
    . A non-standard module must not export any standard API packages. A non-standard module may grant implied readability to a standard module.
    
    . Creation of a JImage does not require an application to be modular because it is meant for creating customized JREs by cherry picking modules from the standard JRE. Customized JRE images can be created for modular as well as non-modular applications.

39  |  ID: 3108  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Which of the following are benefits of the Java module system?

  1. It saves application developers from Jar hell.

_Explanation:_ It does not completely eliminates the use of jar files. You still have to deal with jars for existing applications and third party non-modularized libraries.

  2. It improves security and maintainability.
  3. It makes it easier to expose implementation details.

_Explanation:_ The whole point of having modules is to prevent implementation details from being exposed unnecessarily.

  4. It eliminates the need of making custom runtime linked applications.

_Explanation:_ In fact, custom runtime linked applications require less memory footprint and the Java module system helps in building such applications.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

40  |  ID: 3138  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Given the following module definitions:
```
    module m${
       requires _n;
       exports o;
    }
    
    module _n{
       requires m$;
       exports p;
    }
```java
    Identify the correct statements.

  1. The definitions are invalid because of circular dependency.

_Explanation:_ Modules are not allowed to have circular/cyclic dependencies. Here, module m$ requires module _n and module _n requires module m$. This is not allowed.

  2. The definitions are invalid because the package names in the export clauses are invalid.

_Explanation:_ There is nothing wrong with the package names. It is valid to have `o` and `p` as package names.

  3. The definitions are invalid because the modules names are invalid.

_Explanation:_ There is nothing wrong with the given module names. A module name follows the same rules and conventions as packages and classes. A valid package name would therefore, be a valid module name. In short, the name must be a valid Java identifier (so, special characters such as dash and slash are invalid but underscore and dot are valid). Generally, module names also follow the reverse domain name pattern to avoid name clash, for example, `com.mycompany.hr` . 

  4. The definitions are invalid because they don't declare dependency on `java.base` module.

_Explanation:_ All modules implicitly require the `java.base` module. There is no need to put a requires clause for this module explicitly.

  5. The definitions are invalid because of circular dependency as well as ommission of `requires java.base;`.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

41  |  ID: 3134  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Although jmod is not on the part exam you might still see it mentioned in one of the incorrect options. You may read about jmod if you have time.
    
    Which of the following options are supported by jmod?

  1. create
  2. add
  3. delete
  4. list
  5. extract
  6. describe

_Explanation:_ The describe option prints module details for a jmod file. For example: `jmod describe jmods/ma.jmod` (Observe that there are no hyphens (or dashes or minus sign) before describe.) Assuming that ma requires mb, it prints: ` ma requires mandated java.base requires mb ` `jmod describe jmods/mb.jmod` ` mb requires mandated java.base hashes ma SHA-256 07667d5032004b37b42ec2bb81b46df380cf29e66962a16481ace2e71e74073a `




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4, 5, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Jmod tool has 5 options: create, extract, list, describe, hash.
    
    According to the documentation, JMOD enables aggregating files other than class files, metadata, and resources such as native codes and other things that cannot be stored in a JAR file. Therefore, JMOD files are designed to contain file types that cannot be contained by JAR files. However, unlike JARs, which are executable, the JMOD files cannot be executed. This means this files contained in JMOD can be used only at compile-time or link-time, but not at runtime.

42  |  ID: 3133  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Consider the following code appearing in a module-info.java
```
    module com.amazing.movies{ //1
       exports com.amazing.movies; //2
       exports com.amazing.movies to com.amazing.rentals;//3
       requires transitive com.amazing.customer;//4
    }
```java
    Identify correct statements.

  1. This is a valid module info.
  2. This is an invalid module info because the name of the module and the name of a package that it exports are the same.
  3. This is an invalid module info because line marked //3 uses incorrect syntax.
  4. This is an invalid module info because lines marked //2 and //3 are in conflict.

_Explanation:_ You cannot have duplicate statements in a module info. Lines marked //2 and //3 are exporting the same package in different ways and are therefore in conflict. One of them must be removed.

  5. This is an invalid module info because lines marked //3 and //4 use incorrect syntax.

_Explanation:_ Line marked //4 is valid.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Note that the `exports-to` clause exports a package to a specific list of modules. For example, if you have :
    
    `exports com.acme.db to com.acme.dto, com.acme.service;
```
    then only the classes in `com.acme.dto` and `com.acme.service` modules will be allowed to access the classes in package `com.acme.db`.
    
    The modules after the `to` clause have to be listed explicitly. You can't use * to mean all modules. If you want to expose a package to all modules, just leave the `to` clause.
    
    The `requires-transitive` clause means that if any module that requires this module will also automatically require (hence, will be able to read, without explicitly requiring it) the module mentioned in the requires transitive clause. For example, if you have :
```java
    module com.amazing.app{
       requires transitive com.amazing.customer;//4
    }
```
    
    then any module that contains `requires com.amazing.app;` clause will automatically be able to read the `com.amazing.customer` module without any additional `requires` clause.
    

43  |  ID: 3135  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following tools support the `--show-module-resolution` option?

  1. java

_Explanation:_ It shows module resolution output during startup. Note: Details of this option are not required for the exam. Just knowing that it is used during execution of a java program to see how modules are resolved is sufficient.

  2. javac
  3. jar
  4. jmod

_Explanation:_ jmod is used for mod files, which are not on the exam. However, you might be asked about the describe option which prints module details for a jmod file. For example: `jmod describe jmods/ma.jmod` Observe that there are no hyphens (or dashes or minus sign) before describe. It prints the modules required by the ma module. However, it does not show complete module resolution.

  5. jdeps
  6. jimage

_Explanation:_ The modularized JDK introduced with Java 9 allows you to create a customized JRE image containing just the parts that are required for your application thereby reducing the overall size by a lot. Such a custom JRE image is packaged in a .jimage file. A JImage file can be created and managed using tools such as jlink and jimage, which are available in the JDK.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

45  |  ID: 3132  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Identify legal module names.
    

  1. com.amazing.movie-rentals
  2. com.amazing.movierentals
  3. amazing-movierentals

_Explanation:_ \- is invalid but _ is valid. So, `amazing_movierentals`, `com._amazing`, and `_amazing` are valid module names.

  4. com.amazing.$movierentals
  5. no1movierentals
  6. com.amazing.1movierentals



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 4, 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    A module name consists of one or more Java identifiers separated by " . " tokens. 
    Since, you cannot use dash (-) in a java identifier, any module name that uses a dash is invalid (btw, underscore _ is valid). Further, an identifier cannot start with a number, therefore, any module name that has a token starting with a number is invalid.

46  |  ID: 3131  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Which of the following are valid command line options and their one character shortcuts related to the Java module system?
    

  1. `--module -p`
  2. `--module-path -p`
  3. `--module-source-path -s`
  4. `---module-source-path -m`
  5. `--list-modules -l`
  6. `--show-module-resolution -s`
  7. `--module -m`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 7

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    You should remember the following command line options:
    
    Module options applicable for `java` as well as `javac`:
    `--module` or `-m` : used to run or compile only the specified module.
    `--module-path` or `-p`: used to specify the paths where java or javac will look for module definitions.
    
    Module options applicable only for `javac`:
    `--module-source-path` has no shortcut. Used by javac to look for source module definitions.
    `-d`: used to specify output directory where the class files will be created after compilation.
    
    Module options applicable only for `java`:
    `--list-modules` has no shortcut. It lists observable modules and exits.
    `--show-module-resolution` has no shortcut. It shows module resolution output during startup.
    `--describe-module` or `-d`: It describes a module and exits. 
    
    Note that `-d` works differently in java and javac. Further, `-d` is very different from `-D`, which is used while running a java program to specify name-value pairs of properties at the command line.

47  |  ID: 3129  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Consider the following directory structure and the files contained in the directories:
```java
    c:\javatest
      +- <src>
           +- <foo.bar>
                 +- <foo>
                     +- <bar>
                           -- BazA.java
                           -- BazB.java
    
```
    Assuming that the package name specified in BazA and BazB is `foo.bar`, which of the following statements are correct?

  1. There should be a `module-info.java` file in the `src` directory for it to be a valid module definition.
  2. The `foo.bar` directory is a valid source module definition.

_Explanation:_ The root directory of a source module definition must have `module-info.java`.

  3. There should be a `module-info.java` file in the `foo.bar` directory for it to be a valid source module definition.
  4. There should be a `module.java` file in the `foo` directory for it to be a valid module definition.
  5. There should be a `module-info.java` file in the `bar` directory for it to be a valid source module definition.
  6. There should be a `module.java` file in the `foo.bar` directory for it to be a valid module definition.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    As per JEP 261:
    Each module definition is either:
    1.  A module artifact, i.e., a modular JAR file or a JMOD file containing a compiled module definition, or else
    or
    2. An exploded-module directory whose name is, by convention, the module's name and whose content is an "exploded" directory tree corresponding to a package hierarchy.
    
    In the latter case the directory tree can be a compiled module definition, populated with individual class and resource files and a module-info.class file at the root or, at compile time, a source module definition, populated with individual source files and a module-info.java file at the root.

48  |  ID: 3128  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Java Module system achieves which of the following objectives for a Java project?
    

  1. It encapsulates the project.
  2. It encapsulates the packages.
  3. It provides a central repository for keeping classes.

_Explanation:_ The Java module system does not attempt to be a repository. It merely provides a way to encapsulate the packages.

  4. It enables a project to manage multiple versions of dependencies.

_Explanation:_ The Java module system does not attempt to solve versioning issues.

  5. It provides a reliable mechanism to let components of an application specify their dependencies on other components of the same application.

_Explanation:_ A java module specifies the packages that other modules can use using the `exports` and `exports to` clauses and it defines its dependencies on other modules using the `requires` and `requires transitive` clauses in its `module-info`.

  6. It provides a way to specify services and declare dependence on such services.

_Explanation:_ The Java module system allows a module to specify the services that it provides using the `provides` clause and it allows a module to declare its dependency on services using the `uses` clause. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 5, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    The specific goals of the module system are to provide:
    1. Reliable configuration, to replace the brittle, error-prone class-path mechanism with a means for program components to declare explicit dependences upon one another, along with,
    2. Strong encapsulation, to allow a component to declare which of its public types are accessible to other components, and which are not.

## 📖 08 - Modules - Migration to Modular Application

21 questions

1  |  ID: 3428  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Your application is packaged in `myapp.jar` and depends on a jar named `datalayer.jar`, which in turn depends on `mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar`. The following packages exist in these jars:
    
    `myapp.jar:   com.abc.myapp
    datalayer.jar: com.abc.datalayer
    mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar:  com.mysql.jdbc
```java
    
    You have decided to modularize your application even though datalayer and mysql libraries are still not modularlized. Which of the following would be a valid module-info for your app?

  1. `module abc.myapp{ requires com.abc.datalayer; }`
  2. `module abc.myapp{ exports com.abc.myapp; }`
  3. `module abc.myapp{ requires datalayer; }`

_Explanation:_ Since myapp depends directly only on `datalayer.jar`, you just need to put this jar on `module-path` to make it an automatic module. The name of this module will be `datalayer`. Thus, you must specify `requires datalayer;` in the module-info of myapp. There is no need to add a dependency on msql jar in myapp. datalayer will be able to access mysql classes if you simply put the mysql jar on the classpath. So, your command to run myapp module would be: `java --module-path datalayer.jar;myapp.jar -classpath mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar \--module abc.myapp`

  4. `module abc.myapp{ requires datalayer; requires mysql-connector-java; }`
  5. `module abc.myapp{ requires datalayer; requires mysql-connector-java-0.0.11; }`
  6. `module abc.myapp{ requires com.abc.datalayer; requires com.mysql.jdbc; }`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 8_top_down_approach_points**

2  |  ID: 3427  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Your application is packaged in `myapp.jar` and depends on a jar named `datalayer.jar`, which in turn depends on `mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar`. The following packages exist in these jars:
    
    `myapp.jar:   com.abc.myapp
    datalayer.jar: com.abc.datalayer
    mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar:  com.mysql.jdbc
```
    
    You want to use bottom up approach for migrating your app to a modular app. Which of the following is required before you can do this?

  1. Mysql driver jar and datalayer.jar must first be converted into modular jars.

_Explanation:_ In this case, mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar would have to become modular first, then datalayer.jar. In the top down approach, on the other hand, you would directly make `myapp.jar` modular by including a module-info and adding `requires datalayer;` clause. You would create an automatic module for `datalayer.jar` by simply placing it on `module-path` (instead of `classpath`). You would leave mysql jar on the classpath so that datalayer could access it.

  2. datalayer.jar must first be converted into modular jar. The mysql jar need not be converted.
  3. The mysql jar must first be converted into modular jar. The datalayer.jar need not be converted.
  4. Neither datalayer nor mysql driver need to be converted into modular jars.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 9_bottom_up_approach_points**

3  |  ID: 3429  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Your application is packaged in `myapp.jar` and depends on a jar named `datalayer.jar`, which in turn depends on `mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar`. 
    
    You have modularized myapp without waiting for datalayer and mysql driver to be modularized and the following are the contents of myapp's module-info:
```java
    module abc.myapp{
      requires datalayer;
    }`
    
    Assming that `com.abc.myapp.Main` is the main class of your application that you want to execute, which of the following commands will successfully launch your application?
    

  1. `java -classpath mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar;datalayer.jar;myapp.jar com.abc.myapp.Main`

_Explanation:_ This is the old way to execute a non-modular app. You can still use it to execute a modular app.

  2. `java -module-path mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar;datalayer.jar;myapp.jar com.abc.myapp.Main`
  3. `java -p mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar;datalayer.jar;myapp.jar -m com.abc.myapp.Main`
  4. `java -p mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar;datalayer.jar;myapp.jar -m abc.myapp/com.abc.myapp.Main`

_Explanation:_ Remember that, in addition to getting access to all packages in unnamed module (i.e. classes on the classpath), an automatic module exports all its packages and is allowed to read all packages exported by other modules. Thus, if you put datalayer and mysql jars also on the module path, datalayer will still be able to access classes from mysql jar.

  5. `java -cp mysql-connector-java-8.0.11.jar -p datalayer.jar;myapp.jar -m abc.myapp/com.abc.myapp.Main`

_Explanation:_ Remember that a named module cannot access classes from the unnamed module (unless the named module is an automatic module). Now, myapp says it "requires" datalayer module. Thus, datalayer.jar must be put on module-path so that myapp can access it. Since datalayer is not modularlized, it will be loaded as an automatic module and will be allowed to access classes loaded from classpath. Thus, the mysql jar can be put on the classpath. Note: `--module-path` is same as `-p` `--module` is same as `-m` `-classpath` is same as `-cp` 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4, 5

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 8_top_down_approach_points**

4  |  ID: 3677  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Which of the following are present on the "module-path"?

  1. only named modules
  2. only unnamed modules
  3. only automatic modules
  4. named, unnamed, and automatic modules
  5. only named and automatic modules

_Explanation:_ 1\. Remember that while running a modularized java application, you can put jars "on" module-path and/or classpath. 2\. Classes belonging to any jar that you put on the classpath become part of the "unnamed module". These classes/packages are not on the module-path and so, classes from the named modules cannot access such classes/packages. 3\. All other jars (i.e. named module jars and automatic module jars) are put on the module-path. 4\. Technically, automatic modules are also named modules but their names are derived automatically using the file names of the jar in which they are packaged. Classes in automatic modules CAN access all classes/packages in the unnamed module. 

  6. all modules



✓ Correct Answer(s): 5

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    This options in this question use wordings that are not technically precise because named modules and automatic modules are not two disjoint set of modules. Automatic modules also have names and are, therefore, named modules. In other words, automatic modules are a subset of named modules. Unfortunately, the real also exam uses these terms imprecisely, which is why we have used them here.
    
    Basically, any jar that you put on module-path is a named module, while any jar that you put on the classpath belongs to one "unnamed" module.

5  |  ID: 3435  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Which of the following statements are correct about `--jdk-internals` option of `jdeps` tool when run on a jar file or a class file?

  1. It analyzes all classes of the given jar file for class level dependence on Java API.
  2. It analyzes all classes of the given jar file for class level dependence on jdk's deprecated API. If any such dependence is found, it is printed with a suggestion for replacement.
  3. It analyzes all classes of the given jar file for class level dependence on jdk's internal API. If any such dependence is found, it is printed with a suggestion for replacement.
  4. It analyzes all input modules and list all jdk internal modules on which the input modules depend. If any such dependence is found, it is printed with a suggestion for replacement.
  5. It can only be used with `-summary` or `-verbose` options.
  6. It performs static analysis.

_Explanation:_ Jdeps does not execute any classes/modules. It only inspects the code. Thus, it cannot find out dependencies that are created due classes loaded at run time using reflection API.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 6

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    `-jdkinternals` or `--jdk-internals`
    Finds class-level dependencies in the JDK internal APIs. By default, this option analyzes all classes specified in the --classpath option and input files unless you specified the -include option. You can’t use this option with the -p, -e, and -s options.
    
    For example, if you have the following class (you need to compile it with JDK 8 or less because sun.misc.BASE64Encoder was removed in Java 9):
```
    public class TestClass{
      public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
        sun.misc.BASE64Encoder encoder = new sun.misc.BASE64Encoder();
        String s = encoder.encode("hello".getBytes());
      }
    }
```java
    and if you run `jdeps -jdkinternals TestClass.class`, you will see the following output:
    
```
    TestClass.class -> JDK removed internal API 
       TestClass          -> sun.misc.BASE64Encoder     JDK internal API (JDK removed internal API)
```java
    Warning: JDK internal APIs are unsupported and private to JDK implementation that are subject to be removed or changed incompatibly and could break your application.
    Please modify your code to eliminate dependence on any JDK internal APIs. For the most recent update on JDK internal API replacements, please check:
    https://wiki.openjdk.java.net/display/JDK8/Java+Dependency+Analysis+Tool
```
    JDK Internal API                         Suggested Replacement
    ----------------                         ---------------------
    sun.misc.BASE64Encoder                   Use java.util.Base64 @since 1.8
```java

6  |  ID: 3434  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    A modularized application has been packaged in `graphs.jar` file with module name as `com.xcomp.graphs`. The entry point of the application is a class named `Main` in package `com.xcomp.graphs`.
    
    Which two options can be used to execute this application?

  1. `java --module-path graphs.jar --module com.xcomp.graphs.Main`
  2. `java --module-path graphs.jar --module com.xcomp.graphs/com.xcomp.graphs.Main`

_Explanation:_ If you want to execute a class that belongs to a module using modular options, you need to put the jar file on --module-path (or -p) and use --module (or -m) option to specify the class you want to execute in <modulename>/<fully qualified classname> format.

  3. `java -classpath graphs.jar --module com.xcomp.graphs/com.xcomp.graphs.Main`
  4. `java --module-path graphs.jar com.xcomp.graphs.Main`
  5. `java -classpath graphs.jar com.xcomp.graphs.Main`

_Explanation:_ A class in a modular jar can be executed using the old non-modular option of -classpath. In this case, you just put the jar on -classpath and directly specify the name of the main class. This is possible because the structure of a modular jar is same as the non-modular jar. It just has an extra `module-info.class` in its root.

  6. `java --module-path graphs.jar --main-class com.xcomp.graphs.Main`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 2, 5

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 7_module_execution_options**

7  |  ID: 3679  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    Your are modularizing your java application but it depends on `commons-dbcp-0.9.jar`, which is not modularized. So, you have decided to put this jar on the module-path. What module name will be given to the automatic module created for this jar?

  1. commons.dbcp.0.9
  2. commons.dbcp.0
  3. commons-dbcp-09
  4. commons.dbcp
  5. commons-dbcp



✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    When you use a non modular jar file as an automatic module, the name of the jar file is used to formulate the module name for that module. You need to know the following two basic rules while deriving this name from the file name:
    
    1. The dashs are converted to dots and the version information and the file extension present in the file name are ignored. Therefore, the module name for `commons-dbcp-0.9.jar`  will be `commons.dbcp`
    
    2. If the jar file's manifest contains the `Automatic-Module-Name` entry, then that value is used as the module name (the name of the jar file is ignored completely).  
    
    Since no such information is present in the given problem statement, you need to consider just the file name.

8  |  ID: 3437  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    What will the following command show?
    `jdeps --list-deps moduleA.jar`

  1. It will list all the modules on which moduleA depends.

_Explanation:_ Lists the module dependencies and also the package names of JDK internal APIs (if referenced).

  2. It will list all the packages of all the modules on which moduleA depends.
  3. It will list all the standard JDK modules on which moduleA depends.
  4. It checks whether moduleA.jar contains all the required modules and prints the ones not present.
  5. It will show an error if moduleA requires any other application module.

_Explanation:_ Remember that one modular jar contains only one module. So, moduleA.jar will contain only one module. If moduleA depends only on the standard JDK modules, then jdeps will print them. However, if this module depends on any other application module (for example, moduleB), jdeps won't find it and will therefore fail. You need to put all the module jars in --module-path. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 5

9  |  ID: 3436  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Identify correct statements about `-classpath` and `--module-path` options while executing a java program.

  1. A modular jar placed on the classpath results in a named application module.

_Explanation:_ Anything placed on the classpath becomes part of the unnamed module.

  2. A non-modular jar placed on the classpath results in an automatic module.

_Explanation:_ Anything placed on the classpath becomes part of the unnamed module.

  3. A non-modular jar placed on the module-path results in an automatic module.

_Explanation:_ Remember that automatic modules are also named modules. Their name is inferred using the name of the jar file (or from `Automatic-Module-Name` entry of its manifest).

  4. A modular jar placed on the module-path results in a named application module.

_Explanation:_ Any jar (modular or non-modular) placed on the module-path becomes a named application module.

  5. If a package is available in a jar on the classpath as well as in another jar on the module-path, the one on the classpath is ignored.
  6. Classpath or module-path are mutually exclusive i.e. only one of them should be used.



✓ Correct Answer(s): 3, 4, 5

10  |  ID: 3431  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    You are the maintainer of a library packaged as `bondanalytics.jar`, which is used by several groups in your company. It has the following two packages that are used by other applications:
    `com.abc.bonds
    com.abc.bonds.analytics
```
    You want to modularize this jar with least impact to others. What will you do?

  1. Split the jar into two modules - one exporting `com.abc.bonds` package and another exporting `com.abc.bonds.analytics` package.

_Explanation:_ Although it is technically possible but it will impact others because they will now be required to use two jars instead of one.

  2. Just add module-info.java to the jar with exports clauses for both the packages.

_Explanation:_ For example: module bondanalytics{ exports com.abc.bonds; exports com.abc.bonds.analytics; }

  3. Just add an empty `module-info.java` to the jar.

_Explanation:_ If you don't export a package then other modular jars cannot access classes from this jar.

  4. It cannot be modularized without impacting existing non-modular applications that use it.

_Explanation:_ A modular jar can still be used like a regular jar. So, any non-modular application can use a modularized jar by putting that jar in the classpath just like any other jar.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 2

11  |  ID: 3430  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    Your group has an existing application (`reports.jar`) that uses a library (`analytics.jar`) from another group in your company. Both - the application and the library - use a JDBC driver packaged in `ojdbc8.jar`.
    
    Which of the following options describes the steps that will be required to modularize your application?

  1. 1\. Convert `analytics.jar` and `ojdbc8.jar` into automatic modules 2\. Convert `reports.jar` into a named module. 3\. Add `requires` clauses for `analytics` and `ojdbc8` in `reports.jar` in its module-info.
  2. 1\. Modularize `analytics.jar` and `ojdbc8.jar` into modules by adding module-info to these jars. 2\. Convert `reports.jar` into a named module. 3\. Add `requires` clauses for all packages contained in `analytics.jar` and `ojdbc8.jar` that are directly referred to by classes in `reports.jar` in its module-info.

_Explanation:_ Modularizing third party jars on your own is not a good idea because then you will have to keep doing that whenever you get an updated version of the third party jar. You can simply use third party jars as automatic modules without doing anything. Also, `requires` clauses are used to specify modules (not packages).

  3. 1\. Convert `reports.jar` into a named module. 2\. Add `requires` clauses for `analytics` and `ojdbc8` modules in `reports.jar` in its module-info. 3\. Use `analytics.jar` and `ojdbc8.jar` as unnamed modules.

_Explanation:_ Classes in unnamed modules cannot be accessed by a named module. 

  4. 1\. Convert `ojdbc8.jar` into automatic module. 2\. Convert `analytics.jar` into a named module by adding module-info to it. In this module-info, export all packages that are used by `reports.jar` and add `requires` clauses for all packages of `ojdbc8.jar` that are used by `analytics.jar`. 3\. Convert `reports.jar` into a named module. Add `requires` clause for `analytics` module in `reports`'s module-info. 

_Explanation:_ This is incorrect because of the second point. You shouldn't convert jars from other groups (aka third party jars) to named modules on your own as explained above. Also, `requires` clauses are used to specify modules (not packages).




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    If a module directly uses classes from another jar, then that jar has to be a module - either named or automatic.  
    
    So, if you want to modularize `reports.jar`, then `analytics.jar` and `ojdbc8.jar` must also be converted into modules. Since these two jars are not controlled by you, they can be put on module path and be automatic modules.
    
    module-info for `reports.jar` must have `requires` clauses for the two automatic modules (whose names will be `analytics` and `ojdbc8`).
    
    Since an automatic module is allowed to access classes from all other modules, nothing special needs to be done for `analytics.jar`. It will be able to access all classes from `ojdbc8.jar`.

12  |  ID: 3433  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    You are the maintainer of a library packaged as `bondanalytics.jar`, which is used by several groups in your company. It has the following two packages that are used by other applications:
    `com.abc.bonds
    com.abc.bonds.analytics
```java
    You want to modularize this jar but some groups have not yet modularized their applications. How will such groups use the modularized jar?

  1. They cannot. Two versions of the jar will need to be maintained - one modularized and one old.
  2. They can remove `module-info.class` from your modular jar and use the jar just as before.
  3. They can use the new modular jar just like they use the old jar earlier without any changes.

_Explanation:_ A modular jar has the same structure as the regular jar. So, any group that is running non-modular application can simply add the modular jar on the classpath like any other non-modular jar.

  4. They would have to use the --module-path option to use the new modular jar.

13  |  ID: 3432  |  Difficulty: Medium
    
    
    You have an existing java application that contains the following packages -
    `com.acme.provisioning.client
    com.acme.provisioning.service
    com.acme.provisioning.dto
    com.acme.provisioning.database
```
    The dependency of classes in these packages is as follows - 
    
    Classes in the client package use classes from the service and the dto package.
    Classes in the service package use classes from the dto and the database package
    Classes in the database package use classes from the dto package.
    Classes in the dto package do not use classes from any other package.
    
    You want to modularize the application into three modules, namely, `acme.provisioning.client`, `acme.provisioning.service`, and `acme.provisioning.dto`.
    Which of the following would be a valid module-info for the service module?

  1. `module acme.provisioning.service{ exports com.acme.provisioning.service; exports com.acme.provisioning.database; requires acme.provisioning.dto; }`

_Explanation:_ Since database package is not used by any other module, there is no need to export it from the service module. Technically, it is not incorrect to do so. But unnecessary exports should be avoided and that is why this option is not as good as option 2.

  2. `module acme.provisioning.service{ exports com.acme.provisioning.service; requires acme.provisioning.dto; }`
  3. `module acme.provisioning.service{ exports com.acme.provisioning.service; requires com.acme.provisioning.database; requires acme.provisioning.dto; }`

_Explanation:_ Since database package is not a separate module, you can't put `requires com.acme.provisioning.database;`

  4. Since there are four interdependent packages in the app, it cannot be modularized into three modules.

_Explanation:_ Ideally, it should be modularized into four modules (client, service, database, and dto). However, it is possible to merge service and database packages into one module.

14  |  ID: 3426  |  Difficulty: Easy
    
    
    Your application was developed and packaged on the Java 8 platform as a jar file. It consists of the following packages:
    `com.abc.stocks
    com.abc.stocks.valuation
    com.abc.stocks.analytics
    com.abc.bonds
    com.abc.bonds.valuation
    com.abc.bonds.analytics
    com.abc.derivatives
    com.abc.derivatives.valuation
    com.abc.derivatives.analytics`
    
    You are now trying to modularize it into three different modules. Which of the following module descriptions could potentially be valid?
    

  1. `module com.abc.stocks{ provides com.abc.stocks.analytics; } module com.abc.bonds{ provides com.abc.bonds.analytics; } module com.abc.derivatives{ provides com.abc.derivatives.analytics; }`

_Explanation:_ "provides" is used to specify an implementation class for a service interface. Its sytanx is: `provides <interface name> with <class name>;`

  2. `module com.abc.stocks{ exports com.abc.stocks.analytics; } module com.abc.bonds{ exports com.abc.bonds.analytics; } module com.abc.derivatives{ exports com.abc.derivatives.analytics; }`

_Explanation:_ It is possible that only the analytics package is meant to be used by other modules and so only that package is exported.

  3. `module com.abc.stocks{ opens com.abc.stocks.analytics; } module com.abc.bonds{ opens com.abc.bonds.analytics; } module com.abc.derivatives{ opens com.abc.derivatives.analytics; }`

_Explanation:_ `opens` clause is used to denote that a package can be accessed using reflection API. It is not on the exam but we have seen questions that contain this keyword in wrong options. So, it is better to be aware of it. Note that the definitions given in this option are potentially valid but it is better to not select this option and select the option that does not use open/opens clauses instead if you have to select only one option.

  4. `module com.abc.stocks{ uses com.abc.stocks.valuation; uses com.abc.stocks.analytics; } module com.abc.bonds{ uses com.abc.bonds.valuation; uses com.abc.bonds.analytics; } module com.abc.derivatives{ uses com.abc.derivatives.valuation; uses com.abc.derivatives.analytics; }`

_Explanation:_ "uses" is used to specify the service interface that this module uses. The service interface would be in another module. Thus, this option doesn't make sense.

15  |  ID: 3425  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Identify correct statements about the Java module system.

  1. If a request is made from an automatic module to load a type whose package is not defined in any known module then the module system will attempt to load it from the classpath.
  2. The unnamed module can only access types present in the unnamed module.

_Explanation:_ The unnamed module reads every other module. In other words, a class in an unnamed module can access all exported types of all modules.

  3. Code from a named module can access classes that are on the classpath.

_Explanation:_ A named module cannot access any random class from the classpath. If your named module requires access to a non-modular class, you must put the non-modular class/jar on module-path and load it as an automatic module. Further, you must also put an appropriate "requires" clause in your module-info.

  4. If a package is defined in both a named module and the unnamed module then the package in the unnamed module is ignored. 
  5. An automatic module cannot access classes from the unnamed module. 

_Explanation:_ Remember that named modules cannot access classes from the unnamed module because it is not possible for a named module to declare dependency on the unnamed module. But what if a named module needs to access a class from a non-modular jar? Well, you can put the non-modular jar on the module-path, thereby making it an automatic module. A named module can declare dependency on an automatic module using the requires clause. Now, what if that jar in turn requires access to some other class from another third party non-modular jar? Here, the original modular jar doesn't directly access the non-modular jar, so it may not be wise to create an automatic module out of all such third party jars. This is where the -classpath options is helpful. In addition to reading every other named module, an automatic module is also made to read the unnamed module. Thus, while running a modular application, the classpath option can be used to enable automatic modules to access third party non-modular jars. 




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 9_bottom_up_approach_points**   
**📚 Study Reference: 8_top_down_approach_points**

16  |  ID: 3439  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    Your application consists of two jar files produced by two different teams - `accounting-3.2.jar` and `reporting-5.6.jar`. Classes in `reporting-5.6.jar` uses classes from `com.abc.accounts` package in `accounting-3.2.jar` while classes in `accounting-3.2.jar` do not refer to classes from `reporting-5.6.jar`.
    
    The application is currently launched using the following command:
    `java -classpath accounting-3.2.jar;reporting-5.6.jar com.abc.reporting.Main`
    
    The accounting team has decided to modularize the new version of their jar but the reporting team hasn't. 
    
    Which of the following commands can be used to launch the reporting application using the new version of accounting jar?

  1. `java -classpath accounting-3.3.jar;reporting-5.6.jar com.abc.reporting.Main`

_Explanation:_ Since the structure of a modular jar is same as a regular jar, it can still be used in the old manner.

  2. `java -classpath reporting-5.6.jar --module-path accounting-3.3.jar; com.abc.reporting.Main`

_Explanation:_ Since the Main class is loaded from reporting jar, which is not a modular jar, the JVM does not know which modules are required by this class. So, even though accounting jar is on the module-path, it will not be loaded and so, classes from accounting jar will not be found. You will need to use --add-modules option to make it work but this option is not in scope for the exam.

  3. `java --module-path accounting-3.3.jar;reporting-5.6.jar com.abc.reporting.Main`
  4. `java --module-path accounting-3.3.jar;reporting-5.6.jar \--add-modules accounting \--module reporting/com.abc.reporting.Main` (Assume that the module name in accounting-3.3.jar is `accounting`.) 

_Explanation:_ By putting reporting jar on module path, we are essentially converting it into an automatic module (which means it is now a module with name `reporting` and with no `requires` clause.). Since an automatic module is allowed to read all exported packages of other named modules, classes in the reporting module will be able to access exported packages of the accounting module. Further, since reporting.jar is an automatic module, it doesn't have any module-info and so, java cannot determine the modules that it requires and so, java does not automatically load the modules contained in other jars present on module-path (even though those modules are readable) to classes in reporting.jar. We need to tell java to load the `accounting` module present in `accounting-3.3.jar` by using the `--add-modules` option.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1, 4

    Your application consists of two jar files produced by two different teams - `accounting-3.2.jar` and `reporting-5.6.jar`. Classes in `reporting-5.6.jar` use the class `com.abc.account.Account` in `accounting-3.2.jar` while classes in `accounting-3.2.jar` do not refer to classes from `reporting-5.6.jar`.
    
    The accounting team has decided to modularize their jar. Which of the following would be a valid `module-info.java` for the new `accounting-3.3.jar`?
    
    

  1. `module accounting{ requires com.abc.account; }`
  2. `module accounting{ export com.abc.account.*; }`
  3. `module accounting{ exports com.abc.account; }`
  4. `module accounting{ export com.abc.account; }`
  5. `module accounting{ export com.abc.account.Account; }`

_Explanation:_ A module "exports" packages, not individual classes.

  6. `module accounting{ provides com.abc.account; }`

_Explanation:_ A `provides` clause is used by a module to specify that it implements a particular service . For example, a jdbc driver module may contain: ` module com.mysql{ provides java.sql.Driver with com.abc.jdbc.Driver; }`

    Based on the given options, our guess is that in this question they are just checking whether you know that -cp, -classpath, and --class-path are equivalent options and the nonmodular jars should be put on `classpath` instead of `module-path`.

  1. `jdeps -cp file1.jar;file2.jar app.jar`
  2. `jdeps --upgrade-module-path file1.jar;file2.jar app.jar`
  3. `jdeps --class-path file1.jar;file2.jar app.jar`
  4. `jdeps -classpath file1.jar;file2.jar app.jar`

19  |  ID: 3680  |  Difficulty: Level 6
    
    
    Identify true statement(s) about migrating an application to modules.

  1. In bottom-up migration all classes and jars get moved to the module-path.
  2. In top-down migration all packages/jars are moved to the module-path.

_Explanation:_ In top-down migration you start with the packages that you are most interested in and upgrade them as named modules, without worrying about converting their dependencies to modules. You put the dependencies either on module-path (to make them automatic modules) or on classpath (to make them part of the unnamed module). Note that other jars on which your newly created module depends, but which you don't want to modularize yet, will need to be put on the module-path and you will need to add appropriate "requires" clauses in your newly created module's module-info.

  3. In top-down migration, the dependencies are migrated first.

_Explanation:_ This happens in bottom-up migration. First, the lowest level dependencies are converted into named modules and then the upper level dependents are converted into named modules. This process is repeated until the top level packages/libraries are converted into modules.

  4. Unnamed modules become automatic modules in top-down migration.

_Explanation:_ In top-down migration, classes that cannot be migrated or cannot be upgraded to automatic modules are left on the classpath and they become part of the unnamed module.

  5. In both the approaches for migration, classpath can be used.

_Explanation:_ classpath has no use in bottom-up migration because the lowest level of dependencies are migrated to modules first. So, there are no non-modular classes/packages in this approach. Remember that only packages on automatic modules can read packages present on the classpath. If you migrate a package as a regular explicitly named module and put it on module-path, it can no longer access classes on classpath.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 1

**Explanation:**  
**📚 Study Reference: 9_bottom_up_approach_points**   
**📚 Study Reference: 8_top_down_approach_points**

20  |  ID: 3440  |  Difficulty: Very Tough
    
    
    Your application uses one modular jar (`a.jar`), which, in turn, uses one non-modular jar (`b.jar`). Which of the following commands will cause `jdeps` to include the non-modular jar in its analysis?

  1. `jdeps --module-path lib\a.jar; -classpath lib\b.jar `

_Explanation:_ This is an invalid usage of jdeps. After specifying options, you must specify the target file that you want to analyze. ` Usage: jdeps <options> <path ...>] <path> can be a pathname to a .class file, a directory, a JAR file.` 

  2. `jdeps --module-path lib\a.jar; lib\b.jar `

_Explanation:_ If you put `a.jar` on module-path, then `jdeps` will try to build its module graph. It will look for module `b` (because it is given in the problem statement that `a.jar` uses `b.jar`). But `b.jar` is not on the `module-path`, so `jdeps` will given an error saying a required module `b` is not found.

  3. `jdeps --class-path lib\a.jar; lib\b.jar `

_Explanation:_ Ideally, since `a.jar` is a module, it should be put on the `module-path`. However, if `b.jar` does not refer to classes from `a.jar`, this will work and will do the analysis for `b.jar`. `--class-path lib\a.jar;` is of no use in this case. BTW, `-cp -classpath` and `--class-path` are equivalent.

  4. `jdeps -cp lib\b.jar lib\a.jar `

_Explanation:_ Note that the problem statement says that `a.jar` uses `b.jar`. Since `a.jar` is a modular jar, it cannot access classes from `b.jar` if `b.jar` is put on `classpath`. `b.jar` must be put on the `module-path` so that it can be converted into an automatic module and be accessible from `a.jar`. Thus, while analyzing `a.jar`, `jdeps` will generate an error saying a required module `b` is not found.

  5. `jdeps -cp lib\b.jar;lib\a.jar `

_Explanation:_ A target path is required.




✓ Correct Answer(s): 3

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    Please go through the documentation for jdeps given here: https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/21/docs/specs/man/jdeps.html
    
    jdeps can analyze one or multiple class file(s) or jar file(s). If a given target file depends on a module, then that module must be specified on --module-path (or -p). If the given target file depends on a non-modular jar file (or a class ), then that jar (or class) must be specified on -classpath (or --class-path or -cp). 

21  |  ID: 3643  |  Difficulty: Tough
    
    
    You are trying to modularize an existing application that uses a few Apache open source jar files. Some of those jar files have been modularized but some have not. Details of such jar files are as follows:
```java
    commons-beanutils-1.9.jar 
    
    commons-collections4-4.0.jar 
       (Automatic-Module-Name: org.apache.commons.collections4) 
    
    commons-lang3-3.6.jar 
       (Automatic-Module-Name: org.apache.commons.lang3) 
    
    commons-text-1.2.jar 
       (Automatic-Module-Name: org.apache.commons.text) 
```
    
    What should the module-info file for your application contain? 
    

  1. `module myapp{ requires commons.beanutils-1.9; requires commons.collections4-4.0; requires commons.lang3-3.6; requires commons.text-1.2; }`
  2. `module myapp{ requires org.apache.commons.beanutils; requires org.apache.commons.collections4; requires org.apache.commons.lang3; requires org.apache.commons.text; }`
  3. `module myapp { requires commons.beanutils; requires commons.collections4; requires commons.lang3; requires commons.text; } ` 
  4. `module myapp{ requires commons.beanutils; requires org.apache.commons.collections4; requires org.apache.commons.lang3; requires org.apache.commons.text; }`



✓ Correct Answer(s): 4

**Explanation:**  

    
    
    When you use a non modular jar file as an automatic module, the name of the jar file is used to formulate the module name for that module. You need to know the following two basic rules while deriving this name from the file name:
    
    1. The dashs are converted to dots and the version information and the file extension present in the file name are ignored. Therefore, for example, the module name for `commons-beanutils-1.9.jar`  will be `commons.beanutils`. 
    
    2. If the jar file's manifest contains the `Automatic-Module-Name` entry, then that value is used as the module name (the name of the jar file is ignored completely). Therefore, in the given problem statement, module name for `commons-collections4-4.0.jar` will be `org.apache.commons.collections4`.
